title: Method for Determining the Location of an Autonomous Underwater Vehicle Using Side Scan Sonar Images
authors: Aleksandr Saenko, Andrey Mironov, Ekaterina Fomina
abstract: This paper discusses the possibility of using the visual odometry method for processing side scan sonar images in order to determine the position of an unmanned underwater vehicle. The main attention is paid to evaluating the effectiveness of the use of classical key point search detectors. A classification of sonar images has been introduced, which made it possible to better evaluate the effectiveness of detectors. An approach is proposed for the implementation of area surveys and analysis of the received data in real time. It is shown that the ORB and SURF detectors have the best potential when they are implemented in the technical vision systems of an underwater uninhabited vehicle and used in the process of performing visual odometry.

title: Justification of Requirements to Security Indicators for Incident Investigation in Smart City
authors: Igor Kotenko, Igor Parashchuk
abstract: This paper develops approaches to constructing a new method for substantiating and ranking security performance requirements based on their informational value to investigate incidents at Smart City objects. Algorithms are used to determine the relative informational value of each security performance requirement used in investigating security incidents at Smart City objects. This allows to form an informational value matrix (dependency table) of requirements for such performance indicators. The key features of this approach are analyzed, and a computational experiment is conducted. The proposed approach enables the formation of optimal (non-redundant, minimal in volume and nomenclature, but sufficient) sets of such requirements.

title: An Analytical Approach to Optimization of Emergency Services Location in an Urban Area
authors: Vitaly O. Groppen
abstract: Two formal statements of the problem of optimal placement of emergency service bases within an urban area are presented: one for the cost of these bases placement minimization, the other for minimizing the distance from each base to the most distant object associated with it. Brute force algorithms are proposed for obtaining globally optimal solutions in the general case, as well as their modifications, which allow reducing the solution time in some cases. A theorem has been proven that allows us to reduce the time required to find a solution to these problems using the proposed algorithms in some cases. Several examples are given to illustrate the effectiveness of the tools proposed for solving the problem.

title: A Multi-Layer Framework for Adaptive Resource Allocation in Edge Computing: A Hybrid Reinforcement Learning Approach
authors: Om Khulbe, Cristina Hava Muntean, Shaguna Gupta
publish: 2026 29th Conference on Innovation in Clouds, Internet and Networks (ICIN)
abstract: In edge computing systems, many devices compete due to limited resources, and Reinforcement Learning (RL) has recently been introduced to solve this problem and share resources. Existing incentive mechanisms that are commonly used across edge computing systems, such as an auction mechanism, where devices bid for resources, and a reputation mechanism, where devices that are more trustworthy are given priority. Most of the research focuses on making devices bid smarter in an auction or using isolated strategies when competing for resources. Some research also implements advanced RL techniques such as Deep Q-Learning (DQN) or Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG). The key observation is that when reputation mechanisms are combined with RL, devices are treated as add-ons, and end-to-end RL is rarely used to improve the system. This research study focuses on building an edge computing solution where RL helps to learn the best rules for rewards, trust, and fairness, and not just using fixed rules or helping agents. This work introduces a two-layer hybrid reinforcement learning framework that combines DQN-based device bidding with a DDPG-driven system agent capable of dynamically adjusting pricing and fairness rules. Across three evaluation scenarios, the framework consistently improves fairness, utilization, and revenue stability, particularly under volatile market conditions. The results show that fully adaptive incentive mechanisms can outperform static and semi-dynamic baselines, demonstrating the potential of end-to-end RL to enhance resource allocation in Mobile Edge Computing environments.

title: Methodological Foundations for Improving the Accuracy of Short-Term Forecasting of Wind Power Plant Output Based on Machine Learning Methods
authors: Huseyngulu Bayram Guliyev, Nijat Ramiz Huseynov, Ulviyya Ulduz Huseynova
abstract: Renewable energy, primarily wind and solar power plants, is increasingly occupying a significant position in the structure of the global energy system. At the same time, wind turbines (WTs) are characterized by several specific features, the most important of which is the variability of the wind resource. Changes in wind speed lead to fluctuations in generated power, creating additional challenges for the integration of WTs into modern power systems. As the installed capacity and the number of WTs increase, their impact on the reliability indicators of electrical networks becomes more pronounced. In this regard, the tasks of short-term forecasting of wind speed and generated power, ensuring the stability and reliability of grid operation, as well as organizing effective maintenance of installations in the event of faults, become particularly relevant. Solving these problems plays a key role in maintaining the stable operation of the power system as a whole. The present study provides the results of short-term forecasting of wind speed and WT power output using neural network approaches, namely Extreme Learning Machine (ELM) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM). Modeling and calculations were carried out in the Matlab and R software environments for distribution networks of the Absheron Peninsula (Azerbaijan), which include wind power installations.

title: Automated Anomaly Detection and Cluster Analysis of Online Fiscal Device Data Using Unsupervised Machine Learning
authors: Danil Shestakov, Denis Vasilev, Andrey Oskin
abstract: The rapid digitalization of fiscal control in Russia has led to the accumulation of vast arrays of data generated by online cash registers (OCRs). This data offers significant potential for detecting tax evasion schemes, such as cash rental fraud and the manipulation of fiscal devices. However, the sheer complexity and scale of these datasets pose substantial challenges to traditional monitoring methods. This paper presents an automated, interpretable pipeline for anomaly detection and clustering in fiscal device data. We systematically evaluate dimensionality reduction methods (UMAP and autoencoders) and clustering algorithms (HDBSCAN and DBSCAN) within a comprehensive framework for unsupervised analysis of aggregated time series and textual features from cash receipts. The proposed pipeline, achieving a Silhouette Score of 0.803, successfully isolated 100% of known rental fraud devices into a single, distinct cluster. Our results demonstrate that the combination of UMAP and HDBSCAN robustly identifies anomalous device clusters associated with fraudulent behavioral patterns.

title: OCR Engines Evaluation
authors: Nikolai V. Smirnov, Mikhail A. Mendiukov
abstract: The Optical Character Recognition (OCR) is one of the most complicated tasks while processing scanned textual documents or documents contained in PDF files. Also, this task arises when conducting regulatory control of technical documents. While getting an image of a document page, noise or other distortions may appear in the image. The text in a document may be formatted using various stylistic features and may contain mathematical symbols and formulas. To solve OCR task several models and software products have been developed based on artificial intelligence methods, neural networks, and large language models. The authors selected metrics and several types of OCR models, created the dataset of images, which contained text in Russian and English, various types of distortions, as well as mathematical formulas. The authors divided the images into classes and categories, evaluated OCR models and selected the best model.

title: Field Validation of Prior-Based Image Compression for Tetherless Operation of Underwater Remotely Operated Vehicles
authors: Luyuan Peng, Yuen Min Too, Mandar Chitre, Hari Vishnu, Bharath Kalyan, Rajat Mishra, Soo Pieng Tan
abstract: Efficient visual communication is critical for tetherless operation of underwater remotely operated vehicles, where acoustic links severely constrain bandwidth. Prior work introduced NVSPrior, which uses novel view synthesis with 3D Gaussian Splatting to encode scene priors, together with iNVS, a gradient-based refinement strategy for improving reconstruction quality. However, its performance degrades in real-world environments due to turbidity, lighting variability, and dynamic scene elements. This paper presents a systematic field evaluation of NVSPrior+iNVS in turbid natural waters using ROV trials off St. John's Island, Singapore. To improve robustness, we introduce iNVS-w, which combines a DFNet-inspired pose regressor with a perceptual refinement loss. Benchmarking against classical and learned codecs shows that iNVS-w achieves substantially lower bitrate than scene-agnostic baselines while maintaining high perceptual fidelity on realistic field imagery. Ablation studies further quantify the role of initialization, loss functions, and feature extractors. These results provide a field-based assessment of prior-based image compression and identify practical modifications needed for robust operation in bandwidth-constrained underwater inspection.

title: Development of a Modular Active Probe Card Equipment for Wafer-Level Multi-Parameter Fast Characterization and Prediction of High-Q MEMS Gyroscopes
authors: Yang Zhao, Qin Shi, Guoming Xia, Jinyang Huang, Anping Qiu
abstract: This paper proposes an Active Probe Card (APC) equipment for MEMS gyroscopes offering multi-parameter that address the concerns of manufacturers and end-users. An efficient and reliable frequency and Q-factor (f&Q) measurement method, enabled by a negative impedance converter (NIC), is firstly applied to the one-port sense resonator of the gyroscope. While the gyroscope’s drive loop oscillates continuously, the f&Q measurement periods for drive/sense resonators and the fully operational gyroscope characterization periods are merged. It eliminates the time overhead associated with high-Q mode ring-down, restart-up waiting, and connection reconfiguration. Furthermore, the system incorporates a variable polarization voltage (Vp) function. The true process related monitoring parameters and predictions of gyroscope operating behaviors with specific frequency split (Δf) are automatically extracted from two sequential tests with different Vp loads. The time cost per DUT is <5s. The probes carrier card and active electronics card are interconnected with pluggable shielded cables, facilitating easy upgrading and adaptation to gyroscopes with different pin definitions. Using the proposed APC equipment, we demonstrate it can 1) realize the extraction and monitoring of true dimension and encapsulated pressure parameters in both the same and different process stages；2） perform system-level gyroscope operating behaviors pre-validation and grading at the wafer level.

title: Pound-Drever-Hall Frequency Locking of a Quartz MEMS Resonator for Sensing Applications
authors: Thomas Hinds, Nikhil Bajaj
abstract: Pound-Drever-Hall (PDH) is a commonly used method to control the frequency of laser systems for many applications, including sensing. The resolution, precision, and bandwidth characteristics of PDH and techniques derived from it has led to their adoption where sensitivity and stability is required. Generally, the technique locks the carrier frequency of a frequency modulated excitation to a device's resonance with a servo system that minimizes the reflected power at the modulation frequency. In this work, PDH was used to track the resonance of a 26 [MHz] quartz microelectromechanical system (MEMS) with a loaded Q <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\sim 10^{5}$</tex-math></inline-formula>. The passive resonator circuit, made with off-the-shelf components, incorporates a directional coupler to isolate reflected voltages for measurement. The control loop is implemented digitally using a FPGA-based instrument. The PDH-MEMS resonator system attains an Allen deviation below 10<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^\text{-8}$</tex-math></inline-formula> for a 5 [s] averaging time at 25 [<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^\circ$</tex-math></inline-formula>C], and 35 [ms] settling time to a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\sim -70$</tex-math></inline-formula> [dB] disturbance. The resonant frequency of the crystal was tracked during a heating and cooling cycle to characterize its temperature sensitivity. The work provides an architecture for future high-performance MEMS resonant sensors.

title: A 200-MS/s Multi-Event TDC With Gated-DLL-Based Coarse/Fine Conversion and Event-Driven Phase Encoding for d-ToF Applications
authors: Sadra Tafaghodi Jami, Ali Fotowat-Ahmady, Jie Yuan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: This article presents a multi-channel, multi-event time-to-digital converter (TDC) with a highly-scalable architecture designed for direct time-of-flight (d-ToF) light detection and ranging (LiDAR) sensors with large-scale single-photon avalanche diode (SPAD) arrays. The proposed TDC features an ultra-low-dead-time event arbitration mechanism combined with a gated delay-locked loop (DLL) with an event-driven phase encoding scheme, enabling simultaneous coarse and fine timing measurements with a high conversion rate, thereby minimizing dead time between captured events. The shared architecture among 17 TDC channels significantly reduces on-chip area and power consumption, while the proposed ultra-low-dead-time coarse<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$/$</tex-math> </inline-formula>fine conversion technique ensures high throughput for sensors operating under high photon flux conditions. Fabricated in a 180-nm CMOS technology, the TDC achieves coarse and fine timing resolutions of 10 ns and 156.25 ps at a low reference clock frequency of 100 MHz, covering a 2.56-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>s dynamic range. The measured coarse and fine conversion rates are 600 MS<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$/$</tex-math> </inline-formula>s and 200 MS<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$/$</tex-math> </inline-formula>s, leading to a scalability figure-of-merit (FoM) of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$42.64~1/$</tex-math> </inline-formula>fJ<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$/$</tex-math> </inline-formula>mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, which is the highest among TDCs designed for d-ToF applications. The worst-case differential nonlinearity (DNL) and integral nonlinearity (INL) are measured as <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$+ 0.13/-0.09$</tex-math> </inline-formula> LSB and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$+ 0.66/-0.78$</tex-math> </inline-formula> LSB, respectively.

title: Virtual–Real Hybrid Game Approach for Virtual Power Plants Based on Continual Learning With Forward-Feedback Knowledge Replay
authors: Wei Zhou, Jingang Lai, Zhigang Zeng
abstract: This article proposes a multiagent collaborative game evolution strategy to address issues of distributed resource sharing among virtual power plants, which is based on multicriteria matching and continuous learning. First, a peer matching mechanism is designed considering heterogeneous factor fusion and reasoning, which can enhance the adaptability and effectiveness of matching work. Then, a virtual–real hybrid game evolution framework is proposed based on continuous learning, in which a data-driven SE estimation method is developed to transform the traditional iterative response process into an efficient strategy estimation, thereby improving the efficiency of multiagent games. Furthermore, to further mitigate catastrophic forgetting in continuous learning, a multitimescale forward-feedback knowledge replay method is proposed to dynamically adjust replay timing and content, which can achieve knowledge consolidation through a combination of preventive maintenance and targeted repair, enhancing model evolution capabilities. Case studies validate that the proposed strategy exhibits superior performance in matching rationality, game convergence speed, and resistance to knowledge forgetting.

title: Impedance Contrast Multilayer Electrode Design for TF-SAW Energy Confinement
authors: Haoqin Ma, Sulei Fu, Peisen Liu, Boyuan Xiao, Xinchen Zhou, Jiajun Gao, Haojie Wang, Rui Wang, Cheng Song, Fei Zeng, Feng Pan
abstract: Efficient manipulation of surface acoustic wave (SAW) within thin-film structures is fundamental to advancing the performance of SAW devices, yet it is challenged by unavoidable energy dissipation into the substrate. To suppress acoustic wave energy leakage, this study enhances the quality factor (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${Q}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> of SAW devices by introducing a heterogeneous multilayer electrode structure incorporating Al/Ir and Al/Ru stacks. By strategically exploiting the drastic acoustic impedance mismatch between these heavy and light metals, a reflective boundary is engineered to strictly confine the SAW energy within the piezoelectric layer. An impedance contrast multilayer electrode structure not only minimizes acoustic wave radiation losses but also suppresses electrode damping. Experimental results confirm that acoustic impedance engineering strategy yields a transformative leap in device performance: the Al/Ir-based SAW resonator achieves a remarkable <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${Q}_{\max }$</tex-math> </inline-formula> of 5846 at <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$3~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m wavelength—110% enhancement over the Al baseline value of 2779. Furthermore, a robust <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$Q$</tex-math> </inline-formula> improvement > 70% is observed across various wavelengths based on 10 nm Ru (except <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m) and 10 nm Ir. These findings offer deep insights into the loss mechanisms of thin-film SAW resonators and establish a generic, physically driven pathway for realizing high-performance SAW devices.

authors: N/A

title: Crafting Digital Pathways for Employee Engagement: A Case Study on Communities of Practice and Situated Learning in Digital Transformation_supp1-3684574.pdf
authors: Yan Yan
abstract: Digital transformation (DT) is essential for the survival and competitiveness of contemporary enterprises. Successful DT relies on active employee engagement and contributions, with learning serving as a pivotal enabler. However, the extant literature offers limited insights into how to effectively engage employees in DT. Furthermore, the dynamic, complex, and novel nature of DT often exceeds the capacity of conventional learning practices, underscoring the necessity of “learning by doing.” Drawing on situated learning theory and the concept of communities of practice (CoPs), this longitudinal case study examines how employee engagement in DT occurs through practice-based learning. Research rigour is ensured through systematic triangulation of multiple data sources, iterative cycles of data collection and analysis, and constant comparison across emerging themes. Our analysis identified three critical phases of employee engagement in DT: Domain Identification, DT Community Preparation, and DT Practice Engagement. The first two phases fostered employee engagement and empowerment, while the third one enabled exploratory and exploitative situated learning within DT CoPs, leading to successful outcomes. These findings provide valuable insights into fostering employee engagement in DT and advancing the research on facilitating practice-based learning in DT.

title: Development of a Control System Based on a Deep Generative Model for a 6-DoF Robotic Manipulator
authors: Evgeny Nikolaev, Nataly Zakharova, Vladimir Zakharov
abstract: The functioning of robotic intelligent complexes in a dynamic environment for the effective solution of object manipulation tasks is a challenging scientific problem. Intelligent robotics involves not only the performance of tasks by a robot manipulator, but also safe interaction with humans, which is why collaborative robots are increasingly in demand. The efficiency of robot manipulators in performing tasks depends on the hardware architecture of the robotic device, but to a greater extent on the control system. The use of dynamic models based on physical parameters of the environment allows for the implementation of a high-quality robot control system capable of generalization, but this approach requires complete information about the environment. The use of machine learning models in the development of a robot manipulator control system allows the robot's actions to be generated based on the parameters of the environment perceived by sensors. This approach allows complex behavior algorithms to be implemented in real time in a changing robot operating environment. This paper proposes an architecture for a robot manipulator control subsystem based on a deep generative model.

title: Crowdsourcing Feature Selection via a Distributed Evolutionary Algorithm
authors: Shu-Rui Liang, Feng-Feng Wei, Qiu-Zhen Lin, Wen-Jian Luo, Wei-Neng Chen
abstract: Crowdsourcing leverages the collective intelligence of the crowd to collect data and solve complex computational tasks. Driven by this paradigm, data can now be gathered more efficiently and at larger scales, thereby increasing the need for effective dimensionality reduction, which makes feature selection (FS) essential for efficient learning. In this paper, we refer to the problem in which multiple workers in a crowdsourcing environment collect data and concurrently optimize FS as the crowdsourcing feature selection (CFS) problem. In CFS, workers perform FS on local data and upload candidate feature subsets to complete the outsourced task. Nevertheless, the heterogeneity across multiple data sources hinders the formation of a reliable and high-quality consensus solution. To address this issue, we propose a cooperative learning-based Distributed Evolutionary Algorithm for the CFS problem (DEA-CFS). First, we formulate the CFS problem and define the roles of workers and the server, as well as their interactions in a crowdsourcing environment. Second, on the worker side, we design a distributed cooperative learning strategy that refines local solutions and mitigates data heterogeneity through confidence-aware fitness comparison. Third, on the server side, we introduce an adaptive credibility-based aggregation mechanism that aggregates a robust consensus solution. Extensive experiments on 18 datasets with up to 10,000 features demonstrate the efficiency and effectiveness of DEA-CFS. Specifically, compared to four existing distributed baselines, DEA-CFS achieves a superior average rank of 1.16 and obtains the best performance on 15 of the 18 datasets.

title: Design of Unidirectional Broadband Circularly Polarized Resonator with Integrated Local Oscillator for Rydberg Atomic Receiver
authors: Yuxiao Li, Yi Lin, Kai Yang, Ruibing Ran, Qiang An, Yunqi Fu
abstract: The high-efficiency Rydberg field probe (RFP), as the core of Rydberg atomic receivers, has at tracted widespread attention due to its advantages of high sensitivity and high integration. This letter presents a broadband right-hand circularly polarized (RHCP) enhance ment resonator with unidirectional receiving beam. It can be combined with RFP to enhance the detection sensitivity of the Rydberg atomic receiver. The experimental results demonstrate the excellent broadband CP response of the resonator, with a 3-dB axial ratio (AR) bandwidth of 180 MHz. Using the AC stark effect, the resonator achieves an enhancement of 25.4–28.1 dB for RHCP microwaves (MW), with an optimal AR of 2.15 dB at 2.32 GHz. This designed resonator further expands the range of Rydberg atomic receivers in CP and directional reception applications.

title: Human–Robot Co-Thinking Collaboration Improves Productivity and Reduces Errors in a Combinatorial Optimization Task
authors: Nanami Ishizu, Miharu Kawasaki, Wen Liang Yeoh, Osamu Fukuda
abstract: With the advent of industry 5.0, humans and robots are increasingly collaborating, leveraging both their complementary strengths to increase productivity and flexibility. In this study, focusing on strawberry packaging, we developed a Co-Thinking robot and investigated how this robot could work together with a human operator when performing a simulated packaging task. Strawberry packaging involves solving a combinatorial optimization task in which strawberries of varying individual weights must be selected to achieve a predefined target package weight while carefully excluding defective items. The Co-Thinking robot estimates the weights of all individual strawberries currently using computer vision and calculates the optimal strawberry to be selected. This information is provided to the human operator through a real-time display interface, and the robot moves to select or pick-up these strawberries. The operator collaborates with the robot through a force-sensor-equipped grip where they can choose to intervene when necessary, such as to ignore defective strawberries, or to correct for weight estimation error of the robot.We investigated the benefits of this collaboration through an experiment involving ten participants. The simulated strawberry packaging task was performed under three conditions: human-only, robot-only, and collaborative. We found that, relative to working alone (human-only condition), collaboration between human and robot led to faster task completion times, lower weight error, and higher number of overall optimal solutions. This collaboration also led to significantly lower perceived mental demand, temporal demand, and effort, and higher own performance, as measured by the NASA-TLX questionnaire. Although the robot-only condition showed the fastest and most consistent completion times, collaboration with a human operator reduced the number of defective strawberries and often led to more optimal solutions, as the operators performed corrective interventions. These findings indicate that collaboration with a Co-Thinking robot can enhance productivity and reduce errors compared to when performing the task with human-only or robot-only, demonstrating the value of human-robot collaboration.

title: Artificial Intelligence for Early Wildfire Detection in Northern Morocco: A Robust and Interpretable CatBoost–Transformer Framework Using Multisource Environmental Data
authors: Chaimae Ouazri, Abderrahim El Mhouti, Mohamed Fahim, Nisrine El Ayat
abstract: Forest fires pose a major threat to Mediterranean ecosystems and northern Morocco is particularly vulnerable due to climate pressure, rugged terrain and limited firefighting resources. This study aims to build an effective and interpretable prediction model adapted to these local conditions. Using daily meteorological data, fire danger indices and satellite observations from 2019 to 2024 a range of machine learning, deep learning and hybrid models were evaluated. The hybrid CatBoost transformer achieved the best performance with 92.5% accuracy and a ROC AUC of 97.2% .The SHAP analysis identified geographic location, surface pressure and drought indices as the most influential features . These results highlight the value of hybrid and explainable AI models for strengthening the early warning and prevention of wildfires in the Mediterranean region of Morocco.

title: Variable-Path Media-Based Modulation with Hybrid Constellations for SIMO Systems
authors: Xiaoqiang Zhong, Fuchun Huang, Zhili Zhou, Xiufang Zou
abstract: This letter proposes a novel scheme of single-antenna channel modulation with variable channel-path (SACM-VCP) for single-input multiple-output (SIMO) systems. Using a switch controller, the SACM-VCP dynamically activates either one or two channel paths via radio frequency (RF) mirrors. This introduces a new switching index dimension that enhances the spectral efficiency without requiring additional RF chains. To further improve the bit error rate (BER) performance, hybrid signal constellations are co-designed with the channel path(s) selection under the constraint of squared minimum Euclidean distance. Moreover, the detection complexity and average bit error probability are analyzed by using the union bounding and moment generating function techniques. Simulation results demonstrate that the SACM-VCP achieves significant SNR gains over existing MBM-based schemes, presenting an improved trade-off between spectral efficiency, reliability, and complexity.

title: Development of a Method for Reducing Information Redundancy of Databases Based on Deep Learning Algorithms
authors: Si Thu Thant Sin, I. A. Kugoev, E. M. Portnov
abstract: It has been established that when combining databases formed independently, difficulties arise due to differences in the structure, format and content of records. These differences include variability in text descriptions, differences in images, incomplete or redundant attributes, and inconsistencies in categorization systems. All this makes automatic matching difficult and leads to duplication of information, reducing the efficiency of data storage, processing, and analysis. A method for comparing objects is proposed and implemented. The development was based on the need to overcome the limitations of traditional approaches to deduplication, which do not cope well with text variability, differences in formats, and lack of standardization. The developed methodology is built as a cascade system, in which each subsequent stage clarifies the result of the previous one, which allows taking into account both the lexical form and the semantic and visual content of the records. The results of the experiments confirm that the developed system is a reliable tool for data integration, updating databases and preparing data for subsequent analytical processes.

title: Privacy-Preserving for UTXO-Based Smart City Ledgers
authors: Vasiliy Krundyshev, Maxim Kalinin, Oleg Vasiliev
abstract: In recent years, the distributed ledger systems (DLS) has become an indispensable approach for creating anonymous payment systems in Smart City infrastructures (in transportation and industrial systems, energy planting and distribution, etc.). Although users in such systems are identified indirectly, but through cryptographic protocols, there is a class of attacks aimed at deanonymizing participants by analyzing transactions and constructing a graph of relationships between addresses. To implement these attacks, intruders use statistical analysis methods, graph theory, and specialized utilities for comparing data from external sources, such as exchanges. An analysis of related works in this domain has shown that compromising cryptographic primitives is not a prerequisite for deanonymizing DLS users; in some cases, analyzing public ledger data and the behavioral characteristics of DLS participants is sufficient. The goal of this research is to preserve privacy and develop protocols that minimize the risks of deanonymizing participants in Smart City ledgers built on the UTXO model. This paper presents a developed framework consisting transaction generator, an analyzer for modeling deanonymization attacks, and protocols designed to protect against such attacks. The experimental study has shown that the use of the CoinJoin protocol significantly complicates the deanonymization task and leads to a decrease in the deanonymization accuracy.

title: Hybrid Generative–Discriminative Vision with Diffusion-Based Reasoning for Aerial Platform Inspection of Industrial Surfaces
authors: Irem Bayraktar, Murat Bakirci
abstract: Industrial surfaces rarely behave in predictable ways. New residue patterns emerge, metal plates warp subtly over time, and heat exposure leaves marks that no curated training set fully represents. In this work, we propose a hybrid drone-based inspection framework that pairs a lightweight YOLOv9t detector with a diffusion model trained to reconstruct normal surface appearance. Instead of relying solely on predefined defect categories, the system combines fast supervised detection with generative reconstruction error to highlight both known and previously unseen anomalies. Experiments on a mixed drone dataset, containing cracks, dents, corrosion patches, foreign objects, and several types of out-of-distribution anomalies, show that the hybrid approach consistently outperforms the YOLO-only baseline. While YOLO alone delivers strong precision on familiar defects, it struggles with emerging or ambiguous surface conditions. By integrating reconstruction error, the hybrid model improves F1-scores by 10–15% across known classes and achieves a substantial leap in unseen anomaly detection, raising area under the curve (AUC) from roughly 0.50 to 0.89. Moderate fusion weights offer the most balanced behavior, indicating that neither supervised nor generative cues are sufficient on their own. Despite the added generative component, the system remains practical for field deployment. End-to-end latency averages 350–400 ms per frame, with YOLO inference running comfortably onboard the drone and diffusion reconstruction handled at the edge workstation. The pipeline avoids overwhelming wireless channels by transmitting only frames marked as informative by the onboard detector.

title: Development of a Module for Direct Measurement of Heating Temperatures of High-Voltage Cable Layers to Determine Their Thermal Parameters
authors: Konstantin E. Gorshkov, Denis V. Korobatov, Ivan E. Kolesnikov
abstract: Identification of the thermal parameters of high-voltage power cables is a task addressed in the development of intelligent systems for monitoring the condition of electrical equipment and for the development of digital twins. To calculate the cable heating temperature in real time, it is first necessary to identify the parameters of the equivalent thermal circuit. This can be done both analytically and experimentally. The experimental method is more promising, as it allows taking into account the individual characteristics of samples and can be used for both new and in-service power cables. To perform measurements, a digital measurement module with temperature sensors and a controller was developed, which provides information conversion and transfer to a computer, as well as software for processing and collecting information.

title: Graph-Based Representation and Storage of Data on Failure Correlations of Mainline Pumping Unit Components
authors: Arthur Astakhov, Lyudmila Chernenkaya
abstract: The analysis of historical data in the process of assessing technical condition is a key method for increasing the accuracy of equipment performance forecasts, which complements developing machine learning models for analyzing operational data from equipment sensors. The study considers the analysis of historical failure data for an oil main pumping unit based on the formation of a graph model of failures. The main focus of this study is to develop an approach based on dividing the graph structure into life cycle phases, which allows limiting the sequence of failure chains and reducing the impact of the frequency of different types of failures that may occur more often at different stages of the pump unit's operation. The study also proposes dividing parts and components into critical ones that determine the failure of the unit, as well as consumable elements and parts, the replacement of which depends on critical parts. In addition, the study proposes characterizing the vertices of the failure graph as repairs, which include a set of parts and components that require replacement. A mathematical model of the proposed method has been developed, describing the structure of the graph vertices that determine repairs, as well as the subsequent use of probabilistic estimation to adjust the existing performance estimate determined by statistical analysis of historical data.

title: Leveraging Digital Twins and Machine Learning for Industrial Network Automation
authors: Chiara Grasselli, Chiara Contoli, Franco Callegati
publish: 2026 29th Conference on Innovation in Clouds, Internet and Networks (ICIN)
abstract: The Asset Administration Shell (AAS) is a promising approach to integrate Information Technology (IT) and Operational Technology (OT) as it implements the concept of a Digital Twin (DT) of a manufacturing plant, thus enabling monitoring and interaction with the assets in an Industry 4.0 scenario. However, its role as a DT for the modeling and management of OT network connectivity as a factory asset remains underexplored. In this paper, we propose a solution whose goal is twofold: i) modeling both production assets and the OT network using AASbased digital twins to provide a unified visibility and automated management; ii) incorporating machine learning-based traffic fingerprinting and profiling to provide automated management policy enforcement. We showcase the proposed approach with traffic traces from a real-world production plant, comparing traffic classification and profiling techniques. Achieved results show that it is possible to identify the industrial machines in the OT network with reasonable performance metrics.

title: Models and Algorithms for Building AI-Powered Assistants: A Case Study of PetTech Platform Vetsy.ru
authors: Gleb Guskov, Alexandr Shcherbina, Ramil Zayneev
abstract: This paper presents research results aimed at developing tools for creating AI-Powered assistants in applied information systems. Modern domain-specific information systems increasingly incorporate intelligent user assistants. These assistants serve as domain consultants, performing rapid diagnostics of discussed objects, recommending solutions to achieve target states, and maintaining natural language dialogue with users. Currently, software engineering lacks standardized architectures for intelligent information systems and established methodologies for integrating AI-Powered assistants. The article presents research results in the form of new methods and tools for machine learning (fine tuning) of available language models, as well as means for integrating intelligent assistants with fact databases, problem domain rule bases, and information system modules. The article examines the effectiveness of modern language models (LLM Efficiency) from the perspective of intelligent assistant development (AI/LLM Agent). The reliability of dialogues generated by the intelligent assistant is ensured through connections to both local fact databases and internet search capabilities, utilizing Retrieval-Augmented Language Models. The paper investigates the efficiency of modern language models (LLMs) for developing AI/LLM agents, ensuring response reliability through connections with both local knowledge bases and web search capabilities. The research was conducted during development and implementation of a veterinary assistance system (PetTech) featuring an advanced Russian-language intelligent assistant, available at www.vetsy.ru
