title: Digital Twin for Neural Network Training
authors: Vladislav Grechushnikov, Nguyen Trung Hieu, Alexander Shilin
abstract: This paper addresses the challenge of formalizing technological processes where expert knowledge is expressed primarily as linguistic patterns and cause-and-effect relationships that are difficult to express through rigorous mathematical models. The lack of such models hinders the synthesis of digital twins, which require precise reproduction of transient dynamics and observability of states, especially for long cycles such as lumber drying (lasting 10-12 days). The proposed approach involves developing the most comprehensive mathematical model of the technological process, ensuring repeatability of transient processes and integrating expert algorithms with approximate parameters. A neural network subsystem is developed for this model, focused on identifying hidden patterns in expert descriptions. A structural diagram of a control system based on a programmable logic controller and a digital twin has been developed for a laboratory study of neural network learning ability using the example of lumber drying. The relevance of this architecture stems from the need to accelerate neural network training, which in real-world conditions can take months due to the length of cycles. To ensure PLC compatibility with virtual emulation, a resistive converter is proposed that converts analog resistance signals into discrete pulse-width signals, ensuring compatibility across the connection diagram. This article does not discuss neural network construction methods, but it does formulate a priority task for specialists in this field, emphasizing the potential for extending this approach to other technological processes with non-formalized expert knowledge.

title: A Digital Twin of an Agrocenosis for Modeling Plant Growth Based on an Automated Mapping System
authors: I. I. Naumov, R. R. Ibadov, A. G. Gazaryan
abstract: This paper presents the concept and architecture of a digital twin of an agrocenosis developed for crop growth modeling based on an automated mapping system. The digital twin is structured as a multi-layer system integrating physical-geographical, technological, biophysical, observational, and modeling components. The physical-geographical layer includes a digital elevation model, soil properties, and climatic and hydrological factors that determine spatial variability of growing conditions. The technological layer formalizes agricultural operations in space and time, forming a digital field management map. The biophysical layer describes crop state variables such as phenological stage, biomass, and leaf area index (LAI). The observation layer combines satellite imagery, UAV data, and ground-based sensors to ensure continuous synchronization between the real field and its digital representation. A modular mathematical framework integrating agrophysical balance models and data-driven approaches is proposed. The digital twin enables scenario analysis, yield forecasting, and optimization of agricultural technologies, supporting precision agriculture and risk management under changing climatic conditions.

title: Bearing Fault Recognition Based on CEEMDAN-MSB Feature Enhancement and Transformer Fusion
authors: Decheng Zhi
abstract: As a key component of rotating mechanical systems, precise fault diagnosis of rolling bearings is critical to ensuring the safe and reliable operation of equipment. This system presents an intelligent diagnosis method of bearing fault based on CEEMDAN-MSB and Transformer. Firstly, adaptive noise complete empirical mode decomposition (CEEMDAN) is used to decompose and de-noising the original vibration signal, stripping the interference noise from the signal and highlighting the fault related transient pulse characteristics. After that, the modulated signal bispectrum (MSB) further suppresses the residual random noise, strengthens the fault modulation characteristics, and realizes the accurate extraction of fault information. Finally, the processed features are input into Transformer model. With the help of multi-head self-attention mechanism, the global time sequence correlation and local key features of long sequence signals are captured to realize bearing fault diagnosis. Taking the bearing data set of Southwest University of China as the experimental object, the diagnosis and analysis of three types of typical faults including normal and ball race, inner race and outer race were carried out. The experimental results show that the classification results of the proposed method show a significant diagonal aggregation trend, and the recognition accuracy of inner race, outer race fault and normal state is 100%. For ball race fault with small feature difference, F1 score reaches 0.86.

title: Myths Busted: Things You (Likely) Were Mistaught
authors: Colin C. McAndrew
abstract: Much of what you have been taught, in school, throughout your career, or recently from AI, may be correct when applied to the limited scope of problems presented to you during your education. Those teachings may not work in real-world applications. I have encountered many instances of that during my professional career, some of which took me decades to identify, and then resolve. Here I review some of what I consider to be the most egregious examples.

title: Genetic Optimization of Convolutional Neural Network Architectures Using Topological Crossover
authors: Dmitry Evsyukov, Elena Khudyakova, Mikhail Zhuravlev
abstract: This work explores the possibility of automatically searching for the structure and hyperparameters of shallow convolutional neural networks using genetic algorithms, allowing for higher accuracy than that of manually compiled models. The purpose of the article is to develop a genome structure for creating a neural network, describe the method of operation of a genetic algorithm that allows individuals to train, describe and analyze methods of mutation and crossing genomes to obtain a population with a high level of diversity. The analysis of the learning outcomes of the genetic algorithm, the complexity of its application to generate effective neural network structures, and describes its own method for topologically crossing convolutional neural network structures. The final genetic algorithm in combination with the studied method of crossing neural networks allowed to improve the convergence of the population, and the resulting best model, which has a non-trivial internal structure, was able to achieve an accuracy of 92.91% in the test sample, which is on average 2% higher than that of manually created models.

title: Detection of Black Hole Attack Sources in the AODV Protocol Based on Multi-Stage Segmentation of Network
authors: Dore Moriken
abstract: To detect nodes of black hole attack sources in the AODV protocol for MANETs, the SP-AACK approach (SPlitted AACK) is proposed, based on step-by-step segmentation of transmission paths from the source to the receiver of packets when no acknowledgments of packet delivery to the destination along the entire path are received. And object-oriented, event-driven, discrete simulation platform for computer networks. The SP-AACK approach extends the conventional AACK protocol by incorporating ACK, 2-ACK, and 3-ACK mechanisms combined with 1 to 4 malicious nodes. Results demonstrate that SP-AACK achieves significant improvement in packet delivery ratio (PDR), with average gains of approximately 70% for single black hole attacks and up to 88% for four simultaneous malicious nodes in throughput recovery. SP-AACK outperforms R-AODV, RID-AODV and Enhanced RID-AODV in both PDR and throughput, confirming it’s effectiveness as a robust detection and recovery solution for MANET environments.

title: Selective Standby Failover for Resilience-Aware Service Function Chaining
authors: Artur Mustafin, Aleksei Dukhanov
abstract: Service Function Chaining (SFC) is increasingly used to implement industrial traffic-processing pipelines at the edge-cloud continuum, where resilience and bounded latency are simultaneously required. Existing failover strategies are often polarized: reactive-only recovery minimizes pre-reserved resources but increases service disruption after node failures, while full standby protection provides low downtime at substantial overhead and acceptance loss. This paper proposes Selective Standby Failover (SSF), a resilience-aware policy that places standby VNFs only where redundancy yields the highest utility: critical service classes and high-risk hosting nodes. We formalize the problem as a multi-objective minimization of expected downtime, protection overhead, and SLA penalties under compute, delay, and disjoint-placement constraints. We then evaluate SSF in a reproducible simulation campaign with 30 seeds per setting on a 50-node topology, under both static and dynamic-maintenance scenarios with node failures. Compared with reactive-only recovery, SSF reduces mean downtime by about 29% at baseline failure intensity while significantly lowering critical-class SLA violations. Compared with full protection, SSF keeps substantially higher acceptance and cuts reserved-overhead by about 61%, while preserving class-aware resilience gains. The results indicate that selective redundancy is an effective and practical operating point for Industry 4.0 SFC orchestration.

title: Synthesis of Functional Observers for Uncertain Multi-Agent Systems
authors: Konstantin Zimenko, Daria Galkina, Alexey Margun
abstract: This study is devoted to the synthesis of functional observers for multi-agent systems operating under conditions of signal uncertainty in the interaction between agents. The proposed solution is based on constructing unknown input observers for each agent, whose local dynamics are described by sets of linear differential equations. The paper formally establishes the necessary conditions for the existence of such an observer and presents an analytical method for constructing the corresponding functional matrix. To validate the theoretical results, computer simulation was performed. The simulation results confirm the performance and practical effectiveness of the proposed solution for complex multi-agent networks.

title: Short-Term Wind Power Prediction Based on IPSO-Optimized LSTM Integrated with De-stationary Attention Mechanism
authors: Haitian Fan, Zhao Wang, Mao Yang
abstract: The volatility and intermittency of wind power pose considerable challenges to the stable operation of power systems, and high-precision short-term prediction is of critical importance to power grid dispatching. To improve the accuracy of short-term wind power prediction, this study proposes a short-term wind power prediction model that integrates the De-stationary Attention Mechanism, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network, and Improved Particle Swarm Optimization (IPSO) algorithm. First, a stationarization module is employed to eliminate noise from the time series, enhancing the predictability of non-stationary sequences, and then the De-stationary Attention Mechanism is introduced to compensate for the inherent non-stationarity of the original sequence. Second, the IPSO algorithm is utilized to optimize the hyperparameters of the LSTM model. Finally, short-term wind power prediction is realized using the optimized LSTM model. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms conventional comparative models such as the standalone LSTM and IPSO-LSTM in prediction accuracy, with both the Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) and Mean Absolute Error (MAE) effectively reduced. It can thus provide a reliable reference for power grid dispatching.

title: Machine Learning Data Quality Assessment Pipeline
authors: Maksim Poryvai, Dmitry Namiot
abstract: Data quality represents a critical factor affecting machine learning system reliability and performance, yet systematic assessment approaches remain limited in both research and industrial practice. This paper proposes a comprehensive framework and automated pipeline concepts for evaluating machine learning dataset quality through multi-dimensional analysis. The research establishes a quality assessment methodology based on ten fundamental dimensions: completeness, consistency, accuracy, timeliness, integrity, validity, uniqueness, relevance, representativeness, and balance. Each dimension can be operationalized through combinations of statistical tests, algorithmic detection methods, and configurable validation rules. The proposed framework adapts to different machine learning task types by adjusting dimensional priorities and threshold values according to specific requirements. The proposed pipeline implements modular three-stage architecture enabling systematic quality evaluation. First stage performs comprehensive statistical profiling extracting distributional characteristics and structural patterns. Second stage executes parallel dimension-specific assessments using specialized algorithms tailored to each quality aspect. Third stage aggregates individual dimension scores through weighted combination, generating overall quality ratings with confidence bounds and prioritized remediation recommendations. The proposed framework and pipeline implementation modular design enables customization for domain-specific requirements and extension with additional quality dimensions. The framework establishes quality assessment as essential practice comparable to model validation in importance for reliable machine learning systems.

title: High Efficiency UAV Vision for Smart Industrial Systems Enabled by Adaptive Edge AI Detection
authors: Irem Bayraktar, Murat Bakirci
abstract: Industrial assets, such as pipelines, metal tanks, solar arrays, and factory roofs, often hide small defects that can quietly grow into costly failures. This work presents a UAV-based inspection framework that performs real-time defect detection directly on edge hardware, rather than relying on cloud processing. A lightweight, optimized YOLO11m variant was trained on a combined dataset of real UAV imagery and synthetically generated defects, and the results show both technical robustness and practical value. The model achieved a mAP50 of 0.91 and a mAP50:95 of 0.69, outperforming YOLOv8-Lite, YOLOv9-T, and YOLOv10m across all defect classes. The gains were particularly notable for corrosion (precision 0.92, recall 0.86) and crack detection (precision 0.88, recall 0.80), two categories that typically challenge aerial inspection systems. On the hardware side, the model reduced inference latency to 49 ms on Jetson Nano and 20 ms on Xavier NX, and lowered power consumption by roughly 10–12% compared to other lightweight baselines. An adaptive event-based communication strategy further trimmed the operator burden, cutting unnecessary alerts by nearly 40% and reducing review time to 8.7 minutes per hour of inspection.

title: Hybrid LightGBM-Transformer framework with feature-as-token encoding for interpretable personal loan default prediction
authors: lei zheng, lu Huang
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 International Conference on Digital Transformation and Management
abstract: None

title: C-Shield: LLM-Enhanced Cybersecurity Platform for Intrusion Detection in Kubernetes Clusters
authors: Sarantis Kalafatidis, Antonis Villios, Antonis Garyfallou, Nikos Papageorgopoulos, Giannis Ledakis
publish: 2026 29th Conference on Innovation in Clouds, Internet and Networks (ICIN)
abstract: A holistic solution for intrusion detection in Kubernetes clusters is presented, combining multi-layer monitoring with selective LLM-based analysis. Statistical and rule-based detectors first identify suspicious activity and selectively trigger on-demand LLM reasoning to estimate threat probability and classify attack behavior, producing natural language explanations. The system is validated using realistic attack scenarios such as network flooding, port scanning, authentication abuse, and privilege escalation on vulnerable containerized services, demonstrating how cross-layer threat indicators and selective LLM reasoning support accurate security incident interpretation.

title: Acoustic Detection and Localization of Unmanned Aerial Systems on an Embedded Platform
authors: Alexander Dyachuk, Pavel Illeritskiy, Dmitriy Bondarenko
abstract: This paper presents an acoustic system for detecting and localizing unmanned aerial systems (UAS) designed to operate in noisy environments under the computational constraints of embedded devices. The system runs in two modes: near-real-time UAS presence detection and spatial localization of the sound source. The neural subsystem jointly solves two tasks: binary classification (drone/not_drone) and estimation of the fundamental frequency f<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</inf>, which is used as a physically interpretable cue for subsequent spatial processing (TDOA/DoA). Training is performed on the open DADS dataset. Audio is processed in 1.0 s windows at 16 kHz and represented by normalized log-mel spectrograms (64 mel bands, nfft = 1024, hop = 256). The model is a compact depthwise-separable CNN with two output heads trained in a multi-task setup with regression-loss masking for f<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</inf>. On the test set the detector reaches recall 0.997; at threshold of 0.40 it achieves precision 0.968, recall 0.997, and FPR 0.032. The paper also provides a direction-of-arrival (DoA) simulator for a four-microphone tetrahedral array based on GCC-PHAT and least squares in the far-field approximation, as well as a reproducible PyTorch → ONNX → TensorFlow → TFLite export pipeline where the two-output TinyML model occupies about 23 KB.

title: A Hybrid Statistical and Machine Learning Approach for Time-Series Forecasting of Traffic Accidents in Tashkent
authors: Sokhobiddin Akhatkulov, Islom Yalgoshev, Jamoliddin Jabbarov
abstract: In this study, a hybrid statistical and machine learning models are proposed for forecasting traffic accidents in the city of Tashkent, the capital of Uzbekistan. The hybrid models are based on the following models: Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA), Linear Regression (LR), Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES), and Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost). The data used in this study were collected monthly from 12 districts of Tashkent from 2018 to 2026, and it is available on https://opendata.tashkent.uz/eng (Accessed on Jan. 20, 2026). The model performances are compared with classical models such as SES and ARIMA. The results show that the proposed hybrid models are better performed in all metrics, MAPE, MAE and RMSE. The key features of this time-series dataset are the time and the total traffic accidents across all districts of Tashkent.

title: Machine-Learning Variation-Aware Co-Design for Analog Linearization of VCO-ADCs
authors: Viet Nguyen, Peter Kilgannon, Ciara Lambkin, Hieu Minh Nguyen, Robert Bogdan Staszewski
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Circuits and Systems
abstract: This article introduces a machine-learning (ML)-driven co-design and optimization framework for open-loop analog linearization of VCO-ADCs, especially operating in the low supply-voltage (VDD) regime, and demonstrated on a new coupled-oscillator-ensemble (COE) circuit architecture. The high-dimensional optimization space associated with the embedded linearization tuning ‘knobs’ of the VCOs renders exhaustive transistor-level search infeasible. To address this challenge, a deep neural network (DNN) surrogate is trained on a compact set of transistor-level transient simulations capturing the COE’s composite voltage-to-frequency (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$V$ </tex-math></inline-formula>-to-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$f$ </tex-math></inline-formula>) characteristics. This surrogate enables rapid exploration of the vast tuning-knob landscape and steers an evolutionary genetic algorithm (GA) toward configurations that optimize harmonic distortion (HD). To further enhance HD-prediction robustness and incorporate VDD variation awareness, the framework integrates advanced ML techniques. Monte Carlo (MC)–perturbed GA optimization improves resilience to parameter uncertainty, while a stacked-ensemble surrogate network, constructed from expert-VDD-specific base models fused with a hybrid encoder-combiner meta-learner, facilitates accurate VDD–aware predictions. The resulting optimized tuning settings are transferred to a Cadence simulation environment for transistor-level verification of the VCO–ADC, achieving a mean third-order harmonic distortion (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text {HD}_{3}$ </tex-math></inline-formula>) of 55 dB. Across a wide 0.4—0.6 V supply range (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\approx 40\%$ </tex-math></inline-formula> variation), <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text {HD}_{3}$ </tex-math></inline-formula> remains above 50 dB through the DNN’s supply-adaptive dynamic programming of the tuning-knob values.

title: Consensus-Based Graph Knowledge Construction from Large Language Models for Reliable Decision Support in Industry 4.0 Systems
authors: A.Y. Philippovich, A. K. Bilalov
abstract: This study proposes and evaluates a consensus-based framework for constructing reliable bipartite graph knowledge representations from multiple Large Language Model (LLM) outputs, targeting decision-support applications in Industry 4.0 and medical AI systems. Three frontier LLMs (GPT-5.2 Pro, Claude Opus 4.6, and Gemini 3 Pro) independently extracted structured knowledge from 12 domain-specific documents (industrial predictive maintenance and clinical triage corpora, approximately 48,000 tokens). The extracted graphs were aggregated using a weighted consensus algorithm with configurable acceptance threshold and validated through automated structural diagnostics. The results reveal statistically significant improvements for the consensus method over baselines: (1) the consensus graph achieves an edge-level F1 score of 0.80, representing an 8.1% improvement over the best single model (GPT-5.2 Pro, F1 = 0.74) and a 19.4% improvement over naive union aggregation (F1 = 0.67); (2) structural anomalies are reduced by 81.3% compared with single-model outputs and by 93.9% compared with naive union; (3) the structural violation rate decreases to V = 0.02; (4) automated semantic coherence analysis confirms that the consensus graph achieves a mean coherence score of 0.88, with only 2.1% of edges falling below the plausibility threshold. Ablation experiments demonstrate that each framework component contributes meaningfully to overall performance. This study contributes to understanding how multi-model consensus mechanisms can improve the reliability of automatically generated knowledge graphs and demonstrates the practical value of combining automated structural validation with semantic coherence analysis for safety-critical domains.

title: GMRNet: Deep Residual Network–Based Radiopathomic Glioma Classification with the Fusion of Multi-omics Data
authors: Hongwei Zeng, Fang Zhao, Lingchao Chen, Hongxia Xu, Honghao Gao, Bader Fahad Alkhamees
publish: ACM Transactions on Multimedia Computing, Communications, and Applications
abstract: None

title: Hermitian Symmetric Orthogonal Chirp-Division Multiplexing
authors: Gustavo M. Campos, Túlio. F. Moreira, Mateus de L. Filomeno, Ândrei Camponogara, Andrea M. Tonello, Moisés V. Ribeiro
abstract: This letter revisits orthogonal chirp-division multiplexing (OCDM) for baseband data communication systems, usually employed in wireline communication. We demonstrate that an existing formulation for generating real-valued chirp sequences shows limited temporal spreading. In the sequel, we introduce the hermitian-symmetric (HS)-OCDM technique, which correctly spreads chirp sequences, thereby improving performance. Numerical simulations show that the proposal offers enhanced robustness against cyclic prefix length violations and synchronization impairments granted to the spreading of the chirp signals in the whole symbol duration.

title: Resilient Control of CAV Platoons Under DoS Attacks With Compromised Leader Reachability
authors: Hui-Ting Wang, Chao Huang, Kunwu Zhang, Yong He
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems
abstract: This study addresses the resilient control of connected and automated vehicle (CAV) platoons under denial of service (DoS) attacks. We investigate two main attack scenarios. In the first scenario, zero-input DoS attacks target the communication channels, and progressing from degraded vehicle communication ability to compromised leader reachability may cause complete platoon disconnection. The second scenario considers the simultaneous occurrence of the above zero- and hold-input DoS attacks, where the latter introduces control delay. With Lyapunov functions and dwell time methods, switching distributed controllers are developed to achieve input-to-state string stability (ISSS). This ensures robust platoon performance through spacing regulation, speed consensus, and state error attenuation along the platoon, even under different attack conditions. Numerical simulations demonstrate the platoon’s resilience against simultaneous zero-input and hold-input DoS attacks, highlighting their cascading effects on platoon performance.

title: Evaluation of Graph- and Sampling-Based Path Planners for Multi-Agent Deployment in Known Urban Environments
authors: Daniel Colín García, Javier Izquierdo-Reyes, Isaac López Villegas, Rogelio Bustamante-Bello
abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) have become key tools in emergency missions in urban environments, due to their rapid deployment and ability to operate in hazardous conditions. Path planning is crucial in these disasters, as it determines the safety and efficiency of the mission. This study compares graph-based algorithms, such as A-Star (A) and D-Star Lite (D Lite), as well as sampling-based methods, including Probabilistic Roadmaps (PRM), Rapidly-exploring Random Tree Star (RRT), and Batch Informed Trees (BIT), along with spline-based post-processing applied to the trajectories produced by the sampling-based planners. To evaluate the performance of path planning algorithms, a framework developed in Python generates known three-dimensional urban environments with static obstacles. The benchmark considered 50 scenes, each with 15 obstacles, 5 UAVs, and 5 seed trials, resulting in 10,000 runs across eight different planners. This study used a strict success criterion and analyzed planning time metrics, path length, minimum safety margin, and geometric smoothness. D* Lite achieved the highest success rate (98.8%) and the highest obstacle clearance, but at the cost of longer planning times. RRT* and RRT* with splines were the fastest methods (median ≈ 4.9 ms), although with lower success rates. In contrast, BIT* and BIT* with splines offered the best value, second only to D* Lite, combining high success rates with shorter paths, better path quality, and moderate computational demand. The benchmark provides a quantitative basis for planner selection in non-cooperative multi-agent deployment scenarios, without accounting for flight dynamics, sensor uncertainty, or dynamic obstacles.

title: A DBMS-independent approach for capturing provenance polynomials through query rewriting
authors: Paulo Pintor, Rogério Luís De C. Costa, José Moreira
abstract: Data provenance provides fine-grained information about the origin and derivation of query results, directly supporting traceability, transparency, explainability and reproducibility in modern data-intensive environments. Provenance polynomials offer a formal framework for modelling these derivations, but despite their strong theoretical foundations, practical implementations remain limited, particularly with respect to full aggregation support and DBMS portability. This paper presents a query rewriting-based approach for annotating Structured Query Language (SQL) queries with provenance polynomials. The proposed methods are DBMS-independent and support Selection, Projection, Join, Union and Aggregation (SPJUA) operations, as well as nested queries through recursive propagation of provenance annotations. We implemented our proposals in the DataProv middle-ware, the first complete implementation of semiring-based theory for provenance polynomials extended with semimodule structures. It requires no modifications to the underlying data management and storage systems. DataProv is evaluated using distinct configurations of the TPC-H benchmark, comparing its capabilities and execution time with those of comparable systems. DataProv successfully captured data provenance for approximately 93% of the queries, while Perm, GProM, and ProvSQL successfully captured data provenance for approximately 86%, 50%, and 14%, respectively. DataProv also largely outperformed other tools in terms of execution time, achieving the best performance in 91% of the queries.

title: Cross-Splitting Information Geometry Approach for XL-MIMO Downlink Precoding
authors: Naijia Zheng, An-An Lu, Xiqi Gao
abstract: As the number of transmit antennas and served users increases in extremely large-scale multiple-input multiple-output (XL-MIMO) systems, the computational complexity of linear precoders becomes very high due to the inversion of large-dimensional matrices. In this paper, we propose a novel low-complexity block cross-splitting information geometry approach (BCS-IGA) to efficiently approximate the regularized zero-forcing (RZF) precoder. The proposed method reformulates the precoder design problem as computing the mean of a Gaussian distribution. By leveraging the information geometry theory and a novel natural parameter splitting, multiple manifolds of probability distributions are constructed to calculate the mean via small-dimensional matrix inversions. Finally, we prove that, upon convergence, the mean produced by the BCS-IGA coincides with the intermediate result of the RZF precoder. To further accelerate the convergence of BCS-IGA, a statistical channel correlation-based greedy algorithm (SCC-GA) is developed to adaptively select block structures. Simulation results show that the proposed approach achieves a higher sum-rate under the same number of iterations with only slightly higher complexity.

title: FPGA Implementation of IEC 61000-4-30 Quasi-Peak meter
authors: Philippe Blanchard, Manouane Caza-Szoka, Roger Bergeron, Daniel Massicotte
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: This paper presents a field-programmable gate array (FPGA) implementation of the IEC 61000-4-30 quasipeak (QP) detector. It integrates the complete digital signal-processing chain from sampled data to spectral analysis through a large-scale Fast Fourier Transform (FFT). The QP algorithm relies on a customized CORDIC engine that evaluates the trigonometric quantities required by the standard in a single iterative process, enabling real-time processing in the 9–150 kHz supraharmonic band. It introduces a memory-optimized FFT architecture that computes a 12 500-point transform in real time under stringent memory and throughput constraints on mid-range FPGAs; the design includes on-the-fly windowing, a transposed first-in first-out (FIFO) queue matrix for interstage buffering, and a recursive twiddle-factor generator. Implementation results demonstrate real-time processing of three parallel channels at a 1 kHz update rate, with ongoing work targeting full six-channel support. Overall, the proposed FPGA-based solution meets the standard requirements while remaining compatible with cost-effective hardware platforms, offering a scalable approach for embedded power quality instrumentation.

title: CNC-Former: Multi-Class Unsupervised Surface Defect Detection for CNC precision Parts
authors: Zhenyu Yin, Lijuan Yuan, Feiqing Zhang, Xiaoqiang Shi, Li Qin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: High-precision surface defect inspection is critical for ensuring product quality in precision parts manufactured by computer numerical control (CNC) machining. It can also provide quantitative measurement outputs for quality assessment. Given the limited availability of anomalous samples and the diversity of part categories, unsupervised anomaly detection (UAD) applies to this inspection task. However, existing multi-class UAD methods are susceptible to feature distribution heterogeneity and cross-class semantic interference during joint modeling, which degrades both defect detection accuracy and the stability of quantitative measurement outputs. To address this issue, we propose CNC-Former, a Vision Transformer-based high-precision multi-class UAD framework. First, a semantic-enhanced linear attention is introduced to suppress irrelevant cross-class responses by constraining the attention distribution, thereby improving defect localization performance. Second, an adaptive dual-branch bottleneck is constructed to jointly model semantic and structural information, enabling more effective perception of subtle local defects and their boundaries. Third, a progressive feature fusion module is incorporated to enhance cross-layer feature consistency and reduce inter-class interference. In addition, a measurement-oriented industrial inspection instrument for precision mechanical parts has been developed. The system converts the pixel-level outputs of CNC-Former into a metrologically defined projected defect area, thereby establishing a traceable end-to-end measurement chain from image acquisition to quantitative defect assessment. Experimental results show that, across 12 categories in a CNC-machined precision-part surface defect dataset, CNC-Former achieves a mean image-level AUROC of 94.2% and a pixel-level AUROC of 90.8%. Meanwhile, the measurement uncertainty of the projected defect area is evaluated, and the system output’s stability is validated through repeatability and reproducibility experiments.

title: Energy Efficiency Optimization Based on Multi-Agent DRL for Cognitive UAV-Assisted MEC HetNets
authors: Ke Lv, Sai Huang, Yuzhen Huang, Yu Xue, Weiwei Jiang, Yuanyuan Yao
abstract: The future sixth generation (6G) mobile network will be a highly heterogeneous system that integrates diverse technologies and communication paradigms, encompassing diverse consumer electronics (CE) devices, including human-type user devices and various internet of things (IoT) devices utilizing different protocols. In this paper, a novel cognitive unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV)-assisted mobile edge computing (MEC) heterogeneous network (HetNet) is proposed, where multiple cognitive UAVs equipped with MEC servers assist human-type users within the same signal modulation scheme, establishing one communication link, while selectively collecting data transmitted by the constrained application protocol (CoAP)-based IoT devices within different signal modulations, forming another communication link. Moreover, automatic modulation classification (AMC) technologies enable cognitive UAVs to communicate efficiently with multiple CoAP-based IoT devices. In this work, the reparameterization causal convolutional network (RepCCNet) is innovatively utilized to enable each cognitive UAV to achieve AMC for IoT devices. To fulfill the demand for high uplink data rate while minimizing overall system energy consumption, a superior multi-agent algorithm (RepCCNet-D3QN) is proposed to solve the problem of maximizing average energy efficiency, dueling double deep Q-network (D3QN) is applied to optimize user association factors and the three-dimensional (3D) trajectory of UAVs. Compared with other recent deep reinforcement learning (DRL) algorithms, the RepCCNet-D3QN algorithm can provide 45% energy efficiency improvement.

title: On the Detection and Mitigation of Coverage Holes by means of Relays in Cellular Networks
authors: J. Sánchez-González, J. Pérez-Romero, O. Sallent, A. Umbert
abstract: This paper presents a methodology for the detection of Coverage Holes (CHs) and their mitigation by means of either fixed relays or Relay User Equipments (RUEs), in cellular networks. First, a functional architecture is presented, with a description of the involved entities and their interactions for supporting the proposed solution. This includes different functionalities such as the collection of measurements, the detection of CHs by means of an unsupervised learning approach based on a clustering methodology, the identification of potential UEs to serve as RUE, the analysis of different RUE activation policies, and the placement of fixed relays. Since the considered scenario is fed by real measurements of space/time UE distribution, the proposed work can shed light to the potentials of of RUEs to address CHs in real situations. The obtained results indicate that the use of relays can mitigate the identified CHs and also reduce the power consumption with respect to the case of no relays. Moreover, the solution based on RUEs may lead to a performance close to the case of deploying fixed relays in terms of spectral efficiency and power savings, with reduced deployment costs.

title: Adaptive Resilient Output Feedback Control of Power Buffers in DC Microgrids
authors: Yongliang Yang, Zhenzhuo Shan, Guilong Liu, Qiaohui He, Xiaowei Zhao
abstract: Direct current microgrids (DCmGs) are attractive due to their high efficiency and ease of deployment, yet decentralized controllers remain vulnerable to false-data-injection (FDI) attacks and abrupt load changes. This article develops a resilient decentralized control architecture that achieves performance guarantees under bounded FDI attacks using only local measurements. The key idea is a decentralized high-gain observer that reconstructs each power buffer input impedance and stored energy while explicitly accounting for network coupling. On this basis, a smooth nonlinear feedback term provides attack compensation without chattering. In contrast to existing schemes, our design comes with explicit linear matrix inequality conditions that, first, certify asymptotic stability in the attack-free case and, second, ensure uniform ultimate boundedness of the closed loop under FDI, thereby yielding transparent tuning ranges for the controller and observer gains. Hardware-in-the-loop experiments, in which the DCmG is subjected to staged FDI attack windows and load steps, are conducted to demonstrate the proposed design with robust and practically tunable resilience for DCmGs.

title: Error-Aware Nonlinear Gain-Based PID Tracking Control: A Constructive and Self-Tuning Approach
authors: Kaili Xiang, Yongduan Song, Changyun Wen, Petros Ioannou
abstract: Conventional proportional-integral-derivative (PID) controllers face fundamental limitations in handling unknown nonlinearities and coupling effects, often requiring manual tuning or complex adaptation mechanisms. This paper introduces a novel nonlinear PID tracking control architecture while incorporating three innovative features: 1) analytically constructed error-aware gains with self-tuning capability, utilizing current, past, and predicted error information; 2) inherent anti-windup properties through nonlinear integral design; and 3) fully autonomous operation without the need for precise a priori knowledge of system dynamics or manual intervention. Unlike prior works mainly restricted to single-input-single-output (SISO) systems and only achieving uniformly ultimately bounded stability, our approach handles higher-order multiple input multiple-output (MIMO) systems with unknown coupled dynamics, achieving asymptotic tracking. The controller maintains structural PID simplicity while providing enhanced nonlinear compensation and robust adaptive performance. Comprehensive Lyapunov analysis rigorously validates stability for general normal form nonlinear systems, with superior performance in both regulation and time-varying reference tracking scenarios.

title: Detecting Obstetric Violence Tweets from Mexico: Annotation Guidelines and Classification with LLMs
authors: Mónica Vázquez-Hernández, Helena M. G´ómez-Adorno, Natalia Lerín-Hernández, Israel Islas Barajas, Orlando Ramos-Flores
abstract: This paper presents the construction and analysis of a manually annotated corpus of tweets related to Obstetric Violence (OV) shared on Twitter (now X). The study aims to identify different types of violence experienced by women during the perinatal period, as well as activism efforts that seek to raise awareness about OV. The methodology includes data collection through keyword filtering, manual annotation guided by typologies of OV, and a descriptive analysis using BERTopic to identify themes in the data. The tweets were classified into categories such as OV, Non-OV, and Activism, and further annotated based on narrator type and type of OV violence. The study also evaluates the performance of large language models (LLMs) including ChatGPT, Copilot, and Metas LLaMA for zero-shot classification of tweets, highlighting their limitations in accurately identifying nuanced cases of OV. The research contributes a labeled dataset, a detailed annotation guide, and insights into the challenges of detecting OV in social media texts. It underscores the importance of addressing the invisibility and normalization of OV in both healthcare and NLP research.

title: Bayesian neural network-assisted parameter estimation of the transmuted Teissier distribution for environmental data modelling
authors: P T Amrutha, C S Rajitha
abstract: Machine learning has evolved into a potent tool for analysing patterns and making predictions from complex data. In this machine learning era, we employed neural network techniques to estimate the parameters of statistical distributions. A primary advantage of probability distribution is its capacity to represent and analyse data effectively. Due to the complexity of the data, classical distributions are generalised or expanded to enhance the flexibility of the probability distributions. This study suggests that the modified Teissier distribution presented in this article serves as a more adaptable model of the Teissier distribution for the analysis of environmental data. The parameters of the proposed distribution are estimated utilising three distinct methodologies: Bayesian neural network (BNN), Bayesian estimation, and maximum likelihood estimation method (MLE). The BNN is achieved by combining the Bayesian estimation method with a neural network, indicating that both the BNN and the Bayesian estimation method commence with identical initial steps. The Bayesian estimation method is conducted using the approach of the Markov Chain Monte Carlo technique. The simulation results indicate that the BNN yields more accurate outcomes than the two conventional methods. The new distribution’s validity is assessed using the environmental data sets and compared with the Teissier distribution and two other established distributions. The discriminative metrics demonstrate that the modified Teissier distribution aligns more effectively with the data sets.

title: Multi-Level Embedded Spatial-Temporal Large Language Model for Traffic Prediction
authors: Kan Zhou, Xinmin Zhang, Bojian Chen, Shu Sun, Zhihuan Song
abstract: As a significant component of Intelligent Transportation Systems, traffic prediction attempts to forecast traffic trends in advance based on historical traffic characteristics. Although existing deep learning models for traffic prediction possess the capability to model spatial-temporal dependencies of traffic data, these methods tend to suffer from complex feature engineering and face challenges in natural language feature fusion and few-shot learning. In recent years, the rapid development of pre-trained Large Language Models (LLMs) has shown outstanding performance in time series analysis by leveraging their natural language comprehension and generalization ability. Inspired by LLM, this work proposes a novel Multi-level Embedded Spatial-Temporal Large Language Model (MEST-LLM) for traffic prediction. Specifically, we design multi-level embeddings of traffic data, which incorporate traffic historical features, dynamic spatial-temporal features, and prompt text features to model the complex spatial-temporal dependencies of traffic data and help LLM better understand traffic data through prompt text. Through fine-tuning strategies, LLM is capable of maintaining natural language comprehension and enhancing spatial-temporal prediction performance. Experimental results on four real-world traffic datasets demonstrated the excellent prediction ability and robustness of MEST-LLM in both sufficient and scarce data scenarios.

title: A Non-Contact Framework for Thyroid Disorder Screening Using Infrared Thermography and GLCM-based Neural Networks
authors: Nutan V. Bansode, Vaishali V. Ingale, Sudeshna Ingale
abstract: Thyroid-related disorders are increasingly common and are often identified only after noticeable symptoms appear, which delays timely intervention. This highlights the importance of developing simple and accessible screening approaches. Conventional diagnostic methods, including ultrasound imaging and cytological evaluation is reliable but requires specialized infrastructure and expert interpretation, limiting their use in large-scale or early screening scenarios. This study explores infrared thermography as a non-invasive and radiation-free technique for preliminary thyroid screening. A computationally efficient machine learning framework is developed with a focus on real-time applicability. Initially, thermal images are processed to localize the thyroid region using a U-Net-based segmentation approach. The segmentation model achieves a dice coefficient of 0.91 and Intersection over Union (IoU) of 0.87, ensuring accurate region extraction. Subsequently, texture features representing thermal distribution patterns are derived using GLCM. These features are then classified using a lightweight ANN designed to balance performance and computational efficiency. Experimental evaluation on both public and self-acquired datasets demonstrates a classification accuracy of 94.11% with low inference latency. The findings indicate that thermography combined with feature-based learning can serve as an effective tool for early-stage screening and decision support, particularly in resource-constrained environments. However, the proposed framework is intended to complement, rather than replace, conventional diagnostic procedures.

title: Design and Analysis of an FPGA-Based Authentication and Key Establishment Protocol for Mobile-Edge-Assisted RFID–IoT Systems
authors: Sundararaju Mugunthan, Venkatasamy Sureshkumar, Paramasivam Saravanan, Ruhul Amin
publish: IEEE Journal of Radio Frequency Identification
abstract: Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) plays a key role in enabling low-latency RFID–IoT applications by offering localized computation and storage resources. While this improves responsiveness and scalability, it also increases exposure to security and privacy threats. This paper presents an enhanced authentication and key establishment scheme designed to mitigate privacy and security attacks in mobile-edge-assisted RFID-IoT environments. The scheme incorporates password update and recovery mechanisms to enhance practical usability and resilience. Its correctness is verified using BAN logic, and an AMD Xilinx FPGA prototype demonstrates its practical performance. Comparative results show that the proposed scheme offers strong security with low computational and communication overhead, making it suitable for latency-critical edge applications.

title: Optimization of Breath VOC Sensors Using Porous Anti-Humidity Membranes and Machine Learning-Based Humidity Prediction
authors: Jung Yoon Sarah An, Somayeh Fardindoost, Mina Hoorfar
abstract: Breath-sensing technologies offer a non-invasive approach for detecting biomarkers in exhaled breath, however, high moisture content in exhaled air often compromises sensor–analyte interactions, reducing sensitivity, selectivity, and reliability. In this study, porous polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) and UiO-66@PDMS anti-humidity membranes were fabricated via a sugar-cube templating method and integrated with a commercial ethanol-sensitive Figaro gas sensor to systematically enhance sensor performance under high-humidity conditions. The sensors were tested with ethanol, acetone, and their mixture under dry (20–30% RH) and humid (70–80% RH, up to >90% RH) conditions. While porous PDMS membranes mitigated humidity effects, they significantly reduced analyte sensitivity. In contrast, UiO-66@PDMS membranes maintained high sensitivity to ethanol, achieving a 52% response to 50 ppm ethanol under humid conditions, outperforming the membrane-free sensor (41% response) and demonstrating enhanced selectivity and stability. To further improve sensor reliability, five supervised regression models—Gaussian process, random forest (RF), RF with hyperparameter optimization, k-nearest neighbors (KNN), and support vector machine (SVM) with hyperparameter optimization—were trained to predict relative humidity from sensor outputs. The RF model with hyperparameter optimization achieved 98% accuracy and R² = 0.998, providing robust correction of humidity-induced interference. Pearson correlation analysis showed a reduction from 0.708 to 0.190 after membrane integration, quantitatively confirming effective humidity mitigation. These results demonstrate that combining MOF-based anti-humidity membranes with machine learning enables reliable, accurate, and reproducible VOC detection in humid environments, offering a practical and scalable strategy for next-generation breath-based sensors.

title: Large Language Model-Assisted Evolutionary Neural Architecture Search for Cognitive Diagnosis
authors: Shangshang Yang, Linrui Qin, Xiaoshan Yu, Ziwen Wang, Ye Tian, Xueming Yan, Haiping Ma, Xingyi Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: Neural architecture search (NAS) has made remarkable progress in various field tasks and is also used in fields like cognitive diagnosis (CD) in intelligent education. Due to the optimization challenges of the tree-like and variable-length model topology raised by the search space, existing NAS for CD intuitively uses evolutionary algorithms and empirically develops a genetic operator based on basic tree modifications. Nevertheless, the operator's stochastic nature may impede rapid convergence, as the choice of modifications is random and ignores parent information, thereby failing to generate effective models from most parents. To this end, this paper proposes a novel evolutionary NAS approach for CD based on a tailored genetic operator, where the operator incorporates powerful large language models (LLMs) as its key components for tree-encoded model generation. Specifically, three roles of LLMs are defined in the LLM-assisted operator through structured prompt engineering that explicitly defines the LLM's role, constrains the architecture representation, standardizes the output format, and incorporates Hall-of-Fame guidance. The first LLM focuses on exploitation, enhancing model performance through minor incremental adjustments to existing architectures, the second emphasizes exploration, introducing innovative models through creative combinations that have not been previously considered, and the third aims at balancing the contributions of the first two, ensuring a harmonious integration of exploitation and exploration. In each generation, only one role of LLMs is used through an LLM role-switching strategy. Additionally, the empirically-built genetic operator is also employed for partial models' generation for better diversity. Experiments on several datasets show the performance superiority of models searched by the proposed approach over handcrafted models and automatically-designed models. Besides, the effectiveness of the LLM-assisted operator in accelerating algorithm convergence is validated.

title: Miniaturized GRIN Lens-in-Radome for Pattern Shaping in E-Band Automotive Radar
authors: Coen van de Ven, Abolfazl Haddadi, Carlos Biurrun-Quel, Andrés Alayón Glazunov
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This communication presents a miniaturized gradient-index (GRIN) lens integrated into the radome of an automotive radar sensor at 76–81 GHz, i.e., in the E-band, for azimuth beam shaping. The fully dielectric lens is tightly integrated with a Multi-Layer Waveguide (MLW) array antenna, yielding a compact footprint of 0.47 λ × 1.2 λ. A farfield averaging over simulations with different radome widths suppresses diffraction from radome-guided modes, isolating the contribution of the lens–antenna system in the design phase. Measurements of an 8-channel prototype show that the integrated lens broadens the azimuth half-power beamwidth from 50° to 120° (≈ 140° at −5 dB) while maintaining better than 10 dB return loss and a gain ripple of 4.3–7.9 dB across the band. The proposed approach provides a competitive beamwidth in a smaller form factor, enabling integration in automotive corner radar sensors.

title: Active Sampling and Gaussian Reconstruction for Radio Frequency Radiance Field
authors: Chi-Shiang Gau, Xingyu Chen, Wuqiong Zhao, Xinyu Zhang, Tara Javidi
publish: IEEE Journal on Selected Areas in Information Theory
abstract: Reconstructing Radio-Frequency (RF) Radiance Fields is essential for modeling signal propagation in wireless systems but remains challenging due to complex wave phenomena such as reflection, scattering, and diffraction—particularly when the environment’s geometry and material properties are unknown. Recent neural approaches, such as NeRF2, employ large differentiable models for RF reconstruction but require dense spatial sampling and compute-intensive per-environment training, limiting their practicality in dynamic or quasi-static scenarios. We propose a training-free, physics-assisted alternative based on a Gaussian Process (GP) with an adaptive spatio-temporal kernel, whose hyperparameters are estimated via maximum likelihood. Our model incorporates prior physical knowledge by representing the received signal as a linear combination of rays from different angles, allowing the GP to implicitly capture the spatial structure of the RF field in a physics-assisted manner. The kernel captures local structural and temporal patterns, while the GP framework naturally provides predictive uncertainty. Posterior variance guides active sampling, prioritizing regions of high information content to efficiently collect the most informative measurements. Experiments show that our method substantially reduces the number of required RF observations while maintaining high reconstruction fidelity, enabling real-time adaptation in dynamic environments and offering a scalable, data-efficient solution for RF field estimation.

title: SMSDNet: SAR Multi-Scale Dual-Domain Despeckling Network with Feature-Space Diffusion Refinement
authors: Tanveer Fatema Khan, Ramesh Kumar Thakur, Mansi Gyanchandani
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: The visual quality and interpretability of Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) images are significantly degraded by multiplicative speckle noise.This letter proposes SMSDNet, a multi-scale dual-domain deep learning framework for SAR image despeckling that jointly exploits Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) and Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) representations to capture complementary spectral characteristics. Unlike existing frequency-domain fusion methods, the proposed approach explicitly leverages phase-sensitive global frequency modeling (FFT) and energy-compact structural representation (DCT) through parallel multi-scale analysis modules, namely Multi-Scale Fourier Analysis (MSFA) and Multi-Scale Cosine Analysis (MSCA).The extracted dual-domain features are fused via residual learning to enhance both speckle suppression and structural preservation. Furthermore, a lightweight feature-space diffusion refinement module, formulated as an iterative residual denoising process with stochastic perturbation, is introduced to progressively suppress residual speckle while preserving fine details.Experimental results on simulated and real SAR datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art techniques in terms of both quantitative metrics and visual quality, while maintaining computational efficiency.

title: Design of an Approximate Recursive Multiplier Based on Probability-Driven Intercolumn Error Cancellation and Its Application in Deep Neural Networks
authors: Shuo Cai, Wen He, Huilei Huang, Zhengbang Long
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: This article proposes a probability-driven intercolumn error cancellation mechanism for approximate multiplier design, enabling an improved tradeoff between computational accuracy and hardware efficiency. By statistically characterizing the error behavior of approximate compressors and coordinating positive and negative error units, effective column-level error cancellation is achieved. Asubarray truncation strategy is further integrated into a recursive multiplier architecture to reduce partial product generation and accumulation overhead. Multiple approximate multipliers with different accuracy levels are implemented and synthesized in a 28-nm CMOS process. Compared with an exact <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$8{\,}\times{\,} 8$</tex-math> </inline-formula> multiplier, the high-accuracy ECM8-1 achieves near-lossless accuracy while reducing the power–area–delay product (PADP) by 35.6%, whereas the low-power ECM-TT22 achieves a reduction of up to 99.6%. In image processing applications, the high-accuracy and low-power designs achieve 99.8% and above 85.4% Structural Similarity Index (SSIM) in blurring, and 99.6% and 84.9% SSIM in edge detection, respectively. In quantized deep neural networks (DNNs) inference workloads, ECM8 incurs less than 0.3% accuracy loss on CIFAR-10, while ECM-O and ECM-T maintain near-exact performance on MNIST, demonstrating robustness across different precision levels and suitability for hardware-efficient DNN acceleration.

title: Distributed Dynamic Event-Triggered Fault Accommodation for a Class of Nonlinear Uncertain Multi-Agent Systems
authors: Ming Yuan, Dong Zhao, Christodoulos Keliris, Marios M. Polycarpou
abstract: This paper presents a distributed dynamic event-triggered fault accommodation leader-following consensus regulation strategy for a class of nonlinear uncertain multi-agent systems, remedying time-varying actuator faults and process faults that occur in the local agents and/or are propagated from interconnected neighboring dynamics. The networked system under consideration operates through a bidirectional intercommunication topology with asymmetric weights. Distinct from passive fault-tolerant control schemes and without requiring some a priori knowledge of the specific nature or type of multi-source faults, each local agent is equipped with a distributed fault accommodation control law driven by neural network-based learning algorithms that utilize local measurements and information exchanged only with its neighbors. Additionally, a less conservative Zeno-free dynamic event-triggered control algorithm with a quantitative compensation mechanism online is developed to achieve reduced data transmission loads, while maintaining satisfactory fault accommodation performance. The closed-loop system stability, minimum inter-event time, and cooperative tracking error boundedness in the presence of faults are rigorously investigated and proved. The effectiveness of the proposed scheme is verified through comparative analysis in a simulation example.

title: Group Multirole Assignment incorporating Agents’ Binding Relationships
authors: Shengyou Luo, Jiashuo Su, Sha Tao, Yifan Liu, Yin Sheng, Libo Zhang
publish: IEEE Systems, Man, and Cybernetics Magazine
abstract: Group Multirole Assignment (GMRA) optimizes group performance by assigning appropriate roles to agents based on qualification values. To help address challenges in complex collaborative environments, current GMRA research has explored relationships among agents and roles including conflict and cooperation. Binding relationships among agents are also common in real-world collaboration and commonly arise from factors such as information exchange and efficient collaboration. However, existing GMRA research ignores these binding relationships, potentially impairing team effectiveness and long-term development. Therefore, this paper models the binding relationships by distinguishing binding relationships into Role-Specific binding relationships and Role-Flexible binding relationships. We then extend the GMRA model by introducing a new set and a new matrix to account for these two types of relationships and formalize a new optimization problem. To address this formalized problem, we propose a novel GMRA collaborative model, called GMRA incorporating Agents’ Binding Relationships (GMRA-ABR). Specifically, we introduce two constraints in GMRA to ensure that the assignment of bound agents meets the binding requirements, and solve it using a GMRA-based method. Experimental results validate the model’s effectiveness in optimizing role assignment and its robustness to parameter variations.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Evidential Multi-Modal Fusion Network for Large-Scale Multi-Center Major Depressive Disorder Classification_supp1-3686914.pdf
authors: Chengyu Liu
abstract: The clinical diagnosis of major depressive disorder (MDD) has long relied on subjective assessments, underscoring the need for objective and quantifiable automated diagnostic methods. Existing neuroimaging-based multimodal fusion methods face three core challenges: the lack of mechanisms that dynamically assess modality reliability, the absence of effective prediction uncertainty quantification, and site effects in multi-center data that constrain model generalizability. To mitigate these issues, we propose an uncertainty-aware evidential multimodal fusion network for large-scale multi-center MDD classification. We design deep functional feature extractors (DFFE) and dual-stream hierarchical feature fusion (DS-HFF) modules to encode functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) and structural MRI (sMRI) features, respectively. An Evidential Multimodal Fusion (EMF) strategy based on Dempster–Shafer theory (DST) is designed to convert each modality's categorical support into subjective opinions with explicit uncertainty. Reliable cross-modal evidence integration is achieved through context-adaptive discounting and conflict-aware combination. Site-adversarial regularization is incorporated to learn site-invariant pathological feature representations. Evaluated on the REST-meta-MDD dataset comprising 1,601 subjects from 16 sites under a leave-one-site-out cross-validation (LOSO-CV) scheme, the proposed method outperforms recently published methods. Uncertainty analysis demonstrates superior calibration over Softmax and MC Dropout baselines, and the uncertainty-based rejection mechanism further improves the classification performance. Interpretability analysis identifies key brain regions highly consistent with established MDD pathophysiological findings, further validating the clinical reliability of the framework. Experimental results indicate that our proposed method has the potential to provide a robust, trustworthy, and interpretable solution for multi-center MDD diagnosis.

title: A Smart Projective Imaging and Navigation System for Oral and Maxillofacial Surgery
authors: Alston Zhang, Yi Wang, Hui Peng, Qingdong Zhang, Mingzhai Sun, Peng Yao, Shuwei Shen, Ru Zhang, Min Ye, Chuanjun Chen, Ronald X. Xu
abstract: Objective: In oral and maxillofacial surgery (OMS), surgical outcomes often depend significantly on the subjective experience of the surgeon. While conventional navigation systems, such as those using guide plates or fiducial markers, offer some guidance, they are hampered by instability, obtrusive hardware, and limited accuracy. Markerless navigation presents a promising alternative, but current approaches are constrained by a reliance on specific textures, scarce training data, and inefficient visualization. Methods: This paper introduces a Smart Projective Imaging and Navigation System (SPIN) that integrates synthetic data–driven learning, coaxial augmented-reality (AR) projection, and real-time structured-light 3D scanning for markerless dynamic surgical navigation. Our approach utilizes no more than two teeth as natural markers to enable high-precision pose estimation, even in low-texture regions. To overcome the data scarcity challenge, we develop a preoperative platform that generates a diverse synthetic dataset for robust AI model training, eliminating the dependency on real surgical imagery. Results: Experiments on dental phantoms, patient-specific phantoms, and representative clinical cases demonstrate that the SPIN system achieves submillimeter accuracy and exhibits strong robustness. Conclusion: The proposed SPIN system provides an accurate, intuitive, and markerless navigation solution for OMS. Significance: This work establishes a novel and clinically viable framework for dynamic surgical navigation, demonstrating significant potential for broad adoption in real-world surgical environments.

title: Theoretical Evaluation of Metal–Ferroelectric–Graphene Tunnel Junction With AA-, AB-, and ABC-Stacked Multilayer Graphene
authors: Pengying Chang, Guowei Ma, Enshu Xu, Jinbao Su, Lang Zeng, Yiyang Xie
abstract: A ferroelectric tunnel junction (FTJ) that uses metal–ferroelectric–graphene (MFGr) structure offers giant tunneling electroresistance (TER) effects. This article gives an efficient and physical device model for MFGr-based FTJ, which effectively captures the polarization switching and electron transport of semimetal–ferroelectric system. The semimetallic nature of graphene and its complex electronic band structures that is dependent on the number of graphene layer and the stacking sequences (such as AA, AB, and ABC stacks) are considered. The model is verified by comparing the simulations with the experiments of the Cr/CuInP<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>S<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</sub> (CIPS)/graphene-based FTJs that have monolayer and multilayer graphene as one electrode. Particularly, the effect of graphene stack sequence on TER ratio in MFGr FTJ is that ABC is larger than AB, followed by AA, which is revealed for the first time. The established model provides useful guidelines for quantitatively understanding the novel FTJ devices based on complex vdW heterostructures and emerging 2-D ferroelectric materials.

title: Nothing Is Free: Misogyny in the Mobile Gaming Industry
authors: Daria Loi

authors: Tangila Islam Tanni, Renita Washburn, Yan Solihin, Mary Jean Amon

authors: Feng Ding, Tingting Wang, Ruolin Li, Ying Jin, Junxiang Zhang, Shan Jin, Yicong Li, Xin Ye
