title: OAADet: One-stage Anchor-free Arbitrary Oriented Object Detector via Center-ness Shift Correction
authors: Chenlin Fu, Yingying Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Arbitrary Oriented Object Detection (AOOD) has gained significant attention in the field of remote sensing, owing to its remarkable localization capabilities. Existing AOOD detectors primarily utilize anchor-free approaches to avoid presetting a large number of anchors. However, the center-ness introduced by anchor-free methods exhibits shifting across branches, indicating a discrepancy in localization decoding for the same anchor point in classification and regression tasks. This negatively affects the loss calculation and classification scores, thereby limiting the performance of the detector. In this work, we propose a simple and efficient one-stage anchor-free arbitrary oriented object detector named OAADet that aims to correct center-ness shift by designing a consistent learning strategy as well as constructing a new head structure. First, we propose the Localization Capabilities Consistency Learning (LCL) strategy to dynamically integrate the discrepancy in localization decoding directly into the network’s optimization goals. Moreover, we design a Feature Selection-Interaction Head (SI-Head) to promote interactions between information from two branches as well as mitigate the misalignment between the receptive field and OBBs. Experimental results on four widely-used remote sensing datasets DOTA, HRSC2016, SSDD+, and HRSID show that our approach outperforms existing models. Without bells and whistles, OAADet not only achieves a 79.18% mean Average Precision (mAP) on the DOTA dataset but also showcases the least model complexity at this mAP performance level. Notably, the model complexity experiments demonstrate that our method reduces the number of parameters by up to 58.6 MB and the complexity by up to 285 GFLOPs at similar mAP performance level.

title: PV-Integrated Auxiliary Power Module Using Hybrid Multi-Input Multi-Output DC-DC Converter for Electric Vehicle Applications
authors: Majid Mehrasa, Naser Vosoughi Kurdkandi, Chunting Chris Mi
abstract: This paper proposes a hybrid multi-input multi-output DC-DC converter for integrating Photovoltaic (PV) unit and auxiliary power module (APM) to simultaneously supply low voltage/power loads inside electric vehicle (EV) as well as actively participate in EV battery management system (BMS). Defining three operation modes including charging, discharging and idle, the proposed converter guarantees multiple outputs isolated from EV-high voltage battery (EV-HVB) with significant safe operation regardless of the power flow direction between the auxiliary battery and EV battery cell. Compared to the existing APMs, the proposed PV-integrated APM provides much wider voltage ranges for internal loads based on low voltage auxiliary battery (LVAB) with a smaller voltage rate and size. Using pole placement method (PPM) as well as comprehensive large-signal and small-signal dynamic models, two closed-loop control systems are presented to enable the desirable current flow and regulated output voltages. To this end, the controllability of the proposed system is extensively assessed, and the effective state feedback gains are designed associated with the closed-loop system eigenvalues with the adjusted and sufficient stability margin. Both simulation and experimental results are employed to verify the noticeable contribution of proposed system along with the designed control technique to APM and BMS in EV applications.

title: Impedance-Spectrum-Based Internal Short-Circuit Diagnosis of Two Lithium-Ion Battery Types via Transfer Learning with Sample Imbalance Consideration
authors: Miao Bai, Chao Lyu, Dazhi Yang, Gareth Hinds, Dongxu Shen, Shaochun Xu, Yumeng Zhang
abstract: Internal short circuit (ISC) diagnosis is vital for ensuring the safety and reliability of battery systems. Electro-chemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), which provides comprehensive insights into internal battery dynamics, is a promising tool for ISC diagnosis. However, existing EIS-based methods often lack generalizability, as models trained on one chemistry cannot be readily applied to others. In particular, the imbalance between healthy and faulty samples further exacerbates the data acquisition burden required for both training reliable diagnostic models and achieving cross-chemistry transfer. To address these challenges, this work presents a preliminary exploration of a potential ISC diagnostic approach for cross-system application, using two representative lithium-ion battery chemistries, namely lithium iron phosphate (LFP) and nickel manganese cobalt (NMC). Diagnostic features are extracted from impedance spectra via the distribution of relaxation times (DRT), and a deep neural network is trained on a sufficiently large dataset from one chemistry. The model is then fine-tuned with limited data from another chemistry, enabling robust adaptation across material systems. To mitigate data imbalance, a variational autoencoder generates synthetic healthy samples, yielding a more balanced dataset. Experimental results indicate that the proposed method achieves a certain level of diagnostic accuracy and transferability between LFP and NMC cells, suggesting a possible pathway for EIS-based ISC diagnosis toward cross-system applications.

title: Risk-Assessment and Visual Perception of Autonomous Microrobots using Model-free Reinforcement Learning to Cope with Multi-challenging Environments_supp3-3686841.mp4
authors: Laliphat Manamanchaiyaporn

title: An Integrated Dual-Module Computer Vision System for IV Drip Rate and Fluid Level Monitoring
authors: Nishant Vasantkumar Hegde, Saksham Gupta, Atul Kumar Mishra, D Pratiba, P Ramakanth Kumar, K Sreelakshmi, T Shankar
abstract: Manual monitoring of intravenous (IV) therapy is labor-intensive and susceptible to human error, posing risks to patient safety. Existing computer vision-based approaches have addressed either drip rate estimation or fluid level monitoring in isolation, limiting their clinical applicability. This work presents and validates a dual-module computer vision framework for a practical, automated IV monitoring system that simultaneously addresses both tasks. For the critical task of drip rate estimation, we conduct a comparative study of two distinct deep learning paradigms on a diverse custom dataset of 3,458 images, evaluated on a strictly held-out test set: a state-of-the-art YOLOv8 object detector, achieving mAP<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.50</sub> of 95.34%, and a novel heatmap-based point-event regression network (ResNet-18 encoder with convolutional decoder), achieving a frame-wise accuracy of 88.44% and recall of 95.16%. The second module leverages a bespoke convolutional neural network (CNN) for fluid level classification, reaching a test accuracy of 95.26% on a public benchmark dataset. Finally, we demonstrate a proof-of-concept prototype that successfully integrates both modules into a unified, automated video-based application, validated on pre-recorded clinical video footage. End-to-end counting evaluation on a 7-video set spanning drip rates from 17 to 94 dpm shows the heatmap-based counter achieves 91.0% mean counting accuracy versus 77.5% for the YOLOv8-based counter, with the heatmap demonstrating markedly greater robustness at elevated infusion rates. By validating high-performance solutions for both monitoring tasks and demonstrating a clear path to integration, this study establishes a comprehensive framework for a holistic IV infusion monitoring system with the potential to enhance patient safety and reduce the burden on clinical staff.

title: Prescribed-Time-Performance-Based Attitude Tracking Control for Fixed-Wing UAVs With Input Amplitude and Rate Constraints
authors: Shuang Shi, Xiuqi Chen, Ju Jiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper proposes an attitude tracking control scheme for fixed-wing unmanned aerial vehicles with state, input amplitude and rate constraints, which is able to guarantee the prescribed-time prescribed performance. Firstly, a novel modified prescribed-time performance function is proposed to balance input and performance constraints, with its boundary adaptively adjusted according to the input saturation errors. Secondly, considering the input saturation that occurs during actual flight, an error transformation function and a hyperbolic tangent function are employed to address input amplitude and rate saturation, which prevents abrupt variations in input amplitude. Thirdly, a parameter adaptive estimation method is adopted to approximate the upper bound of external disturbances. On this basis, a back-stepping control method is investigated to guarantee that tracking errors meet the performance boundary within prescribed time under multiple constraints. Comparative simulations illustrate the effectiveness and merits of the proposed control scheme.

title: Adaptive Prescribed Performance Secure Consensus Control for High-Order Constrained Multi-Agent Systems With Time-Varying Powers and Arbitrary Initial States
authors: Ning Xu, Fansen Wei, Guangdeng Zong, Huanqing Wang, Xudong Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: In this paper, the problem of adaptive secure tracking control is investigated for a class of nonlinear high-order multi-agent systems with unknown time-varying powers, full-state constraints, and denial-of-service attacks (DoS). In contrast to existing studies that assume constant powers, this work simultaneously considers unknown time-varying powers, and time-varying full-state constraints. To address these challenges, the adding a power integrator method, nonlinear state-constrained functions, and adaptive control techniques are integrated into the proposed control scheme. Moreover, since the initial states of the system are often unpredictable, whereas conventional prescribed performance control methods require the follower output to remain within a fixed initial performance boundary. Therefore, an improved monotonic tube boundary function is constructed via a shift function. With this design, even if the initial consensus error lies outside the predefined performance boundary, the follower output can still achieve accurate tracking within a prescribed time, and suppressing overshoot and chattering during the transient phase. In addition, a distributed filter is developed to handle the discontinuous signals caused by DoS attacks. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed control scheme is validated through simulation results.

title: Multitrace Bayesian Orthogonal Matching Pursuit Incorporating Multiple Prior Knowledge for Seismic Resolution Enhancement
authors: Kun Li, Wei Ge, Xingyao Yin, Danping Cao, Shuangshuang Zhou
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Broadening the seismic frequency band and enhancing resolution play important roles in reservoir quantitative characterization. Seismic sparse representation provides a vital means of enhancing seismic resolution. Within the framework of sparse representation and Bayesian inference, we propose a multitrace Bayesian orthogonal matching pursuit method incorporating multiple prior knowledge for seismic resolution enhancement. Based on the maximum a posteriori probability solution, we first construct an objective function for sparse representation that couples the low-frequency impedance prior, seismic amplitude-envelope prior, and support-position prior. Incorporating the matching pursuit framework, we propose a multitrace Bayesian orthogonal matching pursuit algorithm for three-dimensional seismic data. Based on the sparse representation results, a broadband Ricker wavelet frequency and phase optimization method using particle swarm optimization is developed to achieve high-resolution seismic processing constrained by well-logging data. The numerical results show that the approach effectively improves the matching accuracy of the positions and amplitudes of seismic reflection coefficients. Applications to field three-dimensional seismic data volume and stratigraphic slice analysis demonstrate the practicality and validity of the proposed approach.

title: GaitSS: Gait Slices as a Set for Gait Recognition in Interfered Sequences
authors: Huakang Li, Wing W. Y. Ng, Yidan Qiu, Ying Gao, Jianjun Zhang, Sam Kwong
abstract: Video-based gait recognition suffers from environmental variations and deliberate disguises, which introduce information loss, redundancy, and complex temporal dependencies, ultimately degrading recognition performance. We propose GaitSS, a novel method that addresses these challenges by treating gait slices as a set rather than maintaining a fixed spatial order within each frame. This treatment effectively mitigates the effects of misalignment, information loss, and redundancy. In addition, we introduce a temporal frame exchange augmentation (TFEA) strategy to enhance the model’s ability to capture robust temporal gait features. We introduce the GOSI dataset, specifically collected to include diverse interference conditions, such as varying lighting, camera heights, and intentional disguises. We also evaluate our method on three widely used public gait datasets: CASIA-B, OU-MVLP, and GREW, providing a comprehensive assessment. Our method outperforms existing methods, yielding rank-1 accuracies (averaged across conditions) of 94.3%, 92.4%, 80.3%, and 42.3% on the CASIA-B, OUMVLP, GREW, and GOSI gait datasets, respectively. (42.3% is the average of NM, WH, and OC). Class activation maps show that GaitSS focuses more on discriminative features and less on interference regions, validating its superior handling of environmental variations. Ablation and validation experiments show that performance improvement is primarily due to treating gait slices as a set, rather than from image augmentation. GaitSS can be seamlessly integrated into existing gait recognition frameworks without introducing extra parameters or computational complexity, demonstrating strong potential for real-world applications.

title: RDG-Three-Branch Network with GAF-Based 1D-to-2D Image Transformation for Pedestrian Scene Recognition
authors: Jiale Wang, Yue Mei, Ming Xia, Chuang Shi
abstract: Scene recognition technology has been widely applied and plays a crucial role in various fields, including transportation, elderly care, smart homes, environmental monitoring, and autonomous driving. Wearable sensor devices have emerged as the core data source for pedestrian scene recognition technology, owing to their significant advantages such as low invasiveness and real-time data collection capabilities. However, constrained by issues like inadequate utilization of multimodal data and a narrow range of recognizable scenes, current technologies still struggle to achieve high recognition accuracy in fine-grained scenes. To address these challenges, this study proposes an RDG-Three-Branch Network for pedestrian scene recognition, which is based on a Gramian Angular Field (GAF)-based 1D-to-2D image conversion algorithm. This method overcomes the limitation of traditional approaches that can only recognize static scenes, enabling the further identification of fine-grained dynamic scenes where pedestrians are situated. Specifically, the proposed method first performs time synchronization and data filtering on six types of sensors embedded in wrist-worn and foot-worn devices to eliminate noise interference. Subsequently, the temporal sequence data is converted into 2D images via GAF to enhance the feature representation of different scenes, which are then fed into the designed RDG-Three-Branch deep learning network. Three branches within the network—the residual branch, depthwise separable branch, and global dependency branch—extract features of the 2D images from distinct perspectives, ultimately realizing the classification of pedestrian scenes. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method can effectively distinguish 14 types of pedestrian activity scenes both during the day and at night. Compared with two control groups using a single type of sensor device, the recognition accuracy is improved by 5.27% and 20.57%, respectively. Meanwhile, the performance of the designed model outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) models such as Swin-Transformer, Max-ViT, and DeiT, with accuracy improvements ranging from 1.05% to 8.41%. This work achieves fine-grained and high-accuracy multi-scene recognition for pedestrians.

title: Adaptive Convergence Indicator and Neighborhood Fuzzy Crowding Distance for Multimodal Multi-Objective Optimization
authors: Qianlong Dang, Xiaochuan Gao, Baosheng Li, Tao Zhan, Maoguo Gong, Xiaoyu He
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: Multimodal multi-objective optimization is a popular research direction characterized by different points on different Pareto optimal solution sets (PSs) in the decision space, corresponding to the same point on the Pareto front in the objective space. In addition, there may exist some multimodal multi-objective optimization problems (MMOPs) with local PSs. Finding all the equivalent global PSs and local PSs is the key to solving MMOPs. To solve these problems, this paper proposes a multimodal multi-objective evolutionary algorithm (MMOEA) based on the adaptive convergence indicator and neighborhood fuzzy crowding distance (ACEA-NFCD). The adaptive convergence indicator is able to calculate the local convergence indicator and global convergence indicator through the adaptive selection of neighborhood knowledge, which can effectively preserve all global PSs and local PSs and exclude the interference of isolated solutions. Moreover, in order to increase the diversity of the population, neighborhood crowding distances in the decision space and the objective space are integrated via fuzzy set theory, improving the truncation and selection process. The superior performance of ACEA-NFCD is demonstrated by experimentally comparing it with nine of the most advanced MMOEAs on four benchmark suites and one practical application of map-based distance minimization.

title: Narrowband Radar Micromotion Target Recognition via Dual-Domain Feature Fusion
authors: Rui Ma, Cunqian Feng, Weike Feng, Yang Wang
abstract: For micromotion target recognition via narrowband radar, single-domain features often provide incomplete information, while conventional multi-domain fusion models typically rely on a uniform branch structure, lacking adaptability to diverse data characteristics. To overcome these limitations, we propose a feature-level fusion method based on the Micro-Motion Dual-Domain Fusion Network (MMDDFNet). Core features extractable from narrowband radar are the radar cross-section (RCS) sequences and time-frequency (TF) spectrograms. Leveraging their distinct temporal and spatial properties, we construct a dual-domain collaborative recognition framework, where dedicated feature extraction branches are designed to accommodate their distinct characteristics. For the RCS branch, a gated recurrent unit (GRU) network is employed to exploit both amplitude and phase information, effectively capturing long-range temporal dependencies. For the TF branch, ResNet50 serves as the backbone network, augmented with a convolutional block attention module (CBAM) to enhance the extraction of discriminative local spatial features. Finally, a feature-level fusion module integrates the complementary information from both domains, enabling end-to-end micromotion target classification. To evaluate the method's performance, experiments were conducted on a dataset comprising four types of micromotion targets—wobbling, tumbling, precession, and nutation—generated using the FEKO full-wave electromagnetic simulation platform. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves higher recognition accuracy and robustness than existing typical methods.

title: Small-gain theorem for systems being ISS with respect to a set
authors: Sergey Dashkovskiy, Oleksiy Kapustyan
abstract: We consider two interconnected systems, each being input-to-state (ISS) stable with respect to a corresponding set A<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">i</sub>, i = 1,2, and look for stability conditions guaranteeing that the whole interconnection is ISS with respect to some set A. We explain that the relation between the given A<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sub>,A<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> and unknown A to be found is nontrivial and develop a small-gain theorem for this kind of interconnections, which allows to derive a suitable set A. The issue of minimality of A remains open. Motivating example, which also demonstrates the applicability of our result is provided.

title: Process-Informed Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Joint Radio-Compute Orchestration in Industrial O-RAN
authors: Xingqi Wu, Yuhui Wang, Junaid Farooq, Hakim Ghazzai, M. Majid Butt, Gianluca Setti
abstract: Industrial open radio access network (O-RAN) systems must support manufacturing workloads with dynamically varying communication and computation demands driven by production processes. Existing O-RAN orchestration mechanisms remain largely network-centric and fail to capture such process-induced variability. This letter proposes a decentralized process-informed reinforcement learning (RL) framework for joint radio and compute orchestration in industrial O-RAN. Distributed agents at radio units (RUs) adapt physical resource block (PRB) and compute allocations based on workload and latency requirements. Using a multi-agent dueling deep Q-network architecture, the approach improves latency fulfillment and reliability under nonstationary industrial traffic. Simulation results demonstrate consistent gains over network-centric multi-agent baselines.

title: Harnessing Diffusion Models for Image Manipulation With Partial Sketches
authors: Tengjie Li, Shikui Tu, Lei Xu
abstract: Controllable image structure editing has attracted increasing attention. While recent interactive point-based methods are convenient and realistic, they often lack fine-grained control over localized content. Partial sketches provide a simple yet expressive interface for local structure manipulation. However, existing partial-sketch-based manipulation methods relying on generative adversarial networks (GANs) suffer from limited generalization and fidelity. Moreover, although diffusion-based adapters excel at global conditioning (e.g., edge maps), localized editing with partial strokes remains challenging due to two key issues: effectively injecting sparse stroke conditions during denoising and preserving non-edited regions to avoid unintended changes. To address these challenges, we propose DiffStroke, a mask-free framework for localized image manipulation with partial sketches. We introduce trainable Image-Stroke Fusion (ISF) blocks to fuse source images and strokes at the feature level, enabling precise local shape control while maintaining appearance consistency. We further develop a self-supervised mask estimator to protect irrelevant regions without manual input. Specifically, we leverage Tweedie’s formula to estimate a clean latent image from noisy latents, blend the denoised result with the source, and train the mask estimator by minimizing the error between the blended latent and the target latent. Experiments on natural and facial images demonstrate that DiffStroke outperforms state-of-the-art methods on both simple and complex stroke-based editing tasks. DiffStroke can also be combined with text prompts to produce diverse and creative results. Code is available at https://github.com/CMACH508/DiffStroke.

title: Women in Engineering
authors: N/A

title: FA-EDNet: A Framework with Fuzzy Attention and Edge Detection for Forest Fire Smoke Detection
authors: Yinfang Wu, Xuanbo Xu
abstract: Forest fire smoke detection remains challenging due to smoke’s amorphous and semi-transparent appearance and its indistinct boundaries in complex forest scenes. This paper proposes FA-EDNet, a deep learning framework that integrates a Fuzzy Attention Mechanism (FAM) and an Edge Detection Auxiliary Module (EDAM) to enhance smoke feature representation and boundary localization. FAM strengthens low-frequency textures and blurred smoke regions, while EDAM employs Sobel and Laplacian operators to extract edge cues. Both modules are embedded into a multi-scale detection architecture based on a Feature Pyramid Network, and an auxiliary edge loss is introduced to further improve boundary accuracy. Experimental results on the D-Fire and self-constructed datasets demonstrate that FA-EDNet achieves superior accuracy with lightweight computational cost, making it suitable for real-time wildfire monitoring.

title: TMVS: Threshold-based Majority Voting Scheme for Robust SRAM PUFs_supp1-3687093.pdf
authors: Sara Faour
abstract: SRAM Physically Unclonable Functions (PUFs) derive secret keys from start-up values for inherent security benefits but suffer from reliability issues due to bit flipping. We introduce the Threshold-based Majority Voting Scheme (TMVS), a lightweight method that eliminates noise and mitigates bias in SRAM PUFs while retaining the simplicity of majority voting decoders used by repetition codes, without the significant entropy loss that repetition codes incur under biased responses. TMVS runs entirely in software, requires no cell-level bit-error rate qualification or SRAM redesign, and avoids the complex decoders of heavy error correcting codes. We derive closed-form expressions for decoding-error probability and expected memory, validate them on experimental data, and present a security analysis that provides exact formulas for min-entropy and secrecy leakage due to helper data and bias, identifying conditions under which TMVS achieves zero secrecy leakage. On a large public dataset, TMVS shows near-zero cross-chip secrecy leakage and preserves average conditional min-entropy above 1 bit despite biased, spatially correlated SRAM statistics. Compared with prior work, TMVS offers the smallest decoding complexity at the cost of a larger PUF size. In a representative configuration, TMVS generates a 128-bit key with failure probability 9.15 · 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−6</sup> and zero secrecy leakage at a bit-flip probability of 10%, requiring only ∼ 248k clock cycles on a 32-bit ARM Cortex-M0. These results show that TMVS is practical and implementation-friendly for resource-constrained, low-power devices.

title: A Novel Information Entropy-Guided Source-Free Open-Set Fault Diagnosis Approach
authors: Lanjun Wan, Hui Chen, Hongwei Tan, Jiaen Ning, Wei Ni, Keqin Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: In actual industrial applications, due to the data privacy protection, the source domain (SD) data are often not directly accessible, while the fault classes that do not belong to the SD often appear on the target domain (TD). To address these challenges, a novel information entropy-guided source-free open-set fault diagnosis (IESOFD) approach is proposed. Firstly, a graph convolutional network (GCN)-based source model is pretrained to extract robust features. The pre-trained source model is transferred to the TD to obtain the target model, which is fine-tuned to achieve the knowledge transfer from SD to TD. Secondly, a multi-group partition pseudo-label (MGPPL) strategy is designed. It initially partitions the TD samples into multiple groups with the pseudo-labels, selects some samples from each group as the known and unknown class sample sets respectively, and minimizes and maximizes the information entropy of the known and unknown class sample sets respectively, aiming to separate the unknown fault classes in the TD and improve the reliability of the pseudo-labels. Thirdly, a difficulty class learning (DCL) strategy is designed, which reduces the intra-class distances of the difficulty classes and uses different weights to learn each fault class by information entropy, so as to more effectively separate the known and unknown fault classes. Finally, extensive experiments are conducted to verify the effectiveness of the proposed approach, and the results confirm that the proposed approach can accurately identify the known and unknown fault classes in the TD while protecting the SD data privacy.

title: On-the-Fly Memory Decompression in GPU Featuring In-Partition Compressed Block Prefetching and Gzip Decompression for Scientific Computing
authors: Paul Xuanyuanliang Huang, Mingoo Seok
abstract: In modern graphics processing units (GPUs), scientific computing workloads are memory-bound due to the everincreasing data size and the limited off-chip memory bandwidth. The problem still remains with the adoption of high-bandwidth memory (HBM). To further increase memory bandwidth, researchers have proposed on-the-fly memory decompression (OTFMD) techniques that aim to store compressed data off-chip while decompressing the data on-chip. However, for scientific computing applications, a critical challenge of prior approaches is their low compression ratios, as the existing works target integer applications and thus employ simple compression algorithms. Employing a compression algorithm with a higher compression ratio, such as Gzip, for floating-point applications could be promising; however, it is not straightforward to adopt such an approach in the OTFMD scheme. For example, Gzip’s compressed block size is much larger than the GPU’s cache line. Additionally, after global-to-local memory mapping, the data in a Gzip block can be physically distributed across multiple DRAM channels, which complicates the decompression process. In this work, to address those challenges, we propose a Gzip-based OTFMD technique for GPUs for scientific computing.We propose an in-partition compressed block prefetching scheme, where data is compressed within each memory partition separately. Then, upon a request, we prefetch the compressed data into the last-level cache (LLC) while fully preserving memory-level parallelism. We also propose a new Gzip decompression accelerator that offers 5.4× higher throughput per unit silicon area than the state-of-the-art design. Lastly, we propose modifications to the GPU’s microarchitecture to fully leverage the proposed OTFMD technique. Our experimental evaluations show average performance improvements of 38% (up to 63%) for CuBLAS and 15% (up to 22%) for CuSPARSE applications compared to the baseline GPU model.

authors: Jingbo Zhou, Jun Xia, Siyuan Li, Yunfan Liu, Wenjun Wang, Yufei Huang, Changxi Chi, Mutian Hong, Zhuoli Ouyang, Shu Wang, Zhongqi Wang, Xingyu Wu, Chang Yu, Stan Z. Li

title: Modeling the Impact of a VR Campus Tour on University Admissions Funnel Conversion
authors: R. T. Galeev, D. M. Fedorov, I. V. Iakovlev
abstract: In the context of the digital transformation of higher education, universities face the challenge of attracting prospective students from geographically remote regions for whom an on-campus visit is often inaccessible. Campus visits are recognized as a critical factor in institutional choice; however, virtual campus tours can partially compensate for their absence. This paper proposes a formal admissions funnel model that incorporates a VR campus tour as a distinct interaction between a prospective student and the university, along with a scenario based parametrization grounded in published data on the impact of campus visits and virtual tours on conversion metrics. As an illustrative case, the paper considers Kazan National Research Technical University named after A. N. Tupolev – KAI (KNRTU-KAI) and additionally analyses the 2024 admissions statistics in terms of average and cut-off scores. It is shown how external estimates (reported application increases and inquiry-rate improvements associated with virtual tours) can be used to construct conservative scenarios for improvements in conversion and cohort quality that are subject to further empirical validation.

title: Surfing the SWAVES: Lifecycle-aware Service Placement in MEC
authors: Federico Giarrè, Holger Karl
publish: 2026 29th Conference on Innovation in Clouds, Internet and Networks (ICIN)
abstract: In Multi-access Edge Computing (MEC) networks, mobile users can offload virtual network functions (VNFs) to edge clouds (ECs) to meet stringent latency requirements through proximity. Under these requirements mobile users require VNFs to migrate across edge nodes as handovers occur. This process is non-trivial when ECs have limited resources and VNFs instantiation involves time-consuming operations, i.e., lifecycle operations. Ignoring lifecycle during placement decisions leads to severe service disruptions, as VNFs may not be ready to serve users when needed. To mitigate this issue, VNFs can be proactively instantiated at anticipated future locations, trading improved service continuity for increased resource consumption. Since exact future user locations are generally unknown, placement decisions must rely on connectivity forecasts, which are inherently imperfect and introduce an additional accuracy-resource tradeoff. We propose SWAVES, a lifecycle-aware VNF placement framework that explicitly accounts for instantiation delays and forecasting uncertainty. Simulation results show that by considering lifeycle operations and future forecasts, SWAVES closes the gap with an oracle approach that knows connectivityߣs ground truth. It also improves by orders of magnitude the number of unsuccessful packets with respect to other approaches ignoring lifecycle operations.

title: Jointly Elements Allocation and Trajectory Design for Optical RIS-Assisted UAV Communications in Low-Altitude Obstructed Environments
authors: Simeng Feng, Jinchao Qin, Chenyan Gao, Baolong Li, Wanting Wang, Qihui Wu
abstract: Uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) exhibit significant potential as mobile relays for establishing line-of-sight (LoS) links in free space optical (FSO) communications. However, providing reliable FSO communication to multiple users in obstructed environments remains a critical open problem. The integration of UAVs with optical reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (ORIS) further broadens the application prospects for FSO in multi-user environments with non-line-of-sight (NLoS) conditions. Therefore, this paper proposes an ORIS-assisted UAV relay system for FSO communications operating in obstructed low-altitude scenarios, aiming to provide services for multiple ground users. To maximize the average user throughput, a hungarian algorithm assisted proximal policy optimization (HA-PPO) method is proposed, which jointly optimizes ORIS elements allocation and UAV trajectory. Numerical simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach effectively plans UAV trajectories to avoid obstacles and maintain LoS links, while achieving higher average throughput than its counterparts.

title: HOVER: A Mobility-Aware UAV Placement Strategy for Highway V2X Connectivity
authors: Abdulhakim Abogharaf, Kshirasagar Naik
publish: 2026 29th Conference on Innovation in Clouds, Internet and Networks (ICIN)
abstract: Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication enhances road safety and efficiency, but sparse terrestrial roadside units (TRSUs) and fast highway mobility cause transient coverage gaps. Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) can complement TRSUs by providing flexible, line-of-sight connectivity and on-demand coverage extension. Existing UAV-aided solutions mainly optimize instantaneous coverage, ignoring sustained connectivity. We propose HOVER, a mobility-aware UAV placement strategy that maximizes vehicle-infrastructure association time under 3GPP-based access and backhaul constraints and addresses the UAV placement via particle swarm optimization (PSO). In simulation, HOVER outperforms two recently proposed UAV placement strategies, namely UAV-IoV and MOURD, achieving a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 6 - 2 3 \%}$</tex> CPM (collective perception messages) delivery gain, maintaining sub-millisecond latency, and reducing UAV movement by up to 45%, demonstrating enhanced reliability and energy efficiency.

title: Prospects for Using Digital Twins of Power Transformers in AC Traction Substations
authors: Yu. N. Makarenko, I. A. Khudonogov, E. Yu. Puzina
abstract: The intensive development of our country's technological complex and the steady growth of energy consumption are currently giving rise to new challenges in the management and monitoring of electric power systems. At the same time, there is a steady trend toward optimizing operational processes by reducing the share of manual labor, which can be achieved through large-scale digital transformation of the country's economic sectors, including railway transport. The concept of digital twins appears to be a highly effective tool in this context. The technology is becoming increasingly important in the electric power industry in general, and in railway power supply in particular, as it enables the creation of detailed virtual models scalable from individual types of electrical equipment, such as power transformers, to complex, distributed energy systems. This, in turn, ensures increased efficiency in the operation, maintenance and management of real physical assets, which could ultimately lead to a large-scale transformation of the country's entire energy sector.

title: Development of Improved Algorithms for the Operation of Interconnected Electric Drives of Continuous Hot-Dip Galvanizing Unit
authors: A. A. Nikolaev, I. I. Maksimov, V. D. Kudimov, N. R. Matushkin
abstract: This article discusses the issues related to improving product quality during continuous strip processing through the integration of improved electric drive control algorithms. The main focus is on the strip fluctuations caused by acceleration or deceleration of the entry looper. As part of the study, an analysis of the existing operating modes of interconnected electric drives on continuous hot-dip galvanizing unit using horizontal storage devices was conducted. Problems with strip tension fluctuations during transitional modes of the electric drives of storage devices were identified. This has a negative impact on the stability of the strip passing through the main nodes of the technological zones of the units, which leads to the appearance of defects on the strip surface and a forced reduction in processing speed. Improved control algorithms for interconnected electrical drives of continuous processing units using an automatic torque control system with speed correction and adaptation to the changing parameters of elastic elements, looper drum cables, were developed to solve this problem.

title: Multispectral Data Clustering Based on a Modified Grey Wolf Optimizer
authors: Philip S. Bulyga, Vladislav I. Danilchenko, Elmar V. Kuliev, Marat B. Shauliev
abstract: The paper addresses the problem of clustering multispectral Earth remote sensing data, which are characterized by high dimensionality of the feature space, pronounced inter-band spectral correlation, and spatial–spectral heterogeneity of observed scenes. These properties significantly complicate the application of conventional data analysis methods. In view of these challenges, the authors propose a modified bio-inspired Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO) tailored to solving the NP-hard problem of global optimization of clustering partitions. The proposed algorithm is enhanced through the integration of dynamic leadership reallocation mechanisms, a stratified organization of agents’ search activity, and adaptive restoration of population diversity. These mechanisms are embedded directly into the evolutionary population update procedure, which reduces the likelihood of premature stabilization of the agent hierarchy and improves the robustness of the optimization process under the complex structural characteristics of the optimization landscape. The effectiveness of the developed approach is validated through extensive experimental studies conducted on multispectral scenes acquired by the Sentinel-2 satellite system using a stratified graph-based data representation. The experimental results demonstrate that the modified algorithm achieves improved integral clustering quality metrics, accelerated convergence dynamics, and enhanced robustness to spectral noise distortions when compared with the classical GWO, ant colony optimization, spectral clustering, and the k-means method.

title: A Quantization Model of Membership Function Sensitivity in Fuzzy Logic–Based Request Distribution
authors: Sanjar S. Kenjaev, Akmal Akhatov, Maftuna Qosimovna Nishonova
abstract: Efficient request distribution is essential for ensuring stability and performance in modern distributed and cloud based systems. Traditional load balancing methods rely on deterministic rules and are often unable to handle uncertainty and dynamic changes in server conditions. Fuzzy logic based request distribution improves adaptability by using linguistic representations of system states. However, the effectiveness of such systems depends on the sensitivity of membership functions used during fuzzification. This paper presents a quantization model for evaluating and optimizing membership function sensitivity in fuzzy logic based request distribution. The proposed approach selects suitable membership functions according to the semantic characteristics of server load states. Experimental results show improved decision accuracy, better load balance, and reduced uncertainty compared to conventional fuzzy models.

title: Identification and Prioritization of Risks in the Graduate Thesis Preparation Process Based on a Cognitive Model Formed from Psycholinguistic Experiment Data
authors: Aleksandr Petrosian, Ashot Kazarian
abstract: The article proposes an approach to the diagnosis and prioritization of risks in the process of preparing graduate qualification theses (Thesis) based on a cognitive model. The approach is based on adapting the "attack surface" metaphor from the field of information security to the educational environment, where the thesis preparation process is considered as a system of interconnected components, vulnerabilities, and threats. The methodological basis of the study is cognitive modeling of expert representations, reconstructed using psycholinguistic methods. The cognitive model is formed based on data from a directed associative experiment involving academic supervisors from Moscow Polytechnic University specializing in Information Technology and Management. The obtained empirical data are interpreted in the form of associative networks, which reflect the implicit connections between the components of the thesis preparation process, their vulnerabilities, and possible negative outcomes. A comparative analysis of the cognitive maps of the two expert groups revealed both common and field-specific key problem areas, which attests to the influence of the professional profile on the perception and prioritization of risks in the process of academic supervision.

title: Business Strategy Transformation and Optimization Strategies Driven by New Quality Productivity
authors: Shasha Chang, Jing Wang, Daowen Ren
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 International Conference on Digital Transformation and Management
abstract: None

title: Binary Classification of Virtual Machines in Data Centers to Optimize Resource Allocation
authors: Georgii Promyslov
abstract: This paper considers the problem of optimizing resource consumption in cloud data centers (DCs). It proposes introducing a binary classification of virtual machine types based on their computing resource consumption patterns to improve the efficiency of DCs resource management. Traditional resource allocation algorithms often rely on the assumption of (quasi-)stationarity of resource consumption. However, this assumption can be violated in the presence of VMs that can dynamically change their behavior and consume all available resources. We introduce a division of VMs into two classes: greedy VMs and standard VMs. The proposed classifier is the variance of changes in VM resource consumption over time. For VMs, classification methods for their type are analyzed and compared. For the constructed cloud system model, the performance of unsupervised classification methods—Otsu's method, k-means, and Gaussian mixtures—is analyzed. The obtained results are compared. It is shown that Gaussian mixture models provide the best classification quality and stability of results. The obtained findings relate to the field of cloud computing and intelligent resource management of distributed computing systems and confirm the practical applicability of the proposed approach.

title: Highly Detailed Simulation for Connected Automated Vehicle Cooperative Driving
authors: Andrei Fizulin, Ilia Dolgov, Andrei Karpukhin
abstract: End-to-end simulation of connected and automated vehicles requires consistent fidelity across mobility, environment modeling, V2X radio propagation, and decision-making/control modules. However, 2D representations of complex road infrastructure often fail to capture critical signal propagation dynamics, leading to overly optimistic connectivity assumptions. This paper presents a unified workflow within CAVISE that integrates map-based scene preparation with microscopic mobility simulation. The proposed framework incorporates a comparative analysis of trace-driven propagation in 3D versus a simplified 2D baseline, and a modular interface for integrating Autonomous Intersection Management (AIM) models. Collectively, these capabilities enable high-fidelity Cooperative Driving Automation (CDA) experiments. Leveraging ray tracing via Using Sionna RT at 5.9 GHz with the same radio and solver configuration for both geometry variants, we show that planar reduction in multi-level road infrastructure can remove physically present occlusions and substantially distort signal-loss dynamics. In a bridge overpass case study, the 2D baseline eliminates an occlusion interval observed in 3D, changing the outage behavior from intermittent to consistently connected and yielding an average signal-loss shift on the order of 10 dB. These propagation-induced biases highlight the inability of planar models to capture vertical occlusions necessitating 3D-aware communication modeling to ensure the validity of cooperative driving automation and intersection control evaluations.

title: Edge-Oriented Evaluation of CNN-Based Vehicle Detection on Embedded V2X Platforms
authors: Marinelio Chintri, Ioannis Seitoglou, Vasileios Efthymiou, Nikos Kekatos, Sophia Petridou, Stylianos Basagiannis
publish: 2026 29th Conference on Innovation in Clouds, Internet and Networks (ICIN)
abstract: Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) systems increasingly depend on low-latency perception pipelines that must operate efficiently across the cloud-edge-vehicle continuum, often under constrained embedded hardware. While Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) achieve strong accuracy on high-end platforms, their performance shifts significantly in edge environments due to limited CPU parallelism, memory bandwidth bottlenecks, cache pressure, and degraded sensor input quality. Building on our earlier offline evaluation of state-of-the-art detectors, this study investigates the real-time deployment feasibility of representative single-stage and two-stage CNN architectures, specifically for vehicle detection, on embedded <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{V} 2 \mathbf{X}$</tex> compute nodes, including near-edge Roadside Units (RSUs) and far-edge in-vehicle platforms. Empirical results reveal a pronounced divergence: lightweight single-stage models sustain real-time performance by leveraging compact activation flows and shallow feature hierarchies, while region-based and deeper networks incur severe latency overheads due to memory-bound operations and constrained instruction-level parallelism. Our deployment-based measurements underscore the limits of complex perception models at the edge and emphasize the need for a re-architected V2X perception stack that balances model complexity with hardware constraints, sensor fidelity, and cooperative sensing capabilities.

title: Ultra-Short-Term Wind Power Prediction Method Based on Dual-Model Collaboration and Dynamic Physical Constraints
authors: Zhengran Zhang
abstract: Ultra-short-term wind power prediction is a key technology for ensuring the safe and stable operation of the power grid and the efficient consumption of wind energy. To address the issues of insufficient prediction accuracy caused by the sparse low-power samples at a single station and the jitter of model switching across intervals, a method for ultra-short-term wind power prediction based on the construction of a dual-model framework using LightGBM and the switching of hysteresis thresholds is proposed. Firstly, through a hierarchical oversampling strategy, the distribution of low-power samples is optimized, and LightGBM models adapted to low-power (0-5MW) and normal power (5-50MW) are constructed respectively; Secondly, a hysteresis threshold mechanism is introduced to avoid frequent model switching caused by power fluctuations, improving the stability of the prediction curve; Finally, by integrating dynamic physical constraints such as icing and precipitation in multiple scenarios, the prediction results are calibrated to meet engineering requirements. Experimental results based on the measured data from a power plant in Jilin Province show that in the 0-4h ultra-short-term prediction, the overall standardized root mean square error (NRMSE) reaches 3.21%, the prediction accuracy rate (Pr) increases to 92.3%, and the accuracy of the low-power segment is improved by more than 65% compared to the traditional single LightGBM model, effectively solving the problems of prediction deviation and model jitter, and providing reliable technical support for real-time power grid dispatching.

title: Potential Control of Industrial Mobile Robots Group in Dynamic Environment Using Modified Bug Algorithm
authors: Mikhail Kozhin, Nikolay Filimonov
abstract: A problem of industrial mobile robot group control in dynamic environment is considered. The paper focuses on the development of potential control of industrial mobile robot group in dynamic environment algorithm. The proposed approach is based on a modified bug algorithm, which combines artificial potential fields methods with classical bug algorithms. Possibility of applying the developed algorithm in an environment with dynamic obstacles is investigated. The algorithm is extended to address the control of industrial mobile robots group. Specifics of industrial mobile robots group potential control strategy are considered. Special attention is given to inter-robot interactions and collision avoidance. A computer simulation of the developed algorithm was conducted in CoppeliaSim simulator environment using Python scripts.

title: Shift Register Implementation in Self-Timed Circuits
authors: Yuri Stepchenkov, Yuri Diachenko, Dmitri Stepchenkov
abstract: This article focuses on the self-timed (ST) shift register development optimized for use with synchronous and ST environments. Combinational ST circuits traditionally employ dual-rail data encoding with a spacer. The state of the dual-rail signal reflects the information bit and its current phase: working or spacer. They do not require a phase control signal to synchronize the data flow. This leads to certain specific features of their use in memory units, i.e., triggers, counters, and registers, which actively interact with the combinational ST environment. This article examines the design features of an ST shift register with dual-rail data inputs and outputs and proposes implementation options that ensure their use as serial-to-parallel and parallel-to-serial ports, ring shifters, and other ST register units. Both the cause-and-effect relationship between the data input and the phase control input determine the ST shift register circuitry specifics. The proposed implementation options for the input, internal, and output bits of the ST shift register, designed in the template forms, provide ST shift register synthesis formalization and automation based on the synthesized circuit's original Verilog description.

title: Position and Velocity Estimation Under Imperfect Timing-Frequency Synchronization in MIMO-OFDM Communications Systems
authors: Liheng Zhou, Zijun Gong, Ying Cui
abstract: Leveraging communications signals for localization has attracted increasing attention. However, most existing studies face two limitations. First, timing-frequency synchronization (TiFSync), essential for reliable demodulation, is often ignored in modeling, leading to over-optimistic bounds and degraded accuracy in localization. Second, joint position and velocity estimation (PaVE) has not been fully investigated, including the analysis of the requirement for local identifiability and the corresponding low-complexity algorithm design. To address these issues, we propose an uplink orthogonal frequency division multiplexing multiple-input-multiple-output (OFDM-MIMO) PaVE framework under imperfect TiFSync and multipath conditions. An equivalent system model is first established to demonstrate the potential of PaVE. We then derive the Fisher information matrix (FIM) and Cramér-Rao lower bound (CRLB). By analyzing the FIM rank, we examine the local identifiability of our PaVE framework. The results show that four synchronous base stations (BSs) are required to ensure the local identifiability of PaVE of an asynchronous mobile device. We also develop a decentralized two-step method for PaVE: (i) estimating the channel parameters through the multi-dimensional discrete Fourier transform (DFT)-based interpolation; (ii) performing PaVE based on a weighted least squares (WLS) estimator, which is solved by the Gauss-Newton method with a good initial guess. This algorithm only requires the exchange of channel parameter estimates and TiFSync adjustments among BSs, resulting in a very low communications overhead. Simulation results demonstrate that our proposed PaVE framework achieves fast convergence and near-CRLB accuracy. Comparisons with existing works confirm its reliability in practical scenarios.

title: HEFLGuard: Backdoor Detection in Homomorphic Encryption-based Federated Learning
authors: Congyi Li, Peizhuo Lv, Jinwen He, Kai Chen, Hong Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: Homomorphic encryption-based federated learning (HEFL) strengthens privacy by aggregating encrypted model updates, but it also renders existing backdoor defenses that assume plaintext updates inapplicable. We present HEFLGuard, a single-server backdoor detection framework for HEFL in which the server constructs overlapping validation models from encrypted client groups and clients locally compare logits of the global and validation models on benign samples to expose backdoor behavior. HEFLGuard further combines consistency verification across non-IID validation groups with Byzantine fault-tolerant aggregation of client reports, ensuring robustness under heterogeneous data and Byzantine participants. We evaluate HEFLGuard on seven vision/text benchmarks under three backdoor types across IID and non-IID settings. HEFLGuard consistently reduces ASR from near 100% to nearly the nobackdoor level while keeping the drop in clean accuracy within 2.5%. Compared with prior work, HEFLGuard achieves higher robustness and deployability.

title: Increasing the Reliability of Distribution Network Equipment and Flexible Manufacturing Systems Based on Coordinated Relay Protection and Intelligent Voltage Control
authors: Huseyngulu Bayram Guliyev, Nijat Ramiz Huseynov, Ganima Huseyingulu Huseynova
abstract: In modern industrial and power engineering systems, ensuring reliable, uninterrupted operation of production processes and energy supply in compliance with quality indicators is one of the key priorities. In the presented study, a comprehensive analysis is carried out of the coordinated control of relay protection and voltage regulation in electric power distribution networks, alongside the automation of technical quality control in the production process of aluminum evaporators within flexible manufacturing environments. In the SCADA-based FMS production environment, real-time sensor data from equipment, industrial robots, manipulators, and furnaces are collected to enable the formation and monitoring of product quality indicators during technological process control. On the other hand, under conditions of nonlinear load variations and uncertainty of voltage regimes in distribution power networks, ensuring the selectivity of relay protection and implementing adaptive voltage regulation based on fuzzy logic enhance the continuity and reliability of power supply. The conducted analyses demonstrate that the intellectualization of technical control systems in manufacturing and the application of adaptive protection and control mechanisms in power systems contribute both to improving product quality and to enhancing the reliability and power quality indicators of energy systems. The obtained results have scientific and practical significance for the development of smart manufacturing and smart distribution networks.

title: What Happens When CHI Reaches 30,000 Submissions in 2030? Preparing for the Tsunami of AI-Accelerated Papers
authors: Johannes Schöning
abstract: None

title: MKD-Net:A Multi-Kernel Downsampling Network for Adjacent and Small-Scale Ship Detection in SAR Images
authors: Yuhu Qiao, Zhihui Xin, Guisheng Liao, Penghui Huang, Rui Wang, Jiahui Wu
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Synthetic aperture radar (SAR), as an important remote sensing technology, is playing an increasingly important role in marine monitoring. Nevertheless, in ship detection tasks of SAR images involving complex near-shore backgrounds, blurred edges of adjacent targets, and dense small ships, false alarms and missed detections remain difficult to avoid. To this end, this letter proposes a ship detection network, termed MKD-Net, which is based on a multi-kernel downsampling (MKD) module. This method integrates two key designs with low parameter complexity. The MKD transfers spatial information into the channel domain and extracts information using multi-kernel convolution, which can obtain features under different receptive fields while preserving fine-grained image details. The content-adaptive coordinate attention (CACA) introduces a dual-layer gating mechanism that consists of basic learnable weights and content-adaptive weights. Compared with other methods, the proposed method uses only 2.9M parameters. Furthermore, it can effectively detect adjacent targets in complex backgrounds, as well as dense small-scale targets. The proposed method achieves optimal detection performance.

title: TARONet:Topology-Aware Road Extraction and Vectorization with Occlusion Reasoning in Remote Sensing
authors: Dongen Guo, Lin Tan, Ying Xia, Bohong Liu, Jiangfan Feng
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: With the rapid development of remote sensing technology, the extraction of road information from high-resolution remote sensing images has attracted widespread attention in various fields. However, existing methods still face challenges such as road fragmentation and topological discontinuity when dealing with complex scenes with severe occlusions.To address these challenges, we propose a multi-branch network named Topology-Aware Road Extraction and Vectorization with Occlusion Reasoning (TARONet), which simultaneously predicts road point locations and feature representations to generate complete road vector maps. Building on this foundation, an Adaptive Strip Convolution Module (ASC) is introduced to enhance the perception of curved and fragmented roads and improve feature extraction in intersection areas. Additionally, a Star Cross Attention Module (SCA) is proposed to explicitly fuse spatial features by integrating multidirectional contextual information through a star-shaped topological structure. To evaluate the performance of the proposed network, a dataset focusing on road occlusions in mountainous regions was constructed. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior performance over existing approaches on public datasets such as City-scale and SpaceNet, as well as on the self-built dataset, particularly exhibiting superior structural reasoning and topological recovery capabilities in heavily occluded areas. The source code and dataset are publicly available at https://github.com/tanlincn/ TARONet.

title: 2D Root MUSIC Approach for Delay-Doppler Parameter Estimation for DFRC-OTFS Modulation
authors: Akshay S. Bondre, Christ D. Richmond, Nicolò Michelusi
abstract: Orthogonal time frequency space (OTFS) is a modulation scheme with properties that are particularly well-suited for integrated sensing and communications (ISAC). The structure of the scheme enables transmission of a dual-function radar communications (DFRC) OTFS frame consisting of pilot and data symbols, with the flexibility to select the pilot symbols to form a desirable sensing radar waveform. In this paper, a two-dimensional (2D) root-MUSIC approach is presented to estimate the delays and Doppler shifts corresponding to multiple scatterers using such an OTFS waveform. Firstly, the MUSIC approach for a single pilot symbol is presented, along with a discussion highlighting the challenges in extending this method to the multiple-pilot scenario. Such an extension proves to be important in practical scenarios, since multiple pilot symbols can provide coherent integration gain for delay-Doppler (DD) estimation, enabling the transmitted waveform to have a lower peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR). Thus the proposed method is extended for multiple pilots, and the widely used linear frequency modulated (LFM) chirp pilot pulse is shown to possess desirable properties for applying the proposed MUSIC approach. Cram´er-Rao bounds are derived and used as a benchmark for the performance of the 2D MUSIC algorithm. Numerical results demonstrate that a DFRC OTFS frame with an LFM pilot sequence can achieve the same estimation accuracy with a lower PAPR as compared to an OTFS frame with a single pilot symbol.

title: Observers for timed DESs with observation delays and losses
authors: Dimitri Lefebvre
abstract: This paper is about observation and states estimation for timed discrete event systems with observation delays and losses. This topic is particularly important for networked discrete event systems. On the one hand, in order to explicitly incorporate timing aspects, labeled timed automata under specific time semantics with a single clock and time interval constraints are used at the modeling level. On the other hand, delays and losses in observations sequences are specified according to delayed observable events and a set of release locations where the delayed observations are outputed. This release observation mechanism is added to the timing specifications and the resulting model is transformed into clock interval automata with release mechanism for which determinisation is obtained as an extension of the untimed reasoning by representing observation of the advance of time with a onetime unit tick event. Our main motivation for this research is to show how the time information and the release of delayed observations both refine the analysis of information flow. Potential applications to safety and privacy issues are discussed.

title: WINTER: Wrapped Interval Normalization for Elevation Representation in Stereo 3-D Sound Event Localization and Detection
authors: Jun-Wei Yeow, Ee-Leng Tan, Santi Peksi, Woon-Seng Gan
abstract: Stereo 3-D Sound Event Localization and Detection (SELD) faces a fundamental geometric conflict: the simultaneous regression of unitless angular direction and unbounded physical distance. This heterogeneity destabilizes training, as large-magnitude distance errors often overwhelm fine-grained directional gradients in the loss landscape. To resolve this, we propose WINTER (Wrapped Interval NormalizaTion for Elevation Representation), a novel embedding that projects the heterogeneous target space onto a unified, homogeneous manifold. By mapping scalar distance to the latent elevation axis of stereo signals, WINTER transforms the problem into a single-head regression on a pseudo 3-D unit sphere. This geometric unification restores the validity of angular metrics and enables the introduction of Stereo-DICE, a differentiable proxy for the polyphonic F1-score that optimizes joint localization and detection. Validated on the DCASE 2025 Task 3 benchmark, our framework achieves a relative reduction of up to 8.19% in overall SELD error compared to standard regression baselines, significantly improving far-field stability. Code is available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/itsjunwei/WINTER-SELD</uri>
