title: A Fault Diagnosis and Fault-Tolerant Control Method for Open-Circuit Fault of Excitation Winding Converter in DSEM Drive System
authors: Yi Lu, Qingyun Chang
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: This paper proposes a fault location and fault-tolerant control method for the open-circuit fault of the excitation winding converter in the doubly salient electromagnetic motor (DSEM) drive system. Firstly, by rearranging the placement of the excitation current sensor and analyzing the change characteristics of the sampled current values before and after the excitation winding converter open-circuit fault, the faulty switch is quickly located. Then, fault-tolerant control strategies for the armature winding and excitation winding converter are designed for open-circuit faults in the excitation winding converter switches at different locations. This ensures that the output torque of the DSEM remains unchanged before and after fault-tolerant control, thus guaranteeing smooth motor operation. Simultaneously, the switching moment of the armature winding converter control strategy is optimized after the occurrence of the excitation winding converter open-circuit fault, in order to prevent the motor from outputting negative torque during the switching process. The proposed method can rapidly locate the faulty switch in the excitation winding converter without the need for additional sensors. It ensures that the motor maintains a consistent output torque before and after fault-tolerant operation, thereby guaranteeing the load-carrying capability of the motor. Finally, experiments conducted on a 12/8 three-phase DSEM verify the effectiveness and practicality of the proposed method.

title: Predicting Bladder Cancer Prognosis by Integrating Multiple Omics Data through an Adversarial Autoencoder-based Cox proportional hazards Network
authors: Yanqiong Ren, Jing Wu, Fei Han
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics
abstract: Bladder cancer prognosis is a critical factor in determining optimal treatment strategies. However, the heterogeneity of multi-omics data and the high dimensionality of gene features pose significant challenges for accurate survival prediction. Traditional single-omics or naive integration methods often struggle to capture complex inter-omics relationships and are vulnerable to noise from redundant genes. To address these issues, a novel deep learning framework——AAE-Cox-M, is proposed to integrate mRNA, miRNA, DNA methylation, and CNV data for survival analysis. This method features a two stage integration strategy based on Cross-omics Pre-training, which first pretrains on one omics type and then incorporates additional omics data to enhance cross-layer feature learning. Additionally, a differential expression-based feature selection module is employed to reduce dimensionality, eliminate irrel evant signals, and highlight biologically meaningful genes. To improve representation robustness, an adversarial autoencoder is employed, combining survival loss with adversarial regularization to better model the underlying data distribution while resisting overfitting. Experiments conducted on the TCGA-BLCA dataset and four independent GEO datasets demonstrate that AAE-Cox M consistently outperforms existing linear and deep models. Ablation studies further verify the contributions of each module.

title: Modeling and Prediction of Additional High Frequency Harmonic losses Created by PWM Supply Utilizing Machine Learning
authors: Vladimír Bílek, Jan Bárta, Hannu Kärkkäinen, Lassi Aarniovuori
abstract: This paper presents an innovative methodology for modeling and predicting high-frequency harmonic losses in the rotating field of an electrical machine powered by a frequency converter. Conventional approaches are often unreliable because these losses depend heavily on switching frequency, modulation index, load conditions, and transient inductance, all of which can vary significantly. To address this challenge, a data-driven surrogate modeling approach based on Gaussian process regression is proposed. The training data were obtained from measurements on three induction machines with rated powers of 15 kW, 37 kW, and 75 kW, all tested under the same relative load conditions. The frequency converter was configured with varying reference switching frequencies, and under each condition, the additional high-frequency harmonic losses induced by the pulse-width modulation supply were quantified through waveform analysis. Model performance was validated using a portion of the measurement data reserved for testing. The results demonstrate that the proposed methodology predicts additional harmonic losses with high accuracy and provides valuable insights for the design of more efficient inverter-fed drive systems

title: Multi-View Hypercomplex Learning for Breast Cancer Screening
authors: Eleonora Lopez, Eleonora Grassucci, Danilo Comminiello
abstract: Radiologists interpret mammography exams by jointly analyzing all four views, as correlations among them are crucial for an accurate diagnosis. Recent methods have employed dedicated fusion blocks to capture such dependencies, but these are often hindered by view dominance, training instability, and computational overhead. To address these challenges, we introduce multi-view hypercomplex learning, a novel learning paradigm for multi-view breast cancer classification based on parameterized hypercomplex neural networks (PHNNs). Thanks to hypercomplex algebra, our models intrinsically capture both intra-and inter-view relations. We propose PHResNets for two-view exams and two complementary four-view architectures: PHYBOnet, optimized for efficiency, and PHYSEnet, optimized for accuracy. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms state-of-the-art multi-view models, while also generalizing across radiographic modalities and tasks such as disease classification from chest X-rays and multimodal brain tumor segmentation. Full code and pretrained models are available at https: //github.com/ispamm/PHBreast.

title: Distributed Location Method for SPG Faults in Distribution Networks Combining Zero-Sequence and Phase-to-Phase Current Fault Components
authors: Jiawei Yuan, Xiaojian Dong, Zaibin Jiao
abstract: Reliable distributed localization of single-phase-to-ground (SPG) faults is becoming increasingly critical as modern active distribution networks demand faster restoration and minimized outage impact. However, SPG fault currents are typically weak, highly distorted by arcs, and easily contaminated by noise, making single-source localization methods inherently unreliable. To overcome these limitations, this paper proposes a distributed localization framework that jointly exploits zero-sequence components (ZSCs) and phase-to-phase current fault components (PPCFCs). The intrinsic behaviors of both source signals are first examined considering transient attenuation, triggering delays, and measurement errors, revealing fundamental limitations that cannot be resolved by improved feature extraction or classification alone. Building upon these findings, a multi-source fusion-based distributed method is developed, where ZSCs- or PPCFCs-based source indicators are selectively activated only when their reliability is ensured, and one compensates for the other under degraded conditions. This cooperative decision mechanism substantially enhances both faulty-feeder identification and fault-section localization. The method requires only local measurements for feeder-level detection and uses simple binary communication between adjacent terminals for section-level location, ensuring scalability and practical deployment. Extensive tests across diverse fault scenarios confirm its strong robustness and high localization accuracy.

title: Rethinking Domain Adaptive Semantic Segmentation With Vision Foundation Models
authors: Muxin Liao, Yingqiong Peng, Yuting Sun, Wenju Huang, Yinglong Wang, Yuhang Zhang
abstract: Unsupervised Domain Adaptive Semantic Segmentation (UDASS) studies the challenge of transferring knowledge from labeled source domains to unlabeled target domains. Existing UDASS methods rely on ImageNet-pretrained Transformer networks, but both the limited scale of ImageNet and the restricted capacity of the pretrained models constrain generalization, ultimately hindering performance. Recently, Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) pre-trained on large-scale datasets have achieved a significant generalization ability on downstream tasks, yet their potential for UDASS remains largely unexplored. Thus, in this work, we rethink and explore the potential of VFMs in UDASS and propose a framework via VFMs for UDASS. Specifically, we first construct a general VFM-based UDASS framework by combining different VFMs with various decoders, and then evaluate its effectiveness on a standard UDASS benchmark under both full-parameter and frozen-parameter fine-tuning paradigms. Then, we propose a generalized parameter-efficient fine-tuning approach containing a Dual Distribution Alignment (DDA) strategy and a Selective Feature Refinement (SFR) strategy. The DDA strategy aligns covariance differences while enforcing semantic consistency, thereby improving source–target distribution alignment. The SFR strategy employs learnable tokens to refine frozen VFM features based on the source–pseudo-target covariance, preserving domain-invariant information while adapting to target-specific cues. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach in multiple UDASS benchmarks. The code is released on <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/seabearlmx/VFM4UDASS</uri>.

title: Parametric Dynamic Mode Decomposition for Nuclear Fusion: Applications to Magnetohydrodynamics
authors: Matteo Lo Verso, Stefano Riva, Carolina Introini, Eric Cervi, Matteo Di Prinzio, Marco Caramello, Francesca Giacobbo, Laura Savoldi, Antonio Cammi
abstract: Magnetohydrodynamics (MHD) investigates the dynamics of electrically conducting fluids interacting with magnetic fields. This theory is widely utilized for magnetic confinement fusion problems since both thermonuclear plasmas and some of the relevant operating fluids flowing in the blanket can be described as conducting fluids subjected to magnetic fields. The intrinsically multiphysical nature of MHD problems leads to complex and computationally expensive models, making them unsuited for multiquery applications such as parameter optimization and sensitivity analysis, as well as for online monitoring and control. Reduced-order modeling (ROM) offers a promising path to reduce computational costs while preserving accuracy; however, its application for MHD problems is still in its preliminary stages. This study continues the investigation started by the authors on ROM techniques for MHD problems by considering a parametric version of the dynamic mode decomposition (DMD), aiming to retrieve a general linear representation of the parametric time-series MHD data. In particular, a Kalman filter (KF)-enhanced version of the technique is tested on a lead–lithium MHD flow subjected to a magnetic field of various intensities. The integration of KF in the reduced model approach aims at improving the accuracy of the reduced model by corrections based on data assimilated from sensors inside the geometry. Results demonstrate the reduced model potential to significantly reduce computational effort while maintaining acceptable accuracy, especially if coupled with the KF, making it suitable for fusion-relevant MHD applications.

title: High-Precision Far-Field Characterization Network for Random Non-Uniform Plane Near-Field Measurements
authors: Ao Peng, Juan Chen, Chunhui Mou, Kaihang Fan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: A novel planar near-field (NF) measurement method for calculating high-resolution far-field (FF) data from random non-uniform NF data is proposed. This approach integrates a graph convolutional network (GCN) and a U-Net model within the plane wave spectrum method (PWSM). The GCN employs physical information propagation to reconstruct dense NF data, while the U-Net model further refines the preliminary FF patterns generated by PWSM. Even when only 40% of the sampling points from traditional PWSM are randomly retained, this method accurately predicts FF patterns. The simulation and measurement results demonstrate that it avoids pattern distortion caused by missing NF data and effectively expands the FF validity region.

title: Bridging the Gap between Implicit and Explicit Representation for Efficient Image Compression
authors: Yufeng Zhang, Yuxuan Li, Wenrui Dai, Chern Hong Lim, Carl James Debono, Thittaporn Ganokratanaa, Junhui Hou, Weiyao Lin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Neural Image Compression (NIC) has achieved superior compression performance by modeling images as implicit feature representations, yet its practical deployment is severely hindered by computational overhead. Recently, GaussianImage was proposed as a computationally efficient explicit image representation paradigm, which renders images from 2D Gaussians. However, it suffers from inferior compression performance relative to mainstream NIC frameworks, mainly due to low compressibility and representation capability of explicit Gaussian parameters. To this end, we propose Pixel-Aligned Generalized 2D Splatting (PA-G2DS) as a computation-efficient and compression-friendly image representation format. Specifically, we deploy a learnable rendering function with implicit coefficients to enhance the reconstructed image quality and improve the compression ratio over explicit Gaussian coefficients. Incorporating the proposed PA-G2DS as a computationally efficient decoder, we further develop a suite of image codecs optimized for either compression ratios or flexible deployment scenarios. Experiments prove that the proposed codecs could achieve 30ms compression latency and millisecond-level decompression latency, reducing the performance and efficiency gap between implicit and explicit image representation. Furthermore, the proposed codec opens potential applications for NIC such as JPEG-like sequential decompression and random-access during decompression.

title: Electrical-domain interference sensing driven by relative modal delay in polymer optical fibers
authors: Ryo Takano, Marcelo A. Soto, Yosuke Mizuno
abstract: This paper reports a previously unobserved interference phenomenon that manifests directly in the electrical spectrum of a photodetected signal when light propagates through polymer optical fiber (POF)-based single-mode–multimode–single-mode (SMS) structures. Unlike some conventional fiber sensors that rely on optical-spectrum interrogation, the proposed approach exploits multimode propagation and modal beating to generate well-defined interference features in the electrical domain. These features appear at sub-cutoff wavelengths of standard single-mode fibers (e.g., 1070 nm) and vanish at 1550 nm, confirming their multimodal origin. When a 57 cm POF segment is used, the interference dips shift reproducibly and reversibly under applied axial strain (0–611 με), enabling high-speed strain sensing using only electrical spectrum analysis. The influence of POF length and source bandwidth is also experimentally investigated, revealing non-monotonic dip-spacing behavior and a systematic frequency downshift with increasing bandwidth, consistent with a relative modal-delay mechanism. The concept is further extended to displacement sensing by introducing a variable air gap between silica fibers, achieving sensitivities up to ~3.7 MHz/μm and potentially enabling nanometer-scale resolution. The simplicity, low cost, and fast response of this electrical-domain interrogation scheme make it a promising alternative to conventional optical-domain fiber sensors for real-time strain and displacement monitoring.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Network for Trajectory Reconstruction: A Hybrid Paradigm Informed by Car-Following Models
authors: Xinkai Ji, Pan Liu, Yu Han
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Vehicle trajectory data serves as a crucial foundation for vehicle-level control in mixed traffic environments incorporating connected and automated vehicles (CAVs). However, due to the limited sensing range of CAVs, perception-blind areas inevitably emerge, resulting in partially unobservable vehicle trajectories. To reconstruct unobservable trajectories between CAVs, this paper proposes a novel physics-informed neural network for platoon trajectory reconstruction under partially observed conditions, termed PINN-PTR. PINN-PTR integrates a physics-uninformed neural network (PUNN) with physics-based computational graphs derived from car-following models, in which each vehicle is assigned a distinct parameter set to capture heterogeneity in driving behavior. The proposed model leverages the advantages of both physics-based models, which are data-efficient and interpretable, and deep learning-based models, which are generalizable. Moreover, a bidirectional Newell computational graph (BNCG) is introduced to comprehensively capture the bidirectional temporal and spatial correlations of the ego vehicle with both its leading and following vehicles, based on the propagation of kinematic waves. Two types of PINN-PTR models are studied: one designed solely for trajectory reconstruction and another that jointly reconstructs trajectories and calibrates parameters within physics-based computational graphs. Experimental results on both real-world and synthetic datasets demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms baseline methods in terms of trajectory reconstruction accuracy and car-following model calibration. Ablation studies further confirm the contributions of the BNCG and heterogeneous modeling to improving reconstruction performance. Finally, vehicle emission estimation is applied as a practical case study to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed model in real-world scenarios.

title: Contextual Semantic Relevance Predicting Human Visual Attention
authors: Kun Sun, Rong Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive and Developmental Systems
abstract: Semantic relevance metrics capture not only the inherent meaning of individual objects but also their relationships to other elements in a visual scene. Prior research has shown their importance in predicting human visual processing. However, many studies have overlooked contextual information or failed to employ advances in deep learning for more accurate computation. This study introduces the metrics of contextual semantic relevance to investigate human visual perception and processing. We compute semantic relationships between target objects and their surroundings from both vision-based and language-based perspectives. Using a large eye-movement dataset on visual comprehension, we apply state-of-the-art deep learning techniques to derive these metrics and examine their effects on fixation patterns with advanced statistical models. The metrics we proposed also simulate both top-down and bottom-up processes in visual perception. Furthermore, we integrate vision-based and language-based measures into a novel combined metric, addressing a critical limitation of previous research that treated visual and semantic similarities separately. Results show that all metrics accurately predict fixation behavior, though with distinct predictive roles. The combined metric outperforms the individual ones, supporting theories that highlight the interaction between semantic and visual information in influencing human visual processing. These findings advance our understanding of the cognitive mechanisms underlying visual processing and inform the development of more accurate computational models in cognitive science and human—computer interaction.

title: Robust Beamforming for Pinching-Antenna Systems-based Multi-User Communications
authors: Mingjun Sun, Xidong Mu, Chongjun Ouyang, Guanyu Shang, Yuanwei Liu
abstract: Pinching-antenna systems (PASS)-enabled communications are a promising paradigm. However, obtaining accurate channel state information (CSI) is challenging due to the limited pilots, model mismatch, and other hardware impairments. In this paper, we study the robust beamforming design for PASS-based multi-user communication under the CSI uncertainty. In particular, we model the CSI error using a complex-ball uncertainty set and study the worst-case beamforming design to minimize the transmit power for satisfying users’ minimum achievable rate requirements. To address this problem, the S-procedure is first applied to convert the resulting semi-infinite formulation into a tractable finite set of constraints. Then, an alternating optimization framework is developed to design the baseband beamforming and pinching beamforming. The base-band beamforming subproblem is handled via the semidefinite programming, while a penalty dual decomposition-based method is developed to address the non-convex pinching beamforming subproblem. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm is verified through simulations.

title: Coordination Control Based on Machine Learning Modified Pressure Observer for Skid-Steering Vehicles Coupled With Hydrostatic Propulsion Systems
authors: Qihui Liu, Taiping Yang, Xiaohan Ma, Wei Wu, Chunhui Wei
abstract: Skid-steering vehicles (SSVs) have a wide range of applications, and coordinated control (CC) of the propulsion system is a key to improving steering capability. SSVs equipped with hydrostatic propulsion systems (HPSs) drive and steer by building up pressure, so pressure determines their steering capability. However, the strong coupling between HPS and vehicle dynamics poses a challenge for CC. To address this issue, this article proposes a CC strategy based on a machine learning (ML)-enhanced pressure observer. First, an ML approach is employed to refine the HPS’s efficiency model, thereby improving the accuracy of pressure estimation. A CC strategy is then developed to adjust the engine operating point, ensuring that the pressure demands of both the driving and steering systems are simultaneously satisfied. Finally, the effectiveness is verified by vehicle tests. Results show that the strategy enhances steering capability and reduces fuel consumption by approximately 10.98%, with a moderate increase in temperature of 1.25 °C (from 88.5 °C to 89.75 °C). This work provides both theoretical and practical support for the design and control of future SSVs.

title: CoMAEA: A Collision-Avoiding Multi-Agent Evolutionary Algorithm for Coverage Path Planning_supp5-3665409.mp4
authors: Ting Huang
abstract: Efficient and safe path planning for multiple autonomous agents, such as unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), is essential in many real-world applications. The objective is to coordinate these agents to cover a target area with minimal cost. However, most existing multi-agent coverage path planning (MACPP) methods rely on purely spatial planning and neglect spatio-temporal interactions among agents, which often result in unsafe paths and a high risk of inter-agent collisions during execution. To address this challenge, we propose a new problem formulation, termed collision-aware MACPP (CaMACPP), which incorporates both spatial coverage efficiency and collision avoidance. To solve this problem, we introduce an efficient multi-agent evolutionary algorithm to minimize costs while avoiding collisions. The algorithm first introduces a trapezoidal decomposition strategy to partition complex environments into convex subareas. Then, a cooperative evolutionary algorithm is designed to optimize the inter-area visiting orders for multiple agents and the intra-area coverage modes assigned to each agent. Next, a spatio-temporal collision avoidance mechanism is incorporated to detect and resolve conflicts between agent paths. The proposed algorithm is extensively evaluated against the state-of-the-art MACPP methods to demonstrate its effectiveness in generating collision-free paths with minimal total cost. The results show that it achieves zero collisions in all tested scenarios, reduces the average path length by 15.9%, and decreases coverage overlap by 58.1%.

title: A unified method for determining parameters of augmented codes (I): p -ary linear codes
authors: Yansheng Wu, Yao Zhou, Weibei Fan
abstract: In coding theory, augmented codes are a useful tool for constructing new linear codes from old ones, and in general it is hard to determine the parameters of those augmented codes. In this paper, we present a unified method to settle the above problem by considering the homogenization of multi-variable functions. Firstly, we extend the results by Hyun et al. (Finite Fields Appl., 103: 102589, 2025) from the binary case to any prime cases. Secondly, we compute the weight distributions of augmented codes of the linear codes constructed from two kinds of simplicial complexes by Hyun et al. (Discret. Appl. Math., 254: 135-145, 2019) and Hu et al. (IEEE Trans. Inf. Theory, 70(3): 1823-1835, 2024). Many classes of optimal and almost optimal linear codes, and some codes with small Griesmer defect values are also obtained. This confirms the strength of the resulting constructions.

title: Rig-Robust BEV Perception via 3D Gaussian Splatting and Cyclic Self-Supervision
authors: Muhammad Adeel Hafeez, Ganesh Sistu, Venkatesh Moorthi, Michael G. Madden, Ihsan Ullah
abstract: Camera rigs for Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) are subject to design choices of each vehicle variant; therefore, ADAS algorithms should be rig-invariant or tunable with limited additional data. Recently, the ADAS community adopted BEV (Bird’s Eye View) perception and planning systems due to their scalability to heterogeneous sensors, computational efficiency, and ego-centric perception, but BEV methods remain highly rig-dependent. To verify this, we study a camera-only BEV model, GaussianLSS, which lifts images into a 3D Gaussian scene and predicts BEV maps. We observe a sharp performance drop under a Sedan-to-SUV rig change, with a similar degradation in the reverse direction. Overlap between predicted and ground-truth BEV maps, measured by intersection-over-union (IoU), falls from 38.42% to 18.30% when trained on Sedan and tested on SUV. To address this, we propose a training-time extension to GaussianLSS that improves cross-rig robustness without using any target-rig images or BEV labels. Starting from sedan images, GaussianLSS lifts features into a 3D Gaussian scene, which we render into rig-aware perspective features for both Sedan and SUV. A lightweight decoder is trained only on sedan perspective features to reconstruct sedan RGB (re-projecting features back to the input image) and is then frozen. During cyclic fine-tuning, SUV perspective features rendered from the same Gaussians are passed through the frozen decoder to produce pseudo-SUV views that provide self-supervision for the BEV model in world/BEV space. This increases SUV performance to 23.46% IoU while keeping Sedan performance near its upper bound, and to 25.15% when the decoder additionally sees 20% SUV RGB (the BEV model still uses no SUV labels). The decoder is not used at inference, keeping deployment cost unchanged while suggests a path toward reducing the need for per-vehicle data collection and annotation.

title: MTUNet: Multi-scale Transformer UNet for Single Image Deraining
authors: Sambasiva Rao Gumma, Chintakindi Balaram Murthy
abstract: Single image deraining(SID), aiming to restore a clear image from rain degraded one, is of great importance for numerous practical systems. However, previous deep learning-driven models usually achieve excellent deraining performance, they heavily rely on a powerful computing environment that makes the algorithm unsuitable to be used in real-world applications. To tackle these problems, we propose a new architecture called an efficient MTUNet (Multi-scale Transformer UNet) to improve the SID task. It concentrates to recover important regions in the image and improves the recovery process. The key component in our method is Transformer Module (TM), which aims at restoring the significant features such as the edge details and the seriously degraded areas. Moreover, we embed Feature Maps (FMs) in the network to enhance efficiency and accuracy especially for dealing with high-resolution data. Built based on an efficient Transformer UNet, our model makes use of these improvements to achieve better performance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MTUNet outperforms state-of-the-art methods, with average performance gain of +0.59 dB in PSNR. Besides, with only 18.746M parameters, MTUNet can process the degraded image within 249ms, which makes it a fast and cost-effective model.

title: Mass Estimation of Hovering Rotor-wing UAV Based on Blade Aerodynamic Characteristics Analysis and Radar Micro-Doppler Feature Extraction
authors: Jiahao Wang, Hang Yuan, Ying Luo, Jia Liang, Yijun Chen, Jia Yao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: RWUAV generates thrust through the rotation of its blades, and the blade rotation causes the micro-Doppler (m-D) effect in the radar echo signal. By utilizing the m-D effect, features such as the blade rotation frequency and blade length of RWUAV can be obtained, providing an essential basis for target identification. However, as RWUAV undertakes diverse missions, the mass of load carried by the same model of RWUAV varies when performing different missions. If the mass information of the target’s load can be further obtained, it will provide an important basis for identifying the mission intent of RWUAV, assessing threat levels, and making interception decisions. We propose a method to estimate the mass of hovering RWUAV by analyzing the relationship between the blade aerodynamic characteristics and the m-D features. Firstly, the calculation formula of the blade thrust coefficient is given based on the blade element and momentum theory (BEMT). Secondly, under the hovering condition of the RWUAV, the relationship between the total thrust of the RWUAV and its m-D features is derived. The m-D feature parameters in the radar echo signal from RWUAV were extracted through autocorrelation and peak detection, and the mass of the RWUAV can be estimated. Outdoor experiments have verified the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: A high depth-resolution, reconfigurable Raman spectroscopy based on programmable cone-shell excitation
authors: Yuetian Ren, Yue Yang, Shuaihang Luo, Zheng Zhou, Keren Chen, Zejun Yan, Wei Qian, Shuo Chen
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Quantum Electronics
abstract: An enhanced depth-resolution Raman spectroscopy technique with rapid acquisition is proposed, leveraging spatial light modulator (SLM) based programmable cone-shell excitation and compressive sensing. To tackle the issues of inadequate depth resolution, substantial signal interference, and inefficient acquisition in conventional point-by-point scanning Raman detection (i.e., detection of one depth at a time), this study employs an SLM and an axicon lens to form a programmable cone-shell excitation with high and flexible depth selectivity, and uses the compressive sensing algorithm to reconstruct the sparsely collected Raman data, thus significantly improving depth resolution and detection time efficiency. The two-layer agar phantom experiment demonstrates that the proposed method can achieve better depth sensitivity and resolution, a peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) above or close to 20 dB, and a detection time efficiency of approximately 500 times better than that of the conventional method, presenting an efficient and non-invasive Raman detection approach for rapidly and precisely examining different depths in layered tissues like skin and significantly enhancing the potential for clinical applications.

title: 3PS-RAN: A Real-time Framework for Securing the O-RAN RACH Against DDoS Attacks Towards NextG
authors: Nurullah Aksu, Irsat Saridas, Utku Gülen, Ramin Fuladi, Ömer Faruk Tuna, Semiha Tedik Basaran
abstract: Even with the introduction of additional security features, the progression toward 6G and Open Radio Access Network (O-RAN) architectures expands security risks, increasing both the scale and complexity of mobile networks. Consequently, robust defenses against threats are crucial to ensure the secure and reliable operation of these evolving systems. However, Denial-of-Service (DoS) attacks continue to exploit weaknesses in the RAN signaling protocols. Specifically, the Random Access Channel (RACH) procedure remains vulnerable to flooding attacks that target resource management and the finite capacity of the CU-UE-ID list, effectively blocking legitimate User Equipment (UE) access. In this paper, the possible vulnerabilities during the RACH procedure within an O-RAN environment are investigated. Based on discovered vulnerabilities, various RACH flooding attack scenarios and intelligent attacks are designed to perform DoS/Distributed DoS (DDoS) attacks on the system. To counteract such attacks, a novel Three-Phase Security (3PS) mechanism is designed to enhance protection during the RACH procedure in the O-RAN architecture. In this regard, both statistical and machine learning-based novel detection algorithms have been developed. Then, the detected attacks are mitigated by enhanced RACH protocol steps. The adverse effects of the aforementioned attacks and the security improvements introduced by the 3PS mechanism are evaluated in a real-time setup using the OpenAirInterface (OAI) 5G platform. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework improves the average connection time for legitimate UEs by a factor of 9 to 25 under heavy attack conditions. Additionally, system profiling reveals that the proposed mechanism introduces a negligible operational CPU overhead of less than 0.2%, while maintaining a remarkably stable memory footprint. Furthermore, this implementation confirms the practical relevance of the designed attack scenarios and the solution’s compliance with 3GPP standards within an O-RAN context.

title: The Minority Report Fallacy: Celebrating Gestures While Forgetting Futures
authors: Marc Hassenzahl, Judith Dörrenbächer, Ronda Ringfort-Felner
abstract: None

title: A Hybrid Covert Attention&#x2013;Augmented Motor Imagery Paradigm for Brain&#x2013;Computer Interfaces_supp1-3683767.pdf
authors: Ying Chen

title: Agentic AI for 6G: A New Paradigm for Autonomous RAN Security Compliance_supp1-3681685.pdf
authors: Mahdi Boloursaz Mashhadi
abstract: Agentic AI systems are emerging as powerful tools for automating complex, multi-step tasks across various industries. One such industry is telecommunications, where the growing complexity of next-generation radio access networks (RANs) opens up numerous opportunities for applying these systems. Securing the RAN is a key area, particularly through automating the security compliance process, as traditional methods often struggle to keep pace with evolving specifications and real-time changes. In this article, we propose a framework that leverages LLM-based AI agents integrated with a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline to enable intelligent and autonomous enforcement of security compliance. An initial case study demonstrates how an agent can assess configuration files for compliance with O-RAN Alliance and 3GPP standards, generate explainable justifications, and propose automated remediation if needed. We also highlight key challenges such as model hallucinations and vendor inconsistencies, along with considerations like agent security, transparency, and system trust. Finally, we outline future directions, emphasizing the need for telecom-specific LLMs and standardized evaluation frameworks.

title: Structured Polynomial Codes for Distributed Matrix Multiplication
authors: Derya Malak
publish: IEEE Journal on Selected Areas in Information Theory
abstract: We consider the practical source-worker-receiver framework for distributed matrix multiplication over finite fields. In a setting with two distributed sources with separate encoders, with the first source having access to A and the second to B, with I distributed workers, where each has a bounded storage (memory) capability reflecting a bounded computational capability, and thus can multiply matrices of smaller size, and one receiver, which aims to reconstruct A⊺B from the transmissions of workers. By combining a structured linear encoding scheme with the polynomial code framework, we introduce a novel class of structured polynomial codes (StPolyDot codes) for distributed matrix multiplication, leveraging the bilinear structure of this problem, and show that our codes can surpass the performance of the existing state of art in terms of both memory and communication costs, while also maintaining certain advantages in terms of security.

title: Multi-scale and Multi-level Feature Fusion for Small Object Detection in Sonar Images
authors: Xu Gao, Cuicui Zhang, Xiaobo Li, Bin Xue
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Target detection in sonar images holds significant implications for ecological conservation, marine engineering, and military applications, etc. However, existing object detection models which are mostly developed based on fixed receptive fields are challenged by detecting multi-scale objects, especially detecting small-scale objects from low-quality sonar images. To address this issue, we propose a novel multi-scale and multi-level feature fusion framework built upon You Only Look Once (YOLO), namely YOLO11s-MSML for object detection in sonar images. This architecture performs feature fusion from varying receptive fields within the same layer while enabling efficient cross-layer fusion of shallow and deep features, thereby strengthening detection capabilities across scales, particularly for small objects. Multi-scale spatial features are extracted using different-sized convolutional kernels via a Receptive Field Block (RFB) module within the same layer; multi-level features from cross-layers are integrated through a novel vertical module combining Content-Guided Attention (CGA) and SimAM attention with slicing operation (SWS). In addition, for model lightweight design, Distribution Shifting Convolution (DSC) is introduced to improve the model efficiency. Experimental results on the public Sonar Common Target Detection dataset (SCTD) demonstrate our model's superiority over existing algorithms, achieving 96.6%mAP0.5 for all objects. Compared with the state-of-the-art method, our algorithm improved detection accuracy of small objects by 3.3%.

title: Adaptive Tracking Control for Quantized Linear Stochastic Regression Systems: Asymptotic Property under Non-Periodic Signals
authors: Chuiliu Kong, Ying Wang
abstract: This paper investigates the adaptive tracking control problem for linear stochastic regression systems with quantized observations, focusing on the analysis of asymptotic properties under non-periodic reference signals. An adaptive tracking control scheme is proposed that integrates a stochastic approximation-type estimation algorithm with an adaptive controller. Compared to the periodic case, the adaptive control input designed in this paper exhibits stronger coupling with the parameter estimates due to its additional dependence on past control inputs. The strengthened coupling renders the existing characteristic analysis methods developed for the periodic case no longer applicable. To address this issue, the analysis in this paper leverages an analytic function method and a backward shifted polynomial technique with time-varying coefficients to handle the inherent complexity of the system. Under appropriate conditions on the quantized weights, the parameter estimates generated by the proposed estimation algorithm are shown to converge to their actual values in the mean square sense, with a convergence rate of O (1/k). Furthermore, the proposed adaptive control law is proven to achieve asymptotically optimal tracking. Simulation results are provided to validate the effectiveness of the proposed adaptive tracking control scheme.

title: High-precision positioning via differential four-quadrant detection
authors: Yanling Ren, Xiansong Ren, Bo Wang, Shifeng Zhang, Ming Zhang, Jiacheng Lai, Xianzheng Lang, Xia Guo
abstract: High-precision light spot positioning is essential for a broad spectrum of applications, including optical communication and precision metrology. While recent advancements have primarily focused on algorithmic optimizations to enhance precision, such approaches frequently necessitate intensive raw data processing, resulting in significant latency and high computational overhead. To circumvent these limitations, we propose a differential detection method utilizing a conventional four-quadrant detector. By facilitating the inherent suppression of common-mode noise through differential signal processing, this approach achieves an approximately 20-fold enhancement in the signal-to-noise ratio. Consequently, the positioning precision is improved by 77% compared to conventional detection methods. The seamless compatibility of this method with existing four-quadrant detector architectures underscores its feasibility for practical deployment. Our findings provide a robust framework for advancing laser spot positioning systems, particularly in scenarios requiring ultra-low latency and energy-efficient operation.

title: FedAKD: A Lightweight Federated Adaptive Knowledge Distillation Method for Fault Diagnosis
authors: Yan Han, Yan Zhang, Qingqing Huang, Yan Zhang, Jie Wang
abstract: Federated Learning (FL) has become a promising solution for collaborative model training in industrial scenarios, especially within the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT), where data privacy is crucial. However, the high computational and communication overhead of traditional approaches hampers the deployment of FL frameworks on resource-limited edge devices. This paper introduces a lightweight framework for mechanical fault diagnosis called Federated Adaptive Knowledge Distillation (FedAKD). First, knowledge distillation is performed locally, incorporating a temperature adjustment mechanism that combines the student’s classification loss with the teacher’s confidence to smooth soft labels dynamically. This improves inter-class separability initially and enhances intra-class compactness later. Second, a gradient-based dynamic loss weighting strategy is applied to reduce gradient conflicts and prevent student models from inheriting misleading knowledge from the teacher. Finally, experiments on two fault diagnosis datasets show that FedAKD achieves diagnostic accuracies of 96.16% and 93.03%, while reducing model size and computation by 15× and 56×, respectively. Moreover, the communication time per round during federated training is reduced by approximately 50%, confirming the framework’s efficiency under limited resource settings.

title: Fiber Bragg Grating Sensing for Condition Monitoring of Power Electronic Devices: A Review
authors: Kaining Kuang, Mike Barnes, Siniša Djurović
abstract: Junction-temperature cycling and the associated thermo-mechanical stresses strongly affect the reliability of power electronic (PE) devices, but conventional sensors are challenged in providing accurate measurement of related localized stresses due to limited electrical insulation, size limitations and poor electromagnetic interference (EMI) immunity. Fiber Bragg grating (FBG) sensors have emerged as a promising solution because of their miniature size, excellent insulation, high bandwidth, and multiplexing capability. This review summarizes recent progress in applying FBG sensing to the condition monitoring of PE devices, detailing bonding strategies, sensor placement, and strain–temperature decoupling across different monitoring schemes, while also examining the effect of airgaps, residual stress, and grating length on the measurement accuracy and dynamic response. Building on the reviewed state of the art, the FBG-based approaches for device ageing assessment and the remaining challenges in this area are discussed.

title: VSAL: A Vision Solver with Adaptive Layouts for Graph Property Detection
authors: Jiahao Xie, Guangmo Tong
publish: Proceedings of the ACM Web Conference 2026
abstract: None

title: Development and Testing of an Intelligent VR Simulator with an Adaptive AI-Based System for Training Specialists in Information-Measuring and Control Systems
authors: Damir Akchurin, Alik Khafizov, Konstantin Kryshko
abstract: The article presents the results of the development and experimental testing of an intelligent VR simulator designed to improve the effectiveness of practical training for specialists in the field of information-measuring and control systems (IMCS). The relevance of the research is driven by the challenges of modern engineering education: high equipment costs, the inability to simulate non-standard situations, limited access to complex technical systems, and the lack of a personalized learning approach. The proposed solution integrates virtual and augmented reality technologies with artificial intelligence (AI) elements, including computer vision based on YOLOv8 for recognizing industrial automation objects and the Mistral 7B language model operating on the Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) principle for contextual learner support. The scientific novelty lies in the creation of an adaptive educational system capable of analyzing user actions in real time, identifying typical errors, and forming individual learning trajectories. During experimental testing involving USPTU students, the practical effectiveness of the simulator was proven: the time to complete laboratory work decreased by 15.79%, and the number of errors during the assembly of measuring equipment decreased by 31.81% compared to traditional methods. Methodological recommendations for scaling the technology into the curricula of technical universities have been developed. The research results are supported by three certificates of state registration of computer programs.

title: Algorithm and Application of Digital Processing for Medical Images Using the Bilateral Digital Filtering Method
authors: Dj. X. Djumanov, M. R. Gofurjonov, U. A. Azimova
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive study of digital medical image processing with a specific focus on gallbladder diagnostics. The research addresses three interrelated objectives: the development and refinement of digital processing techniques for gallbladder images, the application and evaluation of advanced filtering methods, and the construction of a mathematical model describing the movement of gallstones within biliary fluid. Particular attention is given to edge-preserving filters, especially the bilateral filter, which demonstrates high efficiency in suppressing noise and artifacts while maintaining essential structural information such as organ contours, tissue boundaries, and fine texture details. Theoretical foundations of bilateral filtering are analyzed in detail, and its practical application in biomedical image analysis is discussed. The results confirm that the integration of advanced digital filtering with mathematical modeling significantly improves both the visual quality and quantitative reliability of medical images, contributing to more accurate diagnostic assessment of gallbladder pathology.

title: Influence of Load Distribution on the Obstacle-Climbing Ability of an Adaptable Wheel-Leg Robot
authors: Islam Magomedov, Usam Takhaev, Movsar Matygov
abstract: Modern mobile vehicles are well adapted for locomotion on even terrains. Lately there have been many researches aimed at overcoming complex and dynamic environments with the use of transformable or adaptable wheels. These wheels are capable of traveling on even terrains in circular form as classical wheels and when necessary they can rearrange themselves to fit the given environment with obstacles that can come in various shapes such as curbs, doorsteps, stairs and others. Transformable or adaptable wheels are capable of handling various obstacles, but understanding how weight distribution can impact the vehicle’s travel is also an essential part of robotics. Hence, this work looks into examining the ability of the mobile vehicle with an opened legged wheel to climb a stair with various loads applications in different designated areas of the structure. Three areas were selected on the structure which were middle, rear and front. Also, different values of force were applied to each selected area. The results illustrated that the application of force to the middle gives moderate results. The rear part application of force provided the best distance traveling results, with the values around 5 and 10 N added to the rear part of the vehicle illustrated best performing capabilities. The worst results were illustrated when the forces were applied to the front part.

title: Robotic Complex for Performing Urological Surgeries
authors: Sergey F. Jatsun, Dmitry I. Efremov, Gavriil A. Fursov
abstract: This paper analyzes urological procedures performed using retrograde intrarenal surgery. The results of the development of a ureteroscope stand for retrograde intrarenal surgery, including its main components and characteristics, are presented. Remote control and automatic control strategies for this stand are discussed. A stand control algorithm is proposed. It involves defining the workspace using a camera with a machine vision system, then distributing it onto a graph using Delaunay triangulation, and planning the trajectory using Dijkstra's algorithm. The possibility of remotely controlling the stand using console handles is also explored. The result of constructing a trajectory on a kidney model is presented.

title: Development of a Methodology for Photomask Defect Identification Using Computer Vision
authors: Nikolay S. Mironov, Yuri Yu. Yudakhin
abstract: Improving photolithography accuracy and transitioning to finer fabrication processes in microelectronics have created a critical need for reliable and rapid quality control of photomasks used for defining circuit topology. Traditional inspection methods are often insufficiently effective for detecting minute defects. This paper proposes an automated photomask verification methodology based on computer vision. The core component is a two-stage algorithm: segmentation of structural features on an image using a convolutional neural network, followed by a comparison of the result with a reference layout to detect and classify defects. The proposed approach can significantly enhance the accuracy and speed of the inspection process, making it adaptable to the requirements of modern and next-generation manufacturing.

title: Development of a Digital Twin Prototype for a Crushing and Screening Plant
authors: Victor Okolnishnikov, Sergey Rudometov, Sergey Zhuravlev
abstract: This article proposes a five-dimensional model of a digital twin for developing a digital twin prototype for a specific crushing and screening plant. The equipment composition and flow chart of the crushing and screening process for this plant are discussed. The visual-interactive simulation environment MTSS is used as a digital twin environment. MTSS contains a library of models of crushing and screening equipment types. The main part of the digital twin is a simulation model of the crushing and screening process, which is based on a mathematical model. The results of mathematical and simulation modeling are presented. The possibility of converting the digital twin prototype into a digital twin instance is being considered.

title: Optimization and Control of Induction Heating Based on Cyber Physical Systems
authors: Alexandr N. Ivanov, Vladimir A. Bukanin, Alexei E. Zenkov
abstract: New approaches to optimization and control of induction heating processes are described. The use of the Total Flux method allows solving coupled electro-thermal problems rather fast compared with common CAE software. Using this approach makes it possible to integrate simulation software and real induction heating equipment into a single system. To accomplish this task, a cyber-physical system (CPS) was used that consists of one or more industrial computers embedded into power supplies with special applications for ELTA 8 software and several sensors. The possible hardware configuration of the CPS system is described. Examples and results of using this approach in real induction heating technologies are presented. Calculated and measured parameters of technological processes are required for analysis and confirming the product's quality. The described approach may be used in quality assurance procedures of induction heat treatment processes.

title: Effectiveness and Hyperparameter Selection of Ensemble Models of Artificial Intelligence in Diagnosing Diabetes
authors: Shokhrukh Sariyev, Gulzoda Negmatova, Muhammad B. Niyozov
abstract: This study examined models for evaluating and optimizing the performance of machine learning and artificial intelligence algorithms in medical diagnostics, in particular, for solving the problem of multi-class classification of diabetes mellitus. In the study, for a diabetes dataset prepared from real clinical data, the target column "Diabetes" was divided into three classes: "healthy," "prediabetes," and "diabetes," and the dataset was divided into training sets and test sets. In this study, the performance of ten modern machine learning models, GBoost, KNN, Stacking MLP, MLP, Stacking RF, ExtraTrees, Stacking Bagging, XGBoost, LightGBM, and HistGradientBoosting, was tested using various evaluation metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. Preliminary results showed high accuracy and generalization ability in the XGBoost models 0.9048 accuracy, ExtraTrees 0.9026 accuracy, LightGBM 0.9055 accuracy, and HistGradientBoosting 0.9066 accuracy, based on ensemble and gradient boosting. The results of these models after hyperparameter tuning in the next step were: HistGradientBoosting 0.9092 accuracy, LightGBM 0.9095 accuracy, ExtraTrees 0.9066 accuracy, and XGBoost 0.9063 accuracy. Precision, recall, and F1-score also improved accordingly. In this study, the optimal hyperparameters for the selected models were analyzed in tabular form. The results of the study show that the performance of ensemble-based and boosting-based models in multi-class classification was examined in the clinical dataset. The results of this study provide a scientific and practical basis for the use of artificial intelligence technologies in diabetes diagnosis and create a solid foundation for the development of modern approaches to the medical system of Uzbekistan.

title: LivingBench: An IoT/Edge Platform Benchmark Based on an Environmental Observation Use-Case
authors: Haraesh JR, Ammar Kazem, Matthieu Nicolas, François Lemercier, Guillaume Pierre, Christoff Andermann
publish: 2026 29th Conference on Innovation in Clouds, Internet and Networks (ICIN)
abstract: In recent years, a number of edge computing platforms have been proposed to process data produced by IoT sensors. Performing computation close to the sources of data allows faster insight and greater reliability at a lower cost compared to traditional cloud-based deployments. However, designers of IoT/edge platforms face difficult issues. In particular, exercising and testing a new platform in conditions that approach a real deployment requires a sufficient number of standard benchmarking systems capable of generating realistic workloads. In this paper, we propose LivingBench, a benchmarking tool with the capability of incorporating real or synthetic workload injection, developed to exercise edge computing systems. LivingBench integrates a real-world data trace captured in an environmental observatory, together with a collection of actual applications designed for processing these data, and a load injector tool capable of replaying a (possibly pre-processed) trace to benchmark an MQTT-based edge system. We describe the architecture of LivingBench and show how it may be used to evaluate the maximum data processing capacity of an edge system under test.
