title: SPREAD: Scalable Pre-Trained World Model for Adaptive Dynamics Model
authors: Jihun Moon, Seong-Woo Kim
abstract: Autonomous robots must be capable of adapting not only to training datasets but also to unfamiliar environments. World models, which learn predictive dynamics of the environment, have been proposed to overcome the task-specific limitations of conventional RL. However, their capability is typically restricted to training distributions. In this paper, we introduce SPREAD, the first framework that enables a pre-trained world model to learn environmental changes online, and continuously. SPREAD freezes the pre-trained model and updates only a set of LoRA adapters. This design jointly achieves three goals: (i) real-time adaptation, (ii) robustness to forgetting without a replay buffer, and (iii) forward transfer. Experiments demonstrate that SPREAD reduces prediction error by 40% within just 100 steps under diverse visual and dynamic changes, and that such adaptation directly translates into improved task success rates. Moreover, SPREAD validates its effectiveness in continual learning settings. By extending pre-trained world models to the domain of online continual learning, this work highlights world models that autonomously adapt to real-world dynamics.

title: Current Ripple Analysis and Electro-Thermal Co-Optimization of Nonlinear Powder Core Filter Inductors in a 215 kW/9 kHz PCS
authors: Zhuozheng Chen, Zichen Guo, Jianan Cao, Yutao Liu, Xintao Zhang, Weizeng Liu, Xikui Ma
abstract: Powder cores are ideal materials for fabricating filter inductors in high-power power conversion systems (PCS). Allowing these cores to operate in their soft saturation regions significantly reduces the volume, cost, and losses of the inductors; however, this introduces strong nonlinear current dependence, thereby invalidating traditional inductor design methods based on linear materials. This paper proposes a comprehensive electro-thermal co-optimization method for inductors that accounts for saturation effects, current ripple, losses, and thermal behavior. First, based on Space Vector Pulse Width Modulation and an accurate reluctance model, a three-phase three-level PCS current ripple calculation method is established that accounts for saturation characteristics, providing theoretical support for filter design. Based on this, a core and winding loss model considering magnetic saturation was constructed. Second, to tackle the heat dissipation issues arising from high power density, an integrated thermal network incorporating conductive, convective, and radiative thermal resistance is constructed to accurately evaluate the performance of the inductor heat dissipation system. Finally, a parametric sweep optimization tool is implemented to support efficient design space exploration and performance evaluation. Comprehensive testing on a 215 kW/9 kHz PCS prototype verifies the reliability of the proposed method.

title: A Machine Learning Framework for DDoS Attack Detection in SDN-Enabled Mobile Wireless Networks
authors: Ishita Sharma, Satyam Agarwal, Shashi Shekhar Jha, Sumit Chakravarty
abstract: Attacks on network components and devices pose a significant threat to service continuity, necessitating robust detection mechanisms. This paper presents a Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack detection framework tailored for heterogeneous mobile wireless networks within a Software-Defined Networking architecture. A two-tier model is proposed: localized attack detection at access points (APs) using a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) classifier, and centralized detection under mobility at the controller using a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model. The system incorporates novel traffic features such as flow count, speed of source IP, source and destination IP address entropy, proportion of bidirectional flows, and handover frequency, which together enhance detection in mobile environments. An LSTM model analyzes inter-AP traffic correlation over time to address mobility-driven DDoS attack amplification. The proposed approach is evaluated under diverse traffic types (TCP, UDP, ICMP) and varying attack intensities. The MLP model selected for integration into the framework demonstrates consistently strong detection capability across the evaluated scenarios, achieving accuracy values in the range of 95%–99% and showing improved performance relative to existing state-of-the-art schemes. Furthermore, multi-run statistical validation confirms stable behavior under randomized initialization and mobility-driven conditions, while controller-level correlation analysis enhances robustness against mobility-driven attack propagation.

title: Optical Sampling Enabled Nonlinear Fourier Transform for Ultrafast Fiber Laser Characterization
authors: Zhichao Wu, Jingwen Li, Chaoyu Xu, Chao Ma, Dan Luo, Jing Zhang, Xiang Li, Tianye Huang, Perry Ping Shum
abstract: Linear optical sampling (LOS) technique revolutionizes the acquisition of full-field information from the ultrafast optical signals, based on small bandwidth optoelectronic devices. In particular, this innovation facilitates the achievement of nonlinear frequency spectra, with the help of nonlinear Fourier transform (NFT). Here, we experimentally demonstrate the LOS-enabled NFT technique for characterizing various solitons generated from a passively mode-locked fiber laser (PMFL). Concurrently, we are able to discriminate various eigenvalue distributions within the nonlinear discrete spectrum, for the ease to identify the switchable PMFL outputs. Our experimental results verify that, since a high equivalent sampling rate of up to 13.76-TSa/s results in remarkable resolutions of 0.07 ps and 4.2 pm in the time and frequency dimensions, the LOS enabled NFT technique allows for the precise characterization of ultrafast fiber laser with various soliton outputs, in comparison with traditional characterization techniques. Furthermore, LOS facilitates the NFT analysis of ultrafast pulses, leading to advantages of multi-dimensional characterization, cost effective, and high efficiency of data storage.

title: Fixed-Time Privacy-Preserving Distributed Coordinated Control for Grid-Forming and Grid-Following Inverters in Microgrids
authors: Jianwen Zhang, Sha Fan, Yu Wang
abstract: As renewable energy integration continues to grow and microgrid (MG) systems become increasingly complex, it becomes critical to ensure system stability, security, and coordinated control among heterogeneous inverter types. This paper investigates the coordination among grid-forming inverters (GFMIs), grid-following inverters (GFLIs) under PQ control, and GFLIs under droop control. To address this problem, a communication layer based on the leader-follower consensus (LFC) architecture is established to enable information exchange among GFMIs, PQ-controlled GFLIs, and droop-controlled GFLIs. Building on this framework, a novel fixed-time privacy-preserving distributed coordinated controller is proposed by introducing a state decomposition mechanism. The proposed controller achieves fixed-time regulation of GFMI frequency and MG main bus voltage, ensures proportional active and reactive power sharing among all inverter types, and maintains the confidentiality of MG operational data. In comparison with existing studies, this work integrates the coordination of different inverter types, ensuring both operational efficiency and privacy protection, which has not been fully addressed in previous literature. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed approach is demonstrated through real-time hardware-in-the-loop experiments.

title: Interaction-Aware Eco-Driving of Connected Hybrid Electric Vehicles Based on Safe Deep Reinforcement Learning: Speed Planning and Lane Changing
authors: Jin Zeng, Jie Han, Arash Khalatbarisoltani, Hanghang Cui, Fengqi Zhang, Cong-Zhi Liu, Xiaosong Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: The development of vehicles-to-everything (V2X) communication and autonomous driving technologies offers novel opportunities for eco-driving of connected hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs). To enhance vehicle energy efficiency in complex traffic scenarios, this study proposes a novel interaction-aware eco-driving strategy utilizing a Safe Soft Actor-Critic (Safe-SAC) deep reinforcement learning (DRL) algorithm for connected HEVs, which jointly optimizes speed planning and lane-changing decisions. By leveraging V2X technology, multi-source traffic information is integrated into the DRL environment, where the state space encompasses the states of the ego vehicle, traffic flow, signal phase and timing (SPaT), and the states of surrounding vehicles, while the action space contains the target lane and desired acceleration of the ego vehicle. In contrast to previous works, the integration of traffic flow speed and lane occupancy for each lane as the state variables enables a more accurate representation of the dynamic characteristics of surrounding traffic. Furthermore, safety constraints and a multi-objective reward function are meticulously designed to balance energy efficiency, driving comfort, and travel efficiency while ensuring safety. To thoroughly evaluate the energy efficiency of the proposed eco-driving strategy, both dynamic programming (DP) and equivalent consumption minimization strategy (ECMS) are employed as the underlying energy management strategies (EMS). Finally, the effectiveness of the Safe-SAC strategy is successfully validated on the co-simulation platform based on the Simulation of Urban Mobility (SUMO) and Python under various traffic scenarios. Compared to the Krauss-LC2013 model, the findings highlight the superiority of the proposed Safe-SAC strategy in achieving an average energy saving of 38.5%. This strategy also enhances driving comfort and maintains higher travel efficiency.

title: A Sensitive and Selective QCM Sensor Functionalized with Chitosan Biopolymer for the Detection of Linalool in Organic Sikkim Teas_supp1-3681201.pdf
authors: Prolay Sharma
abstract: In this work, a facile and environmentally benign chitosan-functionalized Quartz Crystal Microbalance (QCM) sensor is developed for sensitive Linalool detection in organic Sikkim teas. The proposed sensor exhibits a sensitivity of 1.2903 Hz·ppm⁻¹, with excellent linearity (R² = 0.991) over a concentration range of 10–1000 ppm, representing a measurable improvement over several previously reported polymer- and MOF-based QCM systems. A maximum steady-state frequency shift of −1411 Hz at 1000 ppm is observed, along with preferential selectivity toward Linalool attributed to specific analyte–film interactions. Dynamic response measurements indicate rapid and reversible sensing behavior. Repeatability, reproducibility and long-term stability studies demonstrate consistent sensor performance, including stable operation under periodic 300 ppm exposures over a six-month duration. Surface morphology and chemical functionality of the coating are confirmed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, supporting the proposed sensing mechanism. Real-sample analysis further demonstrates practical applicability, with QCM responses showing strong correlation with GC–MS results (R² = 0.9820) for Sikkim tea samples. These results highlight chitosan as an effective green coating material for reliable and sensitive QCM-based Linalool sensing.

title: Block Customized Topology Term Decomposition for High-Dimensional Image Reconstruction_supp1-3685115.pdf
authors: Xi-Le Zhao
abstract: Recently, the block-term decomposition with rank-(Lr,Lr,1) (termed as LL1 decomposition), which decomposes a third-order tensor into the sum of the outer products between vector and matrix factors, has received increasing attention for high-dimensional image reconstruction. However, the fixed low-rank matrix decomposition in LL1 is restricted to third-order tensors, which hinders its development for higher-order tensor data (i.e., order N>3). To address this, we propose a Block Customized Topology Term Decomposition (BCTD), which represents an Nth-order tensor as a sum of outer products of basis vectors and customized (N-1)th-order coefficient tensors with flexible internal topological structures. The proposed BCTD enjoys two advantages: Firstly, it allows tackling higher-order tensors beyond the third-order tensor setting of LL1, which can better preserve the high-dimensional structure of the tensor. Secondly, it allows each term to have a customized topological structure beyond the fixed topological structure (i.e., low-rank matrix decomposition) in LL1, which can better explore the intrinsic high-dimensional low-rank structures of the tensor. To evaluate the performance of the proposed BCTD, we build the corresponding high-dimensional image reconstruction model and provide a theoretical generalization error bound between the recovered tensor of the proposed model and the underlying tensor. To solve the resulting optimization problem, we apply a proximal alternating minimization (PAM)-based algorithm with a theoretical convergence guarantee. Extensive experimental results on high-dimensional image completion and compression tasks using real-world datasets (color videos and light field images) demonstrate the superiority of the proposed model over other baseline models.

title: Ten Years of Chaos Engineering… Now What?
authors: Joshua Owotogbe, Stefano Fossati, Indika Kumara, Dario Di Nucci, Damian A. Tamburri, Willem-Jan van den Heuvel
abstract: Modern software systems are increasingly built on distributed cloud-native architectures that offer scalability and agility but also introduce significant complexity and fragility. Chaos engineering aims to help make complex systems resilient to various failures that can occur in production environments. This article examines the evolution of chaos engineering over the past decade in academia and industry. Our analysis suggests that chaos engineering has evolved from a niche testing strategy to a foundational discipline for building dependable systems at scale. The next generation of this testing practice should focus on LLM-assisted test automation, hybrid testing frameworks, and integration of chaos engineering into compliance and governance workflows.

title: Distributed Finite-time Observer for Rapid Detection of Multiple Line Outages in Power Systems Under DoS Attacks
authors: Yu Chen, Zhi-Wei Liu, Wenqu Li, Yan-Wu Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Power systems are vulnerable not only to physical faults but also to cyberattacks, which can prevent the communication network from receiving measurement information from bus sensors during such incidents. Therefore, the rapid detection of power line outages (LOs) is crucial for maintaining the stability of the power system in the presence of denial-of-service (DoS) attacks. This paper introduces a real-time multiple LOs (Multi-LOs) detection algorithm based on a novel distributed finite-time observer. Unlike existing observers designed for Multi-LOs detection that depend on secure communication services, the proposed observer can detect Multi-LOs within a finite time, even in the presence of DoS attacks and voltage uncertainties. A comprehensive mathematical analysis establishes the upper bound for the settling time of estimation errors and demonstrates that the proposed distributed observer converges within a finite time during DoS attacks, thereby enabling timely detection of Multi-LOs. Simulation results further validate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed Multi-LOs detection algorithm.

title: Input-Time Coordination Based Distributed Control for Multimachine Power Systems With Nondimensional Analysis
authors: Chutian Sun, Zeyi Liu, Hongjing Liang, Yuhua Cheng
abstract: Traditional rigid-time control in multimachine power systems faces severe actuator saturation risks due to the conflict between strict stability demands and finite generator capacities. To address this challenge, this paper presents a novel distributed control strategy for auxiliary voltage regulation, establishing a control chain comprising a modeling foundation, a core coordination mechanism, and an implementation guarantee. For the modeling foundation, a nondimensional analysis framework is developed to provide a unified structural basis across heterogeneous generators. This framework reveals intrinsic multimachine physical scaling laws, facilitating the active regulation of effective constraint boundaries through base quantity adjustment. Based on this structural foundation, an input-time coordination mechanism functions as the core autonomous saturation mitigation mechanism, which dynamically extends the convergence deadline in response to surging energy demands to relieve overloads. Finally, regarding the implementation guarantee, a distributed adaptive controller is synthesized, where a fuzzy logic system is employed to approximate system nonlinearities, and a singularity-free prescribed-time function is introduced to ensure initial feasibility and eliminate infinite gain risks. For the studied multimachine scenarios, the performance of the proposed scheme is evaluated based on numerical simulations, demonstrating that this synergistic framework significantly alleviates the practical control burden while preserving transient stability.

title: On-Device Deep Learning for RIS Beamforming: Design & Latency-Rate Trade-offs
authors: Luca Urbinati, Nicolò Decarli, Francesco Guidi, Anna Guerra, Barbara Mavì Masini, Alberto Zanella
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RISs) promise a paradigm shift in wireless communication by enabling smart propagation environments. However, traditional RIS control, which relies on base station (BS) decisions and high-volume feedback loops, struggles to meet the stringent real-time demands of next-generation systems. To overcome these limitations, this work explores direct on-board RIS control through deep learning (DL) algorithms. In particular, we shift DL-based codebook selection from the BS to the microcontroller unit (MCU) integrated into the RIS control board via a Tiny Machine Learning (TinyML) deployment pipeline. Unlike prior studies that assume such deployment feasibility without empirical validation, we conduct a hardware-aware design-space exploration that quantifies the trade-off between controller design and communication performance across a range of DL models and MCU-class embedded devices, and identifies the resulting latency-rate Pareto frontier under realistic constraints. This paper provides design guidelines for jointly selecting RIS size, number of active elements, DL model complexity, and MCU class to meet application-specific rate and real-time requirements.

title: IEEE Electron Deviices Society J.J. Ebers Award announcement
authors: N/A

title: Support Structure Detection of Railway Catenary Systems on UAVs Using Spike-Based Brain-Inspired Neural Network
authors: Ning Ma, Haonan Yang, Zhigang Liu, Haorui Cui, Hui Wang, Linjun Shi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: The Catenary Support Component (CSC) is a critical element in ensuring the safe operation of electrified railway systems. In recent years, the use of UAVs for railway maintenance and inspection has steadily increased, making them an increasingly important technological tool. However, UAVs face several challenges during railway inspections. These challenges include energy consumption constraints, which limit their prolonged use, as well as complex backgrounds, structural occlusions, and varying component scales that hinder detection accuracy. To address these challenges, a low-energy consumption, graph-guided spike-based brain-inspired neural network algorithm (GSINet) was proposed. First, a novel backbone called the spatiotemporal multiscale spike fusion network was designed. This network leverages spatiotemporal spike encoding for feature modeling and enables cross-scale feature fusion. Temporal and channel attention mechanisms, along with SpikeASPP technology, were used to enhance the network’s ability to handle diverse scales and complex environmental conditions. Next, a Spike-inspired detection head was designed with a position guidance module (PGM) based on graph convolutional networks. The PGM utilizes prior category relationships, integrating both semantic and geometric information to mitigate challenges such as occlusion and multi-component measurement. Finally, a spiking network architecture was implemented using the improved Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (IB-LIF) Neuron. This reduces energy consumption by exploiting sparse computations and an event-driven mechanism, while ensuring high detection accuracy. Extensive experiments on a CSC dataset captured by UAVs demonstrate that the model achieves superior measurement performance while consuming significantly lower energy.

title: Analysis of Online Partial Discharge Data Trending and Pattern Classification for Life Extension: A SURVEY OF LARGE AC ROTATING MACHINES INSTALLED IN THE PETROLEUM AND CHEMICAL INDUSTRY
authors: Saeed Ul Haq, Greg C. Stone, Keith Lyles, Madu T. S. Moorthy
abstract: IN THE PETROLEUM AND CHEMICAL INDUSTRY, A ROTATING machine has to run reliably in service until a scheduled outage can be planned. In the 1980s and 1990s, some machines operated continuously for three to five years, but recently, production demands have pushed them to run as long as five to eight years, especially for some critical applications. These extended run times lead to an asset availability for maintenance once at the end of a five- to eight-year run cycle. With opportunities stretched out, it is critical to have a well-defined maintenance scope and execution plan. Online partial discharge (PD) testing and monitoring have been widely applied to determine the need for maintenance of the stator winding insulation system and to help establish a more effective maintenance plan. Several high-voltage rotating machines were selected to complete a survey with regard to their online PD trending along with the acquisition of several phase-resolved PD (PRPD) patterns. The obtained PRPD patterns contained characteristic features, which were successfully correlated with the observed trend, acquired during the service life of machines. The upward PD trend and correlation of sources using PRPD patterns helped several end users to determine when maintenance is advisable. During this survey, a few critical units were refurbished based on learning from this survey and then monitored again for an additional five years, with significant financial savings.

title: AdaptiController: VR-Enhanced Fine Motor Assistance Through Finger Pressure Modulation_supp1-3679909.mp4
authors: Hangyu Zhou
abstract: This paper explores finger pressure as a continuous implicit input modality to enhance interaction precision in virtual reality (VR). While motion controllers are widely adopted, their limitations in delicate operations remain a critical challenge. We investigate whether finger pressure signals from conventional VR controllers could offer advantages over traditional kinematic metrics for precision interaction.Through empirical studies, we demonstrate a robust relationship between pressure dynamics and task precision requirements, leading to a lightweight sigmoid-based model that leverages detected pressure to infer desired control granularity. In a comparative evaluation of video-scrubbing tasks, our adaptive method outperforms static sensitivity baselines in both task performance and subjective preference, without elevating cognitive load. Further validation via a VR sketching application demonstrates that our technique maintains task performance while reducing mental demand compared to manual control. Our findings reveal the untapped potential of pressure-based input to bridge coarse and fine-grained VR interactions, offering a path toward more versatile and intuitive input systems.

title: Continual Test-Time Training on Graphs Via Adaptive Prompts Integration
authors: Qianyi Cai, Ziyue Qiao, Rui Cai, Huijie Liu, Junyi Li, Xiao Luo, Hui Xiong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: This paper investigates a novel and critical problem of Graph Continual Test-Time Training, which aims to enable a frozen pre-trained graph model to adapt continuously to evolving out-of-distribution (OOD) graphs without supervision. Existing test-time training methods primarily focus on one-step adaptation and overlook long-term knowledge retention, while conventional continual learning approaches rely on labeled data and static memory replay. Consequently, they are unable to handle sequential OOD domains effectively, often suffering from severe forgetting and limited efficiency in dynamic graph environments. To address these challenges, we propose DPCGL (Dynamic Prompts-based Continual Graph Learning), a data-centric framework that performs continual test-time training through adaptive prompt optimization. DPCGL freezes the pre-trained backbone and maintains a dynamic prompt pool, where prompts are adaptively selected and updated for each incoming graph domain. This design enables parameter-efficient adaptation and mitigates forgetting by organizing transferable knowledge within prompts rather than model weights. Furthermore, DPCGL jointly optimizes three objectives: similarity alignment for representativeness, KL divergence regularization for knowledge preservation, and diversity constraint for generalization, providing both stability and adaptability during continual adaptation. Extensive experiments on multiple evolving OOD graph benchmarks demonstrate that DPCGL achieves state-of-the-art performance, effectively alleviating catastrophic forgetting and enabling robust continual adaptation across domains.

title: Chaotic Circuit Design Leveraging the Analog Nonlinear Dynamics of a Self-Rectifying Memristor
authors: Xianyue Zhao, Alon Ascoli, Ahmet Samil Demirkol, Ioannis Messaris, Jan Dellith, Ilia Polian, Heidemarie Krüger, Ronald Tetzlaff, Nan Du
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Circuits and Systems
abstract: Memristive devices have attracted significant attention due to their intrinsic nonlinearity, memory-dependent behavior, and ability to encode information through continuously tunable resistive states at the nanoscale. Beyond its use in non-volatile memories and neuromorphic architecture, the memristor is increasingly investigated for chaotic and nonlinear circuit design, where its analog hysteretic response can enable compact and energy-efficient chaotic systems. To date, memristor-based chaotic circuit designs have predominantly focused on binary (abrupt-switching) devices, while analog (gradual-switching) memristors, despite their advantages such as low operating current and high reproducibility, have not yet been explored for their potential to generate and sustain chaotic behavior. In this work, we use a self-rectifying BiFeO3 (BFO) memristor as a demonstrator to investigate how analog nonlinear dynamics can be harnessed for chaotic RC oscillator design. Based on experimental characterization of its frequency-dependent response, a physically grounded behavioral model is developed to accurately capture the device’s analog and self-rectifying characteristics, showing excellent agreement with measured data. Using this model, we adapt a low-frequency third-order RC chaotic circuit from the literature by replacing its diode with the BFO memristor and its parasitic capacitance, where the BFO device serves as the single nonlinear element responsible for chaotic signal generation. More importantly, the circuit parameters must be finely tuned to match the analog nonlinear dynamics of the BFO memristor with the RC network and ensure stable chaotic operation, ultimately yielding a fifth-order memristive oscillator. The proposed circuit exhibits rigorous chaos, as confirmed by Lyapunov exponent analysis, features a double-scroll attractor, and demonstrates a broad parameter range of chaotic behavior verified through bifurcation diagram analysis. This work represents the demonstration of chaos generation by leveraging, for the first time, the analog nonlinear dynamics in a self-rectifying memristor, establishing a direct link between device-level nonlinear dynamics and system-level chaotic behavior, and providing a technology-agnostic framework for the development of low-power, scalable chaotic circuits applicable to diverse applications.

title: Methods for Determining DSC Curves Based on Time Series Clustering
authors: Roman M. Bachurin, Irina G. Zakharova, Kanipa T. Ibraeva
abstract: This paper presents the development of an automated approach for the analysis of differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) data from biomass pyrolysis based on time series clustering. The relevance of the study stems from the complexity of visual analysis of extensive experimental datasets comprising multipack curves with varying peak positions. Various similarity measures and clustering algorithms were compared using a dataset of 85 DSC curves. The dynamic time warping (DTW) metric, which accounts for nonlinear distortions along the temperature axis, demonstrated the most meaningful separation. Based on this approach, seven robust clusters were identified, reflecting distinct types of thermal behavior of the samples. The cluster distribution correlates with known physicochemical properties of the feedstocks, confirming the validity of the proposed method. The developed approach enables automated classification of experimental biomass pyrolysis data, facilitates the identification of reference curves for modeling, and supports experimental quality control. The Python-based software implementation is integrated into a suite of pyrolysis data analysis tools, contributing to the systematization of knowledge and acceleration of research in the field of renewable feedstock processing.

title: Lossless Canonical Coding Design with FPGA Implementation Using High-Speed Architecture
authors: Maan Hameed, Hussein Shakor Mogheer, Haveen Yaseen Hussein Al-Zahawi, Yassir Al-Karawi
abstract: This study deals with transmission of multibit symbol characters and large data units. The traditional methods of compression and decompression tend to experience the problem of data loss, poor throughput, and delayed encoding times. In order to eliminate these constraints, proposed a contemporary circuit-driven structure comprising the Canonical Huffman encoding and decoding. Various important techniques have been incorporated in this design such as frequency analysis, sorting mechanisms, optimal state machine control and barrel shifter logic. The restructured hardware accelerator that is developed is successfully decreasing memory footprint and shortening use of hardware resources. The Canonical Huffman encoder/decoder is specifically optimized to work effectively with multibit data, so memory footprint and processing time both decrease greatly. In the implementation process, the system has 160-bit input and 90-bit output using variable length Huffman coding (VLC). Verilog HDL language and Model Sim simulation tools were used to verify the hardware design and the design was implemented on FPGA DE2 device to test it in the practical world. The optimized VLSI realizes data compression and decompression with greater performance of output rates of 144 GB/s and 991 GB/s in encoding and decoding respectively along with an increase in compression ratio of 56.27% percent. The proposed FPGA-based high-speed architecture is well suited for real-time industrial data processing in smart manufacturing and Industry 4.0 environments, where efficient, low-latency, and hardware-accelerated data handling is essential for high-performance computing infrastructures.

title: Comparative Evaluation of IoT-Based Telematics Platforms for Smart Fleet Management
authors: Irwin K. Zgambo, Natalia Staroverova
abstract: The rapid digitalization of the transportation sector has accelerated the integration of Internet of Things (IoT) technologies in fleet monitoring, safety management, and operational optimization. This paper presents a comprehensive comparative evaluation of IoT-based telematics platforms used in transportation systems management, focusing on international solutions (e.g., Samsara) and Russian platforms (Omnicomm, SCOUT, GLONASSsoft, Wialon, and Monitoring Avto). A multi-criteria assessment methodology is applied, encompassing functional decomposition, performance indicators, API integration capabilities, cybersecurity considerations, and total cost of ownership (TCO). A practical case study involving the deployment of Omnicomm Online in a large retail fleet ("Verny") validates the analytical framework. Furthermore, the paper details the development and implementation of a proactive, data-driven maintenance system leveraging API integration, demonstrating a practical pathway to enhance operational resilience. Results indicate that while international platforms excel in AI-driven analytics and ecosystem integration, Russian platforms offer competitive advantages in fuel monitoring accuracy, regulatory compliance, and cost efficiency. The study identifies key technological gaps and proposes actionable directions for enhancing domestic telematics platforms within smart transportation systems. These findings are relevant for engineers, system integrators, logistics operators, and policymakers involved in intelligent transport solutions.

title: A Neural Network System for Classifying Defects and Evaluating the Quality of Infographics on Marketplaces
authors: Viktoriia Akulinina, Alfira Akhmedova, Irina Zhazhneva
abstract: The article presents the implementation of a system for classifying defects and evaluating the quality of product infographics on marketplaces. The system is based on the DualHeadResNet neural network architecture, which simultaneously solves two tasks: the classification of visual defects and the regression assessment of the overall image quality on a 10-point scale. The developed web application is implemented on a Python/Flask stack with an HTML/CSS/JS frontend. The model was trained on its own dataset of more than 300 labeled images collected from the Russian marketplaces Wildberries, Ozon and Yandex Market. Experiments have shown the high accuracy of the system in detecting defects and forming specific recommendations for image improvement. The solution is aimed at sellers of small and medium-sized businesses and is aimed at increasing conversions by improving visual content.

title: Analytical Processing of Measurement Data and Parameter Identification of a Solar Array Simulator Mathematical Model
authors: Anastasia V. Yarkova, Ludmila V. Chernenkaya, Svetlana A. Yarkova
abstract: This paper addresses the problem of analytical processing of measurement data and parameter identification of a solar array simulator mathematical model used for power system testing. An approach based on a piecewise nonlinear approximation of the static current-voltage characteristic combined with a simplified dynamic model for transient response analysis under load variations is proposed. A simulation based computational experiment is conducted to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed method. Synthetic measurement data emulating real experimental measurements with noise are generated. Model parameter identification is performed using regression analysis and numerical simulation. The accuracy of parameter estimation, robustness to measurement noise, and the influence of load conditions and dynamic parameters on transient behavior are analyzed. The obtained results demonstrate the applicability of the proposed approach for processing measurement data from test benches, automating the tuning of solar array simulators, and improving the reliability of performance evaluation. The study is relevant to problems of system analysis, control, and information processing in modern energy systems.

title: State-Dependent Control of Switched Affine Dynamical Systems With Dwell-Time Constraints
authors: Rafael A. F. Carniello, Matheus Souza, Ely C. de Paiva
abstract: This letter addresses the state-dependent switching control design problem for continuous-time switched affine dynamical systems (SADS) under dwell-time constraints. By adopting a state-dependent, piecewise-continuous Lyapunov function, we derive design conditions for a switching rule that guarantees the existence of an exponentially stable invariant set. In addition to ensuring stability, the proposed switching rules satisfy dwell-time constraints, thereby mitigating undesirable chattering effects. The results are illustrated through numerical examples, including two academic benchmark systems and a direct current (DC) motor actuated by a buck–boost power converter.

title: Analysis of Cooperation Strategies Between Ride-Hailing Platforms and Taxi Companies Based on Random Forest Parameter Prediction
authors: Yan Gao, Huihui Wang
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 International Conference on Digital Transformation and Management
abstract: None

title: Joint Quality Assessment and Example-Guided Tone Mapping by Disentangling Picture Appearance from Content_supp1-3684812.pdf
authors: Abhinau Kumar Venkataramanan
abstract: The deep learning revolution has strongly impacted low-level image processing tasks such as style/domain transfer, enhancement/restoration, and visual quality assessments. Despite often being treated separately, the aforementioned tasks share a common theme of understanding, editing, or enhancing the appearance of input images without modifying the underlying content. We leverage this observation to develop a novel disentangled representation learning method that decomposes inputs into content and appearance features. The model is trained in a self-supervised manner and we use the learned features to develop a new quality prediction model named DisQUE. We demonstrate through extensive evaluations that DisQUE achieves state-of-the-art accuracy across quality prediction tasks and distortion types. Moreover, we demonstrate that the same features may also be used for image processing tasks such as HDR tone mapping, where the desired output characteristics may be tuned using example input-output pairs.

title: A Generalized Open-World Referring Expression Segmentation Approach to Biometric Forensics with Generative Multi-Agent System
authors: Neng Xu, Yanchao Guo, Dongcheng Han, Yang Xiao, Jiayu Chen, Liang Gong, Chenggang Yuan, Chengliang Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biometrics, Behavior, and Identity Science
abstract: In open-world biometric recognition, ambiguous descriptions, missing discriminative features, large-scale image collections, and potential target absence pose significant challenges. To address these issues, we propose Generative Multi-Agent Referring Expression Segmentation (GMA-RES), a method that localizes and segments biometric targets from natural language descriptions for biometric applications such as surveillance and forensic search. GMA-RES formulates open-world biometric recognition as an autonomously orchestrated multi-agent decision process, where a vision-language Planner Agent performs high-level semantic reasoning and dynamically coordinates multiple domain-specific Specialist Agents for image retrieval, localization, pixel-level segmentation, and verification. This modular design decouples semantic reasoning from biometric evidence generation, enabling adaptive multi-image reasoning and explicit absence handling without modifying pre-trained model architectures. A two-stage training paradigm further optimizes both reasoning accuracy and inference efficiency. Experimental evaluation on open-world biometric recognition tasks demonstrates that GMA-RES achieves an accuracy of 57.8%, significantly outperforming two-stage visual prompting methods (30.5%) and one-stage generative vision-language models (50.2%). The results validate the superior performance of GMA-RES in handling ambiguous queries, multi-image reasoning, and absent-target scenarios, providing a scalable and interpretable solution for open-world biometric applications.

title: Unified Model Aggregation of Active Distribution Network With Heterogeneous Uncertain Distributed Energy Resources
authors: Zhaocheng Zhang, Mingyu Yan, Houbo Xiong, Wei Gan, Jinyu Wen
abstract: This paper proposes a unified aggregated model of active distribution network (ADN), which aggregates the whole ADN as an equivalent generator/adjustable load with exchange limits considering heterogeneous uncertain distributed energy resources. A min-max bi-level mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) problem is built to check the disaggregation feasibility with given exchange limits. The network-constrained ADN model with DERs’ 0-1 operation status and uncertainties is built, where the adjustable robust optimization is adopted to handle uncertainties. To solve this problem, surrogate Lagrangian relaxation is used to relax the MILP problem into the bi-level linear programming (LP), which is further dualized to obtain a single-level MILP via the strong duality theory. We prove that the primal problem and the dual of its relaxed formulation share the same strong duality property. After the bi-level problem is solved, the initial infeasible dispatch command of all timeslots is obtained. Then, an iterative framework is proposed to adjust the aggregation exchange limits by analyzing the infeasible command and resolving the bi-level problem. The iteration converges until all feasible commands of all timeslots are obtained. The proposed method is tested on IEEE-18 and IEEE-85 systems. Numerical results verify the effectiveness of the proposed method in handling 0-1 operation status and ensuring robustness.

title: Linear Split Search: Enabling Efficient Near-Field Wideband Beam Training for High-Speed Railways
authors: Qingfeng Ding, Lan Chen, Xuanhang Qu
abstract: Near-field wideband beam training is critical for high-throughput communications in extremely large-scale multiple-input-multiple-output (XL-MIMO) systems, particularly in high-speed railway (HSR) scenarios. However, its performance is severely constrained by a spatiotemporal mismatch: high-mobility Doppler shifts disrupt temporal phase coherence, while the conventional two-dimensional (2D) angle-distance joint search is inefficient for tracking trains moving along a deterministic one-dimensional (1D) track. To address this issue, this letter proposes a linear split search (LSS) scheme that repurposes the wideband beam-split effect from a detrimental phenomenon into an effective sensing mechanism, thereby transforming the conventional discrete 2D search into a precise track-aligned 1D search. Specifically, LSS compensates for velocity-induced phase shifts and projects frequency-dependent focal trajectories onto the railway track to enable continuous coverage without frequent grid updates. In addition, through analysis of the near-field beamforming gain, a track-constrained linear coverage region is characterized to significantly reduce search redundancy. Simulation results show that the proposed LSS achieves near-optimal performance, approaching the perfect-CSI benchmark with an overhead of only two time slots, while maintaining a consistently high success rate across the HSR coverage region under typical train velocities.

title: Fast Prediction of Air-Gap Magnetic Flux Density and Electromagnetic Torque in Surface-Mounted Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors
authors: Zihan Li, Xing Zhao, Andy Tyrrell, Chao Gong
abstract: Accurate prediction of electromagnetic field distributions in Surface Mounted permanent-magnet synchronous motors (SPMSMs) is fundamental to achieving high-efficiency and high-performance designs. However, conventional finite element analysis (FEA) is computationally intensive and unsuitable for large-scale performance optimization. This paper proposes a fast prediction framework based on a conditional generative adversarial network (Pix2Pix) that integrates residual U-Net (ResUNet) and attention mechanisms to predict radial and tangential magnetic flux densities (Br and Bt) from motor geometry and current density maps. The proposed model introduces a four-channel input structure combining RGB geometry images with grayscale current-density maps to represent excitation conditions. A large-scale dataset comprising 16,650 paired samples across 31 temporal frames and multiple current levels was constructed, simulating realistic motor operation under dynamic excitation. Experimental results show that the proposed ResUNet-Attention Pix2Pix network achieves prediction accuracy exceeding 99% for Br and Bt distributions and torque estimation compared with FEA, while also demonstrating strong generalization ability across unseen SPMSM topologies (flat, radial, breadloaf). This approach provides a robust and precise framework for advanced electromagnetic characterization in SPMSM analysis, with cross-topology generalization validated across unseen SPMSM configurations.

title: Graph Attention-Driven Distributional Deep Reinforcement Learning for Collaborative Multi-AAV Navigation
authors: Selvam Essaky, Gunasekaran Raja, Harun Siljak
abstract: The rapid growth of Autonomous Aerial Vehicles (AAVs) has underscored their importance across commercial and civil applications. Combining multi-AAVs with Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) approaches can achieve significant results, particularly in navigation and path-planning operations. Nevertheless, collaborative decision-making among AAV swarms faces major challenges, including handling partial observability and re-path planning under uncertainty. To address these challenges, we propose an Adaptive Distributional deep Q-network Graph ATtention (ADGAT) learning framework with a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) modeling approach to perform effective AAV swarm navigation. The proposed ADGAT framework combines an adaptive Distributional Deep Q-Network (Di-DQN) reinforcement learning algorithm with a multi-head Graph Attention (GAT) mechanism to enable effective decision-making by distributing rewards rather than estimating the expected value. Additionally, the GAT mechanism enhances each AAV's capabilities by aggregating information from neighboring AAVs. We compare the proposed ADGAT framework against state-of-the-art baselines and validate it using three key performance metrics, achieving an average success rate of 98%, a collision rate of 3%, and a path efficiency of 96%. The extensive simulation results prove the adaptability of the ADGAT framework for enhancing AAV swarm navigation in partially observable spaces.

title: Data-Centric Deep Learning for Monkeypox Detection: Hyperdatasets Toward Robust and Generalizable Medical AI
authors: Anastasios Andreadis, Panagiotis Georgiadis, Efstathios Kazantzis, Theofanis Kalampokas, Eleni Vrochidou, George A. Papakostas
abstract: In this work, a structured hierarchy of hyperdatasets for Monkeypox image classification is presented, aiming to evaluate the robustness and generalization ability of deep classification models for Mpox detection. A systematic review is first conducted to identify 11 publicly available Mpox image datasets, while five hyperdatasets are constructed from them. Each hyperdataset represents a distinct level of complexity, spanning predefined and standardized class train-test splits, fully merged heterogenous datasets, and clinically-oriented binary classification datasets. Five state-of-the-art deep architectures are trained on both the original datasets and the five hyperdatasets, aiming to systematically examine how dataset composition, class granularity and domain variability may influence models’ performance, as well as the potential of hyperdatasets towards building more robust and generalizable models. Our results indicate that large-scale heterogenous integration of datasets can provide multiple benefits in the case of limited medical data to further strengthen the diagnostic accuracy of models, especially in clinically valuable binary classification, leading to an accuracy of up to 99.17% with DenseNet-169. In the case of multi-class classification, standardized re-split of train-test data is considered essential after datasets merging, leading to improved classification accuracy and avoidance of classical cross-domain overfitting. Results highlight the importance of hyperdatasets as a methodological approach for developing reliable and generalizable artificial intelligence (AI) systems for supporting infectious skin disease diagnosis.

title: Precise and Smooth Contact Force and Stiffness Control for an Electromagnetic Variable Stiffness Actuator Used in Robotic Grinding
authors: Xu Tang, Jixiang Yang, Han Ding
abstract: Precise and smooth force control is significant for ensuring the profile accuracy and surface quality of workpieces in robotic grinding. During the grinding process, fluctuations in contact force due to variations in workpiece stiffness affect grinding performance. This article proposes an integrated impact management and force–stiffness control (IMFS) framework for robotic grinding using an electromagnetic variable stiffness actuator (EMVSA) to stabilize contact forces in variable stiffness workpieces. A triple hyperbolic secant proportional–integral–derivative controller is used for force control, with a proposed analytical simplification of nonlinear gain parameters under small force errors, optimized through a normalized weighted multiobjective approach. A stiffness control strategy based on force feedback errors is developed by incorporating system dynamics and human-inspired error-based modulation, enabling real-time adjustment of electromagnetic spring stiffness. In addition, a method for managing impact forces during contact transitions is proposed, derived from the relationship between maximum impact force and input momentum, eliminating the need for distance sensors. Experiments on a robot equipped with EMVSA show that the IMFS method enhances robotic grinding performance compared to the benchmark method, reducing the average absolute force error, surface roughness Ra, and material removal error by 24.95%, 43.43%, and 50.18%, respectively.

title: X2Video: Adapting Diffusion Models for Multimodal Controllable Neural Video Rendering
authors: Zhitong Huang, Mohan Zhang, Renhan Wang, Rui Tang, Hao Zhu, Jing Liao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: We present X2Video, the first diffusion model for rendering photorealistic videos guided by a sequence of intrinsic channels including albedo, normal, roughness, metallicity, and irradiance, while supporting intuitive multi-modal controls with reference images and text prompts for both global and local regions. The intrinsic guidance allows accurate manipulation of color, material, geometry, and lighting, while reference images and text prompts provide intuitive adjustments in the absence of intrinsic information. To enable these functionalities, we extend the intrinsic-guided image generation model XRGB to video generation by employing a novel and efficient Hybrid Self-Attention, which ensures temporal consistency across video frames and also enhances fidelity to reference images. We further develop a Masked Cross-Attention to disentangle global and local text prompts, applying them effectively onto respective local and global regions. For generating long videos, our novel Recursive Sampling method incorporates progressive frame sampling, combining keyframe prediction and frame interpolation to maintain long-range temporal consistency while preventing error accumulation. To support the training of X2Video, we assembled a video dataset named InteriorVideo, featuring 1,154 rooms from 295 interior scenes, complete with reliable ground-truth intrinsic channel sequences and smooth camera trajectories. Both qualitative and quantitative evaluations demonstrate that X2Video can produce long, temporally consistent, and photorealistic videos guided by intrinsic conditions. Additionally, X2Video effectively accommodates multi-modal controls with reference images, global and local text prompts, and simultaneously supports editing on color, material, geometry, and lighting through parametric tuning. Upon acceptance, we will publicly release our model and dataset.

title: Synergistically Enhanced Toughness and Stability in Q[6]-Based Supramolecular Hydrogels for Wearable Sensing_supp1-3684792.mp4
authors: X. Xiao
abstract: Flexible sensors are confronted with the critical challenge of reconciling mechanical robustness with ionic conductivity. In this study, we present a PVA/PVP-Q[6]-NaCl composite hydrogel that surmounts this trade-off via synergistic supramolecular crosslinking and sodium ion regulation. Ion-dipole interactions between Na⁺ and Q[6] establish dynamic bridges, which concurrently strengthen the network and facilitate ionic dissociation. The optimized hydrogel attains a tensile strength of 303 kPa, an elongation of 308%, and a conductivity of 0.08 S/m. The resulting strain sensor demonstrates high sensitivity (48% change in resistance at 100% strain) and excellent stability over 1,400 stretch-release cycles, thereby enabling reliable monitoring of human joint motion. This research offers a supramolecular design strategy for durable wearable sensors.

title: An Automated Algorithm for Auditing Encryption Policies in Software Systems
authors: Jasur Baratov, Shohruh Mahkamov, Jura Kuvandikov
abstract: This paper presents an automated auditing algorithm for evaluating the enforcement of encryption policies in software systems. The proposed approach addresses key limitations of existing auditing practices, including the complexity of heterogeneous configurations, the inconsistency between policy requirements and operational settings, and the reliance on manual inspection. The methodology formalizes encryption policies as structured requirements and evaluates their compliance against configuration and runtime data using deterministic compatibility and risk assessment functions. By aggregating local deviations into a unified risk index, the algorithm provides a quantitative and reproducible measure of system-level non-compliance. The proposed method enables continuous monitoring of encryption policy enforcement across multiple architectural layers, including application, network, and storage components. Experimental evaluation on a test software system demonstrates that the algorithm effectively identifies policy violations, captures their relative impact on security risk, and tracks compliance improvements over successive audit iterations. The results indicate a consistent reduction in the overall risk index following corrective actions applied to cryptographic parameters such as key length, protocol configuration, and certificate management. The presented approach is platform-agnostic and relies on formal modeling rather than heuristic inspection, making it suitable for integration into enterprise and large-scale information systems. The findings confirm that a mathematically grounded auditing framework can enhance the precision, transparency, and repeatability of encryption policy compliance assessment.

title: NanoCockpit: Performance-optimized Application Framework for AI-based Autonomous Nanorobotics_supp1-3687493.pdf
authors: Elia Cereda
abstract: Autonomous nano-drones, powered by vision-based tiny machine learning (TinyML) models, are a novel technology gaining momentum thanks to their broad applicability and pushing scientific advancement on resource-limited embedded systems. Their small form factor, i.e., a few 10s grams, severely limits their onboard computational resources to sub-100mW microcontroller units (MCUs). The Bitcraze Crazyflie nano-drone is the de facto standard, offering a rich set of programmable MCUs for low-level control, multi-core processing, and radio transmission. However, roboticists very often underutilize these onboard precious resources due to the absence of a simple yet efficient software layer capable of time-optimal pipelining of multi-buffer image acquisition, multi-core computation, intra MCUs data exchange, and Wi-Fi streaming, leading to sub optimal control performances. Our NanoCockpit framework aims to fill this gap, increasing the throughput and minimizing the system's latency, while simplifying the developer experience through coroutine-based multi-tasking. In-field experiments on three real-world TinyML nanorobotics applications show our framework achieves ideal end-to-end latency, i.e. zero overhead due to serialized tasks, delivering quantifiable improvements in closed-loop control performance (−30% mean position error, mission success rate increased from 40% to 100%).

title: Rapid Indirect Diagnosis of MEMS Gyroscope Initial Bias With On-Board Capability for Guided Missiles
authors: Jinseong Kim, Gunwoo Lee, Hyunseok Choi, Younghun Heo, Sangmoon Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: This paper presents a rapid, on-board capable method for diagnosing initial bias failures in micro-electro-mechanical system (MEMS) gyroscopes, a critical factor for the navigation accuracy of guided missiles. The proposed technique operates exclusively on internal missile data, eliminating the need for sensor disassembly or external tools. The method is structured as a two-stage process: first, anomaly detection using the gyroscope output from the Initial Alignment, followed by fault diagnosis using the INS output from the Initial Navigation Mode. This integrated two-stage approach enables rapid identification of failures within seconds. Optimized for real-time embedded applications, the method achieves minimal computational overhead through simple averaging and matrix operations, while also offering high interpretability by linking its diagnostic results to physical models. Validated on a dataset of over 10,000 guided missile tests, the proposed method demonstrates high diagnostic accuracy and millisecond-level performance, confirming its effectiveness as a practical solution for on-board health monitoring of MEMS gyroscopes.

title: Fair Recourse for All: Ensuring Individual and Group Fairness in Counterfactual Explanations
authors: Fatima Ezzeddine, Obaida Ammar, Silvia Giordano, Omran Ayoub
abstract: Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) is becoming increasingly essential for enhancing the transparency of machine learning (ML) models. Among the various XAI techniques, counterfactual explanations (CFs) hold a pivotal role due to their ability to illustrate how changes in input features can alter an ML model’s decision, thereby offering actionable recourse to users. Ensuring that individuals with comparable attributes and those belonging to different protected groups (e.g., demographic) receive similar and actionable recourse options is essential for trustworthy and fair decision-making. In this work, we address this challenge directly by focusing on the generation of fair CFs. Specifically, we start by defining and formulating fairness at: 1) individual fairness, ensuring that similar individuals receive similar CFs, 2) group fairness, ensuring equitable CFs across different protected groups and 3) hybrid fairness, which accounts for both individual and broader group-level fairness. We formulate the problem as an optimization task and propose a novel model-agnostic, reinforcement learning based approach to generate CFs that satisfy fairness constraints at both the individual and group levels, two objectives that are usually treated as orthogonal. As fairness metrics, we extend existing metrics commonly used for auditing ML models, such as equal choice of recourse and equal effectiveness across individuals and groups. We evaluate our approach on four benchmark datasets, showing that it effectively ensures individual and group fairness while preserving the quality of the generated CFs in terms of proximity and plausibility. Our work opens a broader discussion on hybrid fairness and its role and implications for XAI and beyond CFs.

title: Stealing AI Model Weights Through Covert Communication Channels
authors: Valentin Barbaza, Alán Rodrigo Díaz-Rizo, Abdelrahman Emad Abdelazim, Emilien Dole, Hassan Aboushady, Spyridon Raptis, Haralampos-G. Stratigopoulos
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) models are often regarded as valuable intellectual property due to the high cost of their development, the competitive advantage they provide, and the proprietary techniques involved in their creation. As a result, AI model-stealing attacks pose a serious concern for AI model providers. In this work, we present a novel attack targeting wireless devices equipped with AI hardware accelerators. The attack unfolds in two phases. In the first phase, the victim’s device is compromised with a hardware Trojan (HT) designed to covertly leak model weights through a hidden communication channel, without the victim realizing it. In the second phase, the adversary uses a nearby wireless device to intercept the victim’s transmission frames during normal operation and incrementally reconstruct the complete weight matrix. The proposed attack is agnostic to both the AI model architecture and the hardware accelerator used. We validate our approach through a hardware-based demonstration involving five diverse AI models of varying types and sizes. We detail the design of the HT and the covert channel, highlighting their stealthy nature. Additionally, we analyze the impact of bit error rates (BERs) on the reception and propose an error mitigation technique. The effectiveness of the attack is evaluated based on the accuracy of the reconstructed models with stolen weights and the time required to extract them. Finally, we explore potential defense mechanisms.

title: Temporally-Preserving Quantum LSTM With Multiscale Attention Mechanisms for Soft Sensor Applications
authors: Xian-Bing Meng, Bi-Cheng Guo, Kangkang Xu, Han-Xiong Li, C. L. Philip Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Soft sensor modeling is the task of using measurable variables to predict target ones, which may involve nonlinearity and temporally dynamics. Despite significant progress, there is a relative lack of research on soft sensor-oriented Quantum Long Short-Term Memory (QLSTM) design and application. As a novel quantum soft sensor approach, a temporally-preserving QLSTM (TP-QLSTM) is proposed by integrating with multiscale attention mechanisms. Specifically, a temporally-preserving-based adaptive variational quantum circuit is designed by establishing entanglement across temporal inputs to improve the temporally learning capability of QLSTM. Moreover, multiscale attention mechanisms are designed in the input and output parts of TP-QLSTM to learn nonlinearities and temporal dynamics from different feature dimensions and temporal ones, as well as from historical hidden states and temporal outputs. In addition, a referential attention-based autoregressive forecasting strategy is proposed as output module of TP-QLSTM to predict the target results. Experiments on two soft sensor cases demonstrate the effectiveness and practicality of our method. Note to Practitioners—In industrial processes, soft sensors are critical for achieving comprehensive state awareness and predictive management. However, practical soft sensors often struggle with complex nonlinear and time-dependent relationships in dynamic processes. As an innovative attempt to enhance soft sensing from a quantum algorithm perspective, we propose a TP-QLSTM by integrating multiscale attention mechanisms. Through designing adaptive quantum circuits that preserve temporal correlations and incorporating multiscale attention mechanisms, our method effectively captures multiscale dynamics and nonlinearities from process data. This enables accurate and robust predictions of key variables in industrial settings. Experiments on real cases confirm that our method achieves high prediction accuracy while maintaining low computational latency—with an average inference time of less than 0.6 ms per sample. A current limitation is that the quantum algorithm is implemented using simulated quantum circuits, which restricts the full exploitation of quantum parallelism. Future work will focus on deploying the model to hardware platforms that support real quantum circuit computation, thus enabling efficient training and inference.

title: ACOF: An Adaptive Container Orchestration Framework for Cloud-Native Systems
authors: Muhammad Usman, Bilal Maqbool, Muhammad Shahid Iqbal
publish: 2026 29th Conference on Innovation in Clouds, Internet and Networks (ICIN)
abstract: The widespread adoption of containerized microservices has made Kubernetes the de facto standard for managing modern cloud-native applications. Despite Kubernetes's advanced features, it presents steep usability hurdles for users, even during common tasks such as workload specification and deployment. These challenges arise from fragmented workflows, a lack of usability-oriented design, and a reliance on low-level configuration languages such as YAML. To address these challenges, we present an Adaptive Container Orchestration Framework (ACOF) that aims to streamline the management of containerized workloads by offering guided workflows and integrated instant performance monitoring in a simplified interface. ACOF supports the full deployment lifecycle while reducing dependency on command-line expertise and exposing the internals of distributed clusters to users. We evaluated the usability aspects of ACOF through a mixed-methods study. Results from task completion analysis, the System Usability Scale (SUS), and post-study interviews highlight improvements in usability and efficiency.

title: Formalizing Distributed Industrial Monitoring: A Phased Data Flow and Knowledge-Centric Architecture for IIoT-Enabled Production Systems
authors: Arsen Tagaev, Murad Abdulzagirov, Dmitriy Nikitin
abstract: This article presents a formalized architecture of a portable hardware-software complex (PHSC) for monitoring the condition of production systems as a distributed IIoT platform based on multi-level data processing: sensor → broker → analytics → control → reporting. A phased data-flow model is proposed to support the transition from raw signal acquisition to management decision-making. Particular attention is paid to the organization of data across the Data Lake → Knowledge Base → Reports levels, which enables the implementation of a scalable and adaptive system for digital management of production assets. In addition, managerial and information mechanisms are developed for the seamless integration of the PHSC into existing IT and production environments, including the delineation of microservice responsibility zones, the adoption of CI/CD approaches in industrial monitoring, and OTA sensor firmware updates via BLE. The proposed architecture is shown to provide flexibility, fault tolerance, and the possibility of evolutionary system development without interrupting the production cycle. Unlike traditional studies, the focus is placed not on sensor types, but on lifecycle management of the hardware-software monitoring infrastructure.

title: Skin Cancer Detection from Dermatoscopic Images through Integrated Deep Features and Ensemble Classification
authors: Akhmadkhon Bobokhonov, F. T. Mullajonova, M. A. Kuchkarov
abstract: The increase number of skin diseases year by year and their variety cause some difficulties in identifying the disease and classifying them. Skin diseases exhibit different sizes, shapes and visual characteristics that require an individual treatment strategy for each type of disease. Early detection of skin diseases is an important factor in preventing the adverse effects of the disease and treating them. Today, a lot of scientific researches are being conducted to solve this problem. However, the detection of skin diseases remains inefficient, imprecise and labor-intensive in manual procedures, emphasizing the need for automated methods. This research work presents an effective approach for early detection of skin diseases based on medical images and minimizing dependence on manual intervention. In the proposed method, the guided filtering techniques were combined with anisotropic Gaussian side windows (AGSW) to improve image clarity. On the basis of morphological analysis, regions of non-disease were removed from images, and with the help of deep neural networks, images were segmented, extracting high-quality regions of interest (ROIs) and multidimensional features. The proposed methodology used a soft voting classifier on the Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Machine (SVM), and K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) models to categorize skin diseases identified from images. The proposed technique achieves an overall accuracy of 98.67% on the publicly available ISIC 2019 skin image dataset.

title: Resource-Saving Distance Protection for Power Lines
authors: Dauren Dzhambulovich Issabekov
abstract: The publication presents the development and operating principle of an alternative resource-saving remote protection for electrical equipment connected to the cell of closed switchgears (CS), 110 kV and above. Here traditional measuring current transformers (CT) with metal cores are not used. Such current transformers have significant weight and dimensional parameters, as well as high cost. Scientific novelty of the work consists in the developed theory of construction of protection on inductive coils, based on measurement of electromotive force values in different modes, including points inside the cell of a closed switchgear. The use of inductive coils, proposed as a new element base in relay protection instead of traditional current transformers, is an innovative solution. It represents an excellent prospect for further development of relay protection. The scientific paper is purely research in nature. The result of the work is the creation of an alternative resource-saving distance protection on inductive coils.

title: The Field Effect Transistor Journey Through Our Lives: Tracing the First Bits of Its Applications [Speaker’s Corner]
authors: Alba Avila

title: Channel Gain Map Estimation Based on 3-D Virtual Scatterer Model
authors: He Sun, Lipeng Zhu, Jie Xu, Rui Zhang
abstract: This paper proposes an efficient method for modeling and reconstructing the channel gain map (CGM) based on virtual scatterers. Specifically, we develop a virtual scatterer model to characterize the channel gain distribution in three-dimensional (3D) space, by capturing the multi-path propagation environment structure and exploiting the angular-domain spatial correlation of scatterer response. In this model, the CGM is represented as a function over a set of tunable parameters for virtual scatterers, including their number, positions, and scatterer response coefficients (SRCs), which can be estimated from a limited number of channel gain measurements at a given set of locations within the region of interest. This new representation offers a flexible and scalable modeling framework for efficient and accurate CGM reconstruction. Furthermore, we propose a progressive estimation algorithm to acquire the scatterers’ parameters. In this algorithm, we gradually increase the number of virtual scatterers to balance the computational complexity and reconstruction accuracy, and derive the closed-form solutions of SRCs with any given number and positions of virtual scatterers. In addition, by exploiting the spatial correlation of scatterer response, we propose a Gaussian process regression (GPR)-based inference method to predict the SRCs that cannot be directly estimated. Finally, ray-tracing-based simulation results under realistic physical environments validate the effectiveness of the proposed method, demonstrating that it achieves higher reconstruction accuracy compared to conventional CGM estimation approaches, especially for the scenario with limited channel measurements.

title: StudyFlow Collaborative Note-Taking: New Opportunities for Interactive Learning
authors: Oleg K. Malkov, Maria Skvortsova
abstract: In the context of the digital transformation of higher education (Education 4.0), students face the problem of fragmentation of educational materials and the complexity of their systematisation, as well as the problem of cognitive overload when they need to simultaneously perceive lecture material and take structured notes. Existing note-taking tools work in isolation, failing to provide integration of collaborative editing and audio streaming within the academic data structure. This study proposes the StudyFlow architectural solution – a microservice platform for synchronous note-taking during the learning process. The system implements a cyber-physical approach, combining AI-assisted recognition and collective content verification through WebSocket synchronisation. Experimental evaluation of the prototype demonstrated a median synchronisation delay of p50 = 55 ms under a load of 20 concurrent users and a word error rate (WER) of 0.18 in a lecture hall setting. The results confirm the technical feasibility of creating a unified environment for formalising collective knowledge in real time. In the context of the digitalisation of education, students face the problem of fragmentation of educational materials and the complexity of their systematisation. Existing solutions often do not support the specifics of the academic process and real-time collaboration. This paper presents the StudyFlow software solution, a web-based platform for collaborative note-taking, designed to reduce cognitive load and formalise student knowledge. The proposed system implements a microservice architecture using the WebSocket protocol for real-time synchronisation of changes and integration with cloud APIs for streaming conversion of the teacher's speech into text.

title: Reclaimed Land Identification Model Based on Earth Remote Sensing Data Analysis
authors: Vladimir E. Borovoy, Maria Skvortsova
abstract: Recognition and vectorisation of reclamation land boundaries based on Earth remote sensing (ERS) data are critical tasks for land use monitoring, but manual polygon marking is extremely labour-intensive. In this work, the task of field selection is formulated as instance segmentation, which ensures the correct separation of adjacent areas and the obtaining of closed contours suitable for integration into systems. YOLOv11-seg was chosen as the base architecture, providing an optimal balance between accuracy and inference speed. The model was trained on the large-scale FBIS-22M dataset and fine-tuned on a unique sample of fields in the Volgograd region (Russian Federation). Experiments have shown that fine-tuning on regional data allows achieving amAP@0.5of 0.747 at a processing speed of 115 ms/image, which significantly exceeds the performance of two-stage analogues in terms of speed. Experiments show an increase in quality with an increase in data volume and the preservation of high inference speed; fine-tuning on regional data improves metrics compared to the baseline model and ensures applicability for the analysis of agricultural fields in the Russian Federation.

title: Short-term Wind Power Prediction Based on 1D-CNN with Historical Information Reuse
authors: Peilong Xiao
abstract: Wind power prediction is crucial for the safe and stable operation of the new-type power system. However, the volatility and intermittency of new energy output as well as errors in NWP data affect prediction accuracy. Existing methods mostly focus on NWP error correction, which is difficult to completely solve the problem. This paper proposes a short-term wind power prediction method based on 1D-CNN with historical power reuse. Taking 170m wind speed as the matching bridge, the historical power sequence is obtained through similarity matching and used as model input together with NWP data. Experiments based on data from a 400MW wind farm in Jilin Province show that compared with the CNN without the historical power reuse module, LSTM, and random forest models, the proposed method effectively improves prediction accuracy. In the future, the model will be further optimized to enhance its ability to capture local fluctuation details of the prediction curve.

title: AI-assisted Independent Learning Model in Education of Industry 4.0
authors: O.Kh. Rashidova, D. Begmatova, A.E. Rashidov
abstract: Today, when artificial intelligence and digital technologies are rapidly developing, training qualified specialists for Industry 4.0 is one of the most relevant issue. The solution to this problem is to train future specialists in accordance with the requirements of Industry 4.0 during their student years. In particular, the correct organization of the independent learning process plays an important role in the formation of knowledge and skills of future specialists. However, in today's existing approaches, there is a problem of individual orientation of the educational process, insufficient provision of quick feedback, and lack of opportunities for continuous assistance in the development of knowledge and skills. One of the most correct solutions to this problem today is the use of artificial intelligence and digital technologies. For this purpose, in this research work, an AI-assisted Independent Learning Model was developed to organize the independent learning process of future specialists. The general scheme of this model and the system architecture for it were developed in the research work. At the same time, the methodology for implementing this model in practice was studied. At the end of the study, the experimental results were evaluated and analyzed. The indicators Knowledge Performance Score, Independent Learning Score, Time Efficiency Score, Motivation Score, Academic Integrity Score were used to evaluate the results of the pilot test. The results obtained proved that the proposed model can be used as an important tool for training specialists with knowledge and skills for Industry 4.0.
