title: Manipulating Proof Certificate Invariants in the Presence of Model Transformations
authors: G. Cabodi, P. E. Camurati, M. Palena, P. Pasini
abstract: Trust in the outcomes of state-of-the-art hardware model checkers is more and more related to the availability of external certification tools, especially when the result is a “pass” verdict that cannot be simulated. This paper addresses one of the major weaknesses of proof certifiers: the difficulty of exploiting a proof certificate obtained after the model was pre-processed with optimizations and transformations. Most proof engines provide a certificate in terms of an inductive invariant that could be easily certified by an external checker on the transformed model. On the other hand, proving the correctness of each model transformation is basically infeasible using an external independent tool. Recent works have followed the idea of post-processing both the model and the inductive invariant, by generating a witness model and a transformed invariant: the certifier then checks that the invariant holds on the witness model and that the witness model is consistent with the original one. We follow here the alternative path of fully lifting the invariant, back to the original model, with a two-fold target: (a) simplifying the task of the model checker, which is only required to output the final invariant and a log of the transformations done; (b) possibly reusing the invariant for other tasks on the model, such as logic synthesis optimizations and proofs of other properties. Specifically, we target the following transformations: temporal decomposition, phase abstraction and equivalence and constant propagation. As this process requires existential quantification operations, we show how to exploit Craig’s interpolation to generate new inductive invariants. We also address scalability issues by applying a state-of-the-art SAT-based simplification procedure capable of reducing the size of the generated invariants when converted to NNF form. We experimentally demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach on a set of invariants derived from publicly available hardware model-checking benchmarks.

title: Residual Interference Analysis and Feasible Power Allocation under Imperfect SIC in NOMA Systems
authors: Hyojin Lee, Jinu Gong, Jimyung Oh, Jeehaeng Han, Joonhyuk Kang
abstract: Non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) relies on successive interference cancellation (SIC) to separate superposed signals, but practical SIC is imperfect due to demodulation errors. In downlink NOMA, such errors make the post-SIC residual interference depend on the power allocation, creating a tradeoff between SIC reliability and desired signal detection. This letter derives a closed-form expression for the average residual interference power in two-user M-ary quadrature amplitude modulation (QAM) NOMA and characterizes the feasible power allocation interval via a complete graph-shape characterization. The proposed framework provides a rigorous basis for power allocation feasibility under practical SIC conditions.

title: SAVLT: Structure-Aware Vision-Language Tuning for Multi-Center Cervical OCT Diagnosis
authors: Mi Yin, Yuchen Pei, Yixiong Zou, Yan Zhang, Yutao Ma
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Cervical optical coherence tomography (OCT) enables micrometer-scale visualization of tissue, yet trustworthy diagnosis under limited supervision remains challenging. While vision-language models (VLMs) offer a solution by leveraging consistent clinical semantics, their adaptation is hindered by confounding artifacts that masquerade as biological structures. This phenomenon hijacks global attention, obscuring the pathological layer degradation that is crucial for diagnosis. To overcome this limitation, we propose SAVLT, a structure-aware tuning framework that adapts VLMs via parameter-efficient fine-tuning. To shift from global matching to anatomical grounding, SAVLT introduces a region-aware spatial attention (RaSA) module that enforces spatial constraints. RaSA purifies visual representations from non-biological noise, restoring the model's focus on intra-tissue structural integrity. Furthermore, a dual-constraint objective couples image-text alignment with learnable visual prototypes to stabilize optimization against multi-center domain shifts and prompt variations. Validated across a multi-center cohort and two external test datasets, SAVLT delivers robust few-shot generalization and clinical interpretability, establishing a reliable paradigm for deploying foundation models in heterogeneous OCT imaging. Source code is publicly available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/rabbit-my/SAVLT</uri>.

title: Privacy Concerns in the Use of Instant Messaging Apps in Healthcare: a Systematic Review
authors: Giorgia Panico, Emanuele Raso, Elisabetta Biasin, Lorenzo Bracciale, Pierpaolo Loreti
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Instant messaging applications are an integral part of everyday life, facilitating communication in various settings, including work and healthcare. This systematic review aims to analyze the healthcare contexts in which these applications are most commonly used (i.e., work organization, education, communication among healthcare personnel, communication with patients), with a particular focus on privacy issues raised by the handling of sensitive data on these platforms and how, or if, these issues are addressed. Following a systematic literature review process conducted according to PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses) guidelines, the articles were examined with the goal of extracting the aforementioned information. The results reveal that the majority of studies (82.6%) adopt commercial solutions to improve communication in medical field, with most of them using WhatsApp as the primary communication channel. Although the studies acknowledge the privacy risks introduced by these applications, the description of how such risks are mitigated or addressed is rarely reported in detail; what is consistently highlighted instead is the practical lack of clarity in existing regulations about the pratical applicability.

title: A Robust Fault Diagnosis Method for Rotating Bearing of Inverter-fed Machine Using PWM Switching Oscillations
authors: Hao Li, Jun Zhu, Dawei Xiang, Jialiang Han, Yifan Chen
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: Bearing fault diagnosis is very important for the safe and reliable operation of inverter-fed machine systems. Bearing fault diagnosis on inverter-fed machines has long been a research hotspot with fruitful results. In recent years, deep learning (DL) has been widely used in bearing fault diagnosis due to its strong feature extraction ability for big data. However, the bearing of an inverter-fed machine operates under variable working conditions, making it difficult to meet the requirements of DL methods in practical scenarios. Therefore, this paper proposes a bearing fault diagnosis method based on high-frequency PWM switching oscillations. The approach utilizes MHz-level common-mode current single switching oscillation segments extracted via an endpoint detection algorithm for online mechanical bearing fault diagnosis. Firstly, the mechanism of bearing faults and the bearing current in inverter-fed machines are analyzed. This current, as a component of the motor's common-mode current, can be measured non-invasively. Secondly, to address the high-frequency and weak nature of the signal, a dedicated endpoint detection algorithm is designed to reliably extract the maximum-energy oscillation events strongly correlated with the bearing condition. Finally, a lightweight one-dimensional convolutional neural network is constructed. Experimental validation on a 3-kW induction motor drive system confirms the accuracy and operational adaptability of the proposed method.

title: Supporting Real-Time Mobile Applications in the Cloud-to-Things Continuum through Dynamic Resource Allocation
authors: Marco Pettorali, Francesca Righetti, Carlo Vallati, Sajal K. Das, Giuseppe Anastasi
abstract: The increasing adoption of Internet of Things (IoT) devices and growing demand for real-time applications have driven a shift in the computation paradigm from Cloud computing toward Edge computing, giving rise to the Cloud-to-Things Continuum (C2TC). Many real-time IoT applications involve Mobile Nodes (MNs), such as mobile robots or autonomous vehicles, which may join or leave the system dynamically. In addition, future reconfigurable IoT systems will be characterized by multiple applications with heterogeneous requirements dynamically deployed or removed on the same infrastructure. To support such environment, dynamic management mechanisms are needed to ensure that the requirements of different real-time applications are guaranteed despite frequent system reconfigurations (e.g., application deployment and removal). This paper proposes DJ-NECORA, an online algorithm for joint allocation of networking and computing resources in C2TC, capable of guaranteeing the requirements of real-time applications while efficiently managing system reconfigurations and a varying number of MNs. DJ-NECORA is evaluated via simulations in a realistic scenario against two state-of-the-art solutions and an Ideal algorithm. Results show that DJ-NECORA manages system reconfigurations efficiently, provides better Quality of Service (QoS), and performs close to the Ideal algorithm.

title: Accurate Estimation of Peak Performance in Automotive Electric Machines Using Advanced AI based Deep Saturation Extrapolation Technique
authors: Amit Ranjan Roy, R.M. Ram Kumar, Gaurang Vakil, Gaurav Kumar, Muhammad Raza Khowja, Kuldeep Singh, Tianjie Zou, Chris Gerada, Arvind Choubey
abstract: High-performance electric steels are used in automotive electric machines to realize superior efficiency and power density. These laminations are pushed to deep saturation during peak operation of the electric machine. The lack of material data on deep saturation results in inaccurate modeling of peak operating conditions of automotive electric machines. This has led to the development of several empirical models for extrapolating the available B-H curves. These empirical models require additional test data and are found to have limited accuracy in deep-saturation prediction. Additionally, experimental deep saturation characterization requires dedicated high-power amplifier-based test equipment, which is not always available. To overcome these challenges, this paper introduces a practically impactful AI-based application using a multi-material artificial neural network (ANN) model to predict the deep saturation behavior of high-performance electric steels accurately. Several machine learning (ML) algorithms are studied with single- and multi-material data input strategies. Among them, the ANN model with multi-material input is found to consistently outperform the others in predicting the saturation region, indicating its reliability and generalizability. Finally, the performance of the proposed multi-material input-based ANN model is demonstrated by predicting the peak torque of a prototyped and experimentally validated high-speed permanent magnet-assisted synchronous reluctance (PM-SynRel) machine.

title: Discrete-Time Nonlinear Flux Observer for Sensorless SPMSM Drives With Low Carrier Ratios
authors: Mei Su, Zhongyu Li, Hanbing Dan, Jiong Li, Feng Zhou, Yao Sun, Marco Rivera, Patrick Wheeler
abstract: This paper proposes a discrete-time nonlinear flux observer based sensorless control scheme for surface-mounted permanent magnet synchronous motor (SPMSM) drives, specifically under low-pulse width modulation (PWM) to operating fundamental frequency ratios. As the motor speed increases, the pole positions of the nonlinear flux observer in the discrete-time domain tend to approach the unstable region. To address this issue, a complex-vector discrete-time SPMSM flux model is established, from which a discrete-time nonlinear flux observer is proposed. To improve stability across the entire speed range, appropriate gain selection is required. The gains are obtained by minimizing an error function derived from a current prediction model, yielding a gain design method that minimizes the amplitude error of the estimated flux. The effectiveness of the proposed method is verified through experimental results, even at a low carrier ratio of 5.2.

title: DeWDRoP: An Automated Framework for Detecting and Characterizing Welding Defects Using High-Density Point Clouds
authors: Sergio C. Vidal, Carlos V. Regueiro, Margarita Amor, Pablo Mayobre, Berta Antelo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: A stringent, stable, and accurate system for the detection of welding defects is essential in Industry 4.0 manufacturing environments, aiming for near-zero defects. This work introduces DeWDRoP, an offline system for detecting and characterizing welding defects using high-density point clouds (between 20 and 30 million points, with a resolution over 130 μm). We propose a novel theoretical framework based on two key innovations: Region-Based Seam Extraction (RBSE), a segmentation method tailored to isolate welding seams from high-density point clouds, and Tolerance-Based Deviation Analyzer (TBDA), a method that quantifies local deviations for geometry tolerance evaluation. These methods improve the identification, classification, and characterization of defects. The system is designed to augment the capabilities of expert non-destructive testing inspectors, facilitating more precise and consistent evaluations. Experiments on real weld datasets confirm the framework’s suitability for high-quality manufacturing environments. It accurately identifies spatters, pores, and fillet weld asymmetries, achieving a recall rate of 98.48 % and a Type II error of 1.52 %.

title: Hybrid Attention and Transformer Network for Fault Signal Analysis in Rotating Machinery
authors: Depei Shao, Dezun Zhao, Tianyang Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Fault signals of rotating machinery exhibit pronounced non-stationary behavior under time-varying conditions, posing significant challenges for current state-of-theart deep learning-based time-frequency analysis (TFA) methods, which often fail to achieve high-resolution characterization of closely-spaced and crossover instantaneous frequencies (IFs). To address this challenge, a novel TFA model, termed the hybrid attention and Transformer network (HATN), is proposed. In the HATN, the multi-stage hybrid enhancement module (HEM) and adaptive layer attention module (ALAM) are proposed to optimize the time-frequency representation (TFR). Specifically, an efficient grouped Swin-Transformer (EGST) is first designed in the HEM to efficiently capture the time and frequency transformation characteristics of the TFR by exploiting global dependency. Second, the EGST and two attention mechanisms are alternately arranged to recalibrate the importance of dynamic TFR, and the energy concentration is iteratively enhanced. Finally, the ALAM is developed to adaptively adjust the hierarchical weight based on cross-layer feature correlation to strengthen the transfer and fusion of key features. The simulated signal, bearing signal with multiple faults, and planetary gearbox signal with a single fault are used to analyze, and the effectiveness of the HATN is verified by comparing with the various TFA methods.

title: A Novel Non-Invasive Non-Recursive Impedance Estimation Strategy for Converter Dominated Systems: An Approach for Wind Farms
authors: Mirhamed Pourmirasghariyan, David Campos-Gaona, Panagiotis N. Papadopoulos, Olimpo Anaya-Lara, Georgios Rogdakis, Helena Perez-Martinez
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: This paper proposes a strategy for estimating the output admittance of a converter and the impedance of a network simultaneously. The strategy is based on an optimized P-Q perturbation through the Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) system, which ensures that the system's operating point is fairly maintained by minimizing the Phase Locked Loop (PLL) error. To achieve this, a mathematical relationship between the PLL error and active and reactive powers is derived through the Phasor Diagram (PD), and then an optimization problem is proposed to inject a wide-band multi-sine perturbation through the P-Q channels optimally. The proposed strategy brings about several advantages which address some of the drawbacks of the existing methods as being non-invasive, economical, non-recursive, online, and accurate. Moreover, in the grid-following converters, the output admittance is very sensitive to the PLL performance. Hence, the proposed strategy is suitable for grid-forming converters where there is no PLL. In this paper, a detailed industry-based benchmark power system containing Offshore Wind Farm (OWF) is studied and assessed with the collaboration of OceanWinds (OW) company. Simulations are conducted in MATLAB/PSCAD models to verify the validity of the proposed strategy. Downscale experimental verification is provided to validate the performance of the proposed strategy.

title: Canonical Form and Finite Blocklength Bounds for Stabilizer Codes
authors: Dimiter Ostrev
abstract: First, a canonical form for stabilizer parity check matrices of arbitrary size and rank is derived. Next, it is shown that the closely related canonical form of the Clifford group can be computed in time O(n3) for n qubits, which improves upon the previously known time O(n6). Finally, the related problem of finite blocklength bounds for stabilizer codes and Pauli noise is studied. A finite blocklength refinement of the hashing bound is derived, and it is shown that no argument that uses guessing the error as a substitute for guessing the coset can lead to a significantly better achievability bound.

title: Universal Cloud Hardware Component Firewalls as an Element of Adaptive Cyberprotection of Electric Power Facilities
authors: Sergei V. Koryakin, Igor V. Bochkarev, Rifkhat R. Khramshin
abstract: Modern automated information systems in the electric power sector face rising computational demands, expanding network connectivity, and increasingly sophisticated cyber threats. These challenges require specialized hardware and software solutions to ensure effective cybersecurity. This paper develops principles for organizing a hardware cloud within cybersecurity systems for power facilities and proposes the architecture of a cloud-based firewall designed to provide adaptive protection for automated control and monitoring systems. The study reviews existing hardware and software approaches to information and cybersecurity in electric power applications and outlines methods for building a hardware cloud based on functional microelectronic components. A prototype cloud-based firewall operating within a hardware-oriented communication environment for power facilities is introduced. The paper presents an approach to configuring firewall functionalities integrated into a hardware cloud and managed by neural network–based adaptive algorithms. In addition, the architecture and operational algorithm of a hardware firewall intended for industrial process networks in the power industry are described. Clear definitions of "hardware cloud" and "hardware cloud network" are formulated specifically for energy infrastructure cybersecurity. The proposed solutions can support the design, upgrade, and deployment of cybersecurity systems at power facilities, including dispatch control centers, digital substations, and distributed power grids. Overall, the architecture enhances scalability, adaptability, and operational efficiency of firewall systems, enabling them to respond effectively to dynamically evolving cyber threats characteristic of the energy sector.

title: Organizing the Educational Process Using Artificial Intelligence for Teaching Secure Programming to Undergraduate Students
authors: Andrey M. Shabalin, Kirill I. Gladkikh, Aleksandr A. Zakharov
abstract: This paper addresses the pressing problem of insufficient preparation of computer science undergraduates in the field of secure software development. It analyzes contemporary research highlighting the significance of this approach to software engineering and identifying a deficit of corresponding skills among early-career professionals. The paper examines the causes of this situation, including the absence of mandatory specialized courses in university curricula. Existing approaches to improving the quality of education in secure programming are reviewed, with particular attention devoted to the use of automated tools. The paper proposes a novel approach to addressing this problem, based on employing artificial intelligence as an intermediary between the student and the instructor, aimed at enhancing the competence of future professionals in ensuring the information security of the software products they develop.

title: Blind Evaluation of Large Language Models Applied to Employee Feedback Tasks
authors: Konstantin Babushkin, Nikolai Timofeev, Ivan Filianin
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used in HR systems. However, selecting suitable models remains difficult. Employee feedback (EF) tasks are subjective, and existing benchmarks do not capture LLM performance in such domain. This paper presents a structured blind evaluation of LLM-generated EF texts. Several LLMs are compared across three EF tasks: summarization, interpretation, and idea-to-text transformation. Outputs are evaluated by human volunteers and LLMs. The evaluation criteria are organized into three dimensions: linguistic quality, faithfulness, and practical utility. The results show systematic differences between human and LLM evaluations. They also provide task-dependent evidence for comparing and selecting LLMs for EF systems.

title: Grounding DINO–Based Detection of Bacilli in Microscopy Images
authors: Aleksei Samarin, Dmitry Nazarenko, Anastasia Mamaeva
abstract: This paper investigates the application of the open-vocabulary detector Grounding DINO for bacilli detection in microscopy images. Detecting rod-shaped bacteria in routine microscopy remains challenging due to strong appearance variability caused by staining differences, uneven illumination, background debris, and sensor noise, while the targets themselves are often small, low-contrast, and visually similar to artifacts. To address this gap, we propose a practical detection pipeline that combines microscopy-oriented preprocessing (contrast and exposure correction with edge-preserving sharpening) with text-guided inference in Grounding DINO. We study prompt formulation and confidence-threshold calibration to increase sensitivity to thin, elongated bacillary structures while suppressing false positives on stain precipitates and smear artifacts. The approach is evaluated on a curated microscopy dataset with bounding-box annotations collected under heterogeneous acquisition conditions and compared against representative one-stage, two-stage, and keypoint-based detectors under the same training protocol. The results show that preprocessing consistently improves localization quality across models, and the best overall performance is obtained with Grounding DINO 1.5 under the full preprocessing configuration, achieving mAP<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">50</inf> = 0.959 and mAP<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">50:95</inf> = 0.918 on the held-out test split. These findings indicate that vision-language detectors can be transferred to microbiological microscopy through lightweight domain adaptation, enabling robust bacilli localization without costly full-scale retraining.

title: Design and Economic Evaluation of an Integrated Information System for Agricultural Transport Automation
authors: Sergei Kurashkin, Vladimir Edreev, Timofey Mariupolskiy
abstract: Transport logistics in medium-sized agricultural enterprises is frequently managed using manual planning tools, fragmented monitoring mechanisms, and non-integrated documentation systems. This results in limited data visibility, inefficient route coordination, increased labor intensity, and elevated operational costs. Existing research addresses route optimization, GPS monitoring, and Logistics 4.0 concepts separately, but lacks integrated system architectures tailored to farm-level transport environments with structured economic validation. This study proposes an integrated client–server information system for agricultural transport automation based on AS-IS to TO-BE process transformation. The system architecture combines automated route planning, real-time vehicle monitoring, electronic document management, and analytical reporting within a unified digital environment. Business process reengineering was performed using IDEF0 and structured decomposition modeling to eliminate redundant manual operations and integrate logistics workflows. Comparative cost analysis demonstrates a reduction of approximately 50% in daily operational logistics expenses. Annual savings reach approximately 15,300 USD, with an estimated payback period of about two years. The results confirm that process-oriented system integration provides a technically feasible and economically justified solution for digital transformation of transport operations in medium-sized agricultural enterprises.

title: Adaptive Magnetometer Calibration for Accurate Heading Estimation in Unmanned Aerial Vehicle Navigation in a Varying Magnetic Environment
authors: Nikita Nosov, Mikhail Budko, Alexei Guirik
abstract: This paper addresses accurate heading estimation from magnetometer measurements for unmanned aerial vehicle navigation in varying magnetic environments. Conventional preflight calibration is often insufficient when magnetic distortions change due to powered equipment, current-carrying wiring, or nearby ferromagnetic objects, leading to heading errors and reduced navigation robustness. To overcome this limitation, we propose an adaptive algorithm that combines an initial static magnetometer calibration based on ellipsoid fitting with dynamic refinement using an Extended Kalman Filter. The filter estimates and updates calibration parameters in real time, allowing the magnetic measurements to be continuously corrected and used for stable onboard heading computation. To maintain consistency between the prediction and correction stages, the prediction model is driven by the incremental platform rotation obtained from a Madgwick orientation filter. This leads to stable heading estimation when the magnetic environment changes during operation and reduces sensitivity to short-term distortions. Experimental validation on an embedded platform with a controlled disturbance source confirms the robustness of the proposed approach to magnetic-environment variations and demonstrates its suitability for real-time onboard navigation systems.

title: Autonomous Aerial Mapping of Structural Assets through Multi-View Fusion for Digital Twin Refinement
authors: Murat Bakirci
abstract: Digital twins hold great promise for industrial monitoring, yet their usefulness depends on how closely and how consistently they reflect the evolving physical environment. This paper introduces a UAV-assisted digital twin framework that integrates real-time perception, dense 3D reconstruction, and dynamic semantic–geometric updating to maintain accurate models of solar farms, pipeline corridors, and factory rooftops. The proposed system reduces end-to-end latency by over 70% and produces far more stable reconstructions across repeated inspections. Experiments show strong semantic alignment (F1 scores up to 0.91, mAP<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">50</sup> values reaching 0.95) and low spatial deviation, with pipeline segments achieving alignment errors below 2 cm. Temporal analysis further demonstrates that automated updates suppress long-term divergence: while manual workflows accumulate substantial drift over six cycles, the UAV-assisted twin remains tightly grounded, with significantly lower positional and semantic variation. Displacement fields and processing latency complement these findings, illustrating where the system maintains consistency and where environmental complexity introduces challenges.

title: Cloud and High-Performance Computing for Smart Manufacturing
authors: Vadim Barkalov, Daniel Ena, Irina Matytsina
abstract: As industrial enterprises transition to smart manufacturing, the role of cloud and high-performance computing is increasing as a foundation for processing and analyzing operational data in real time. In this paradigm, warehouse logistics is viewed as a distributed computing subsystem that generates significant flows of heterogeneous data and places increased demands on the scalability and resilience of information solutions. This article examines a cloud-based intelligent warehouse management system, implemented using a microservice architecture and designed for the analytical processing of warehouse data under variable computing loads. An approach to organizing a distributed computing circuit is described, in which accounting data is supplemented with the results of statistical and predictive calculations without disrupting the continuity of operational processes. Particular attention is paid to mechanisms for horizontal scaling of analytical services, the distribution of computing tasks, and ensuring system resilience as data volumes grow. It is shown that the use of cloud infrastructure allows for the isolation of computationally intensive analytical modules from critical accounting services, ensuring stable system operation and the possibility of gradual expansion of functionality. The presented approach demonstrates the practical applicability of cloud computing for improving the efficiency of warehouse process management within a smart manufacturing environment.

title: Software System for Monitoring and Forecasting Passenger Traffic Based on Video Detection of City Streets
authors: N. N. Belianin, V.S. Moshkin
abstract: The article presents a description of a developed software system for monitoring and forecasting passenger flow in an urban street environment, based on computer vision and machine learning methods and integrated with video surveillance cameras. The system utilizes video streams that are processed by a context-aware detector consisting of a set of specialized deep learning models for the detection of potential passengers. Unlike the traditional approach that relies on a single universal detection model, this work proposes a meta-algorithm for model selection based on a decision tree that takes into account external observation conditions, including season, time of day, and weather conditions. To improve detection robustness, preliminary preprocessing of video data is applied, including contrast enhancement and object silhouette extraction.

title: Rekeying Procedure for Side-Channel Resilient NIST SP 800-90A DRBG Algorithms
authors: Murad Efendiev, Sviataslau Redzkin
abstract: We expose a side-channel vulnerability in NIST SP 800-90A DRBG algorithms caused by static internal key reuse. Our countermeasure dynamically refreshes the operational key each iteration via hashing, effectively decoupling side-channel leakage from the master secret. An implementation in Crypto++ shows a 28–39% performance overhead while raising the attack barrier from efficient multi-trace DPA to a cryptographically hard single-trace problem, offering a practical path to leakage-resilient standards-compliant DRBG algorithms. The performance study involved using the Windows API's QueryPerformanceCounter method to measure the CPU time consumed by the original DRBG algorithm's pseudorandom number generation, as well as by the DRBG algorithm implementing the rekeying procedure.

title: Real-Time Semantic Modeling of Pedestrian Zones Using a Modified U-Net for Autonomous Navigation
authors: Kirill D. Volkov, Pavel A. Panilov, Tatiana Y. Tsibizova
abstract: We present a novel method for the automatic real-time analysis and semantic modeling of pedestrian zones, designed to enhance spatial orientation and autonomous navigation systems in complex urban environments. The proposed approach integrates an optimized image processing pipeline comprising three key stages: semantic segmentation using a modified U-Net convolutional neural network, iterative post-processing for noise reduction and boundary refinement, and the generation of semantic embeddings for key scene points.The modified U-Net architecture incorporates encoder-decoder pathways with skip connections, batch normalization, and dropout regularization, enabling precise pixel-level classification of critical scene elements such as sidewalks, crosswalks, obstacles, and other navigable zones. Post-processing combines Gaussian and median filtering with edge detection and Hough Transform-based line extraction to produce smooth, continuous pedestrian-area boundaries. A distinguishing feature of this method is the generation of high-dimensional semantic embeddings for intersection points and other key features. These embeddings allow for the establishment of robust correspondences between different views, enabling accurate 3D reconstruction, alignment of multiple images, and the creation of semantically enriched spatial maps. Performance evaluation demonstrates that the approach maintains high accuracy and efficiency under dynamic urban conditions, including varying illumination and dense pedestrian traffic. The proposed framework provides a comprehensive solution for real-time autonomous navigation, robotic path planning, and augmented reality applications, combining both geometric and semantic understanding of pedestrian spaces. Its modular design facilitates integration into existing navigation pipelines and represents a significant step toward fully intelligent urban mobility systems.

title: Neural Network Techniques for Radio Signal Prediction in 5G Intelligent Communication Systems
authors: Behruz Saidov, Daler Anvarzoda, Azamjon Davlatov
abstract: This study explores the potential of artificial neural networks for forecasting radio signal characteristics within intelligent 5G wireless systems, specifically focusing on varying spectral densities. We modeled and analyzed the performance of several key architectures—including ADALINE, Focused Linear Network (FLN), and Nonlinear AutoRegressive network with eXogenous inputs (NARX)—utilizing the MATLAB Neural Network Toolbox. To assess the models' effectiveness, we conducted both single-step and multi-step predictions, evaluating them against standard error metrics such as Mean Square Error (MSE), Mean Absolute Error (MAE), and Mean Percentage Error (MPE). Our analysis indicates that the accuracy and stability of the predictions are heavily dependent on the signal's spectral complexity and the specific training parameters chosen. It is shown that the Focused Linear Network provides an optimal trade-off between training speed and prediction accuracy, while the ADALINE model exhibits higher robustness when operating within a narrow frequency band. The obtained results confirm the feasibility of applying neural network-based prediction methods in 5G wireless systems to improve adaptive resource allocation, reduce transmission latency, and enhance the reliability of radio channels under dynamically changing propagation conditions.

title: Development of an Architecture for an Intelligent Plant Logistics Management System Based on the Integration of ERP Systems, MES, and Industrial Robots in the Context of Industry 4.0/5.0
authors: Artur A. Fomenko, Pavel A. Panilov, Tatiana Y. Tsibizova
abstract: This article presents the concept and architecture of a hybrid intelligent system for plant logistics management, designed in accordance with the Industry 4.0 and Industry 5.0 paradigms. The primary challenge addressed is bridging the gap between strategic planning in ERP/MES systems and operational execution at the level of production assets. The proposed solution features a modular, localized AI core deployed on-premises, providing digital sovereignty, low latency, and secure closed-loop data processing. The system’s foundation is an AI Engine that converts high-level business tasks into executable equipment commands through natural language interfaces, while modular subcomponents—Planner, Dispatcher, and Real-Time Analyzer—enable adaptive decision-making and continuous feedback. An Integration Layer ensures connectivity with industrial robots, AGVs/AMRs, cobots, IoT sensors, and production equipment via standardized interfaces and protocols. The end-to-end functional workflow illustrates the continuous cycle of task generation, semantic planning, command dispatching, execution, and real-time feedback. The architecture follows a service-oriented approach, ensuring loose coupling of modules and resilience to technological obsolescence by enabling the replacement of AI models without redesigning the system. Additionally, the design anticipates multi-agent extensions, where specialized agents manage distinct plant segments, improving scalability, decentralization, and fault tolerance. Human–AI balance is achieved through collaborative robots, augmented reality, and natural language interfaces, supporting operator oversight and cognitive load reduction. Overall, the proposed system demonstrates adaptability, operational efficiency, and long-term relevance, providing a methodological and technological foundation for secure, human-centric, and self-optimizing production ecosystems aligned with both Industry 4.0 and 5.0.

title: A Method for Detecting Impurities in Grain Products Using Convolutional Neural Networks and Spectroscopy
authors: Pavel Titilin, Alena Rusak
abstract: The study is devoted to the problem of automatic recognition of weed impurities in wheat. A method based on the joint analysis of spectroscopic data in the visible and near-infrared ranges using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) has been developed and implemented as software. A balanced dataset of 320,000 photographs has been created, including samples of wheat and three types of weed seeds. The proposed two-stage image processing scheme combines computer vision algorithms for primary filtering and a CNN model for accurate classification. The results obtained confirm the effectiveness of the combined approach to building automatic systems for objective and rapid grain quality control.

title: MEF-Net: Mamba-Based Multi-Domain Edge Feature Fusion Network for City Infrastructure Detection in Remote Sensing Images
authors: Pengyuan Sun, Lina Yang, Lianhui Liang, Haoyan Yang, Dongchang Liu, Xichun Li
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Timely detection of urban infrastructure is crucial for city planning, routine maintenance, and disaster response. Remote sensing imagery provides large-scale coverage, enabling monitoring of key facilities such as buildings. However, arbitrary orientations, dense distributions, small sizes, and blurred boundaries of targets in remote sensing images pose significant challenges to accurate detection. To address these issues, we propose a Multi-domain Edge Feature Fusion Network (MEF-Net), which integrates Mamba and attention-guided receptive field mechanisms to enhance edge perception and multi-scale feature fusion. Specifically, the Mamba-Based Multi-Scale Edge Fusion and Selection Block (Mamba-MEFS Block) fuses multi-domain feature information, including spatial, frequency, channel, and multi-scale features, and utilizes Mamba to effectively fuse critical cues, alleviating issues related to boundary ambiguity and scale variation. The Attention-guided Receptive Field Interchange (AG-RFI) Block adaptively enlarges the receptive field through attention mechanisms, incorporating spatial and channel information to improve the perception of small and oriented targets. The Gated Mamba Shuffle Fusion (GMSF-Block) employs Mamba with a gated mechanism to suppress irrelevant regions and enhance feature selection and fusion. The Receptive Field Expansion-based Adaptive Multi-task Detection Head (RFE-AMD) improves global feature capture while preserving local details via adaptive gated decomposition and group normalization. RFE-AMD supports horizontal and rotated object detection, and instance segmentation for comprehensive infrastructure analysis. Extensive experiments are conducted on four public datasets, compared to 46 detection and instance segmentation models. The results show that MEF-Net achieves state-of-the-art mAP across all tasks, demonstrating superior performance in complex remote sensing scenarios while balancing parameters and inference speed.

title: Observer-Based Optimal Braking Force Distribution for Urban Rail Vehicles With Axle-Controlled Electro-Pneumatic Brake System
authors: Rui Zhang, Peidong Zhu, Lingshuang Kong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Precise braking force distribution is essential for the safety and efficiency of urban rail vehicles equipped with axle-controlled electro-pneumatic brake systems. However, conventional distribution strategies predominantly rely on static or empirical adhesion estimation, leading to suboptimal performance under time-varying track conditions and longitudinal load transfers. To address these issues, this paper proposes a dynamic distribution strategy based on real-time adhesion estimation. First, a longitudinal vehicle dynamics model incorporating dynamic axle load transfer and the LuGre friction formulation is established. An extended state observer is then designed to reconstruct the wheel-rail contact states and estimate the maximum available adhesion coefficient in real time. Subsequently, the braking force distribution is formulated as a constrained multi-objective optimization problem. An adaptive weight adjustment mechanism is also employed to dynamically coordinate total braking force tracking and wheel slide prevention. The proposed method is validated through hardware-in-the-loop experiments. Compared with conventional load-based and empirical adhesion estimation-based distribution methods, the proposed strategy significantly improves adhesion utilization and reduces braking distance under degraded adhesion scenarios.

title: Identification and Localization for Inter-Turn Short Circuit Fault of PMSMs Using α-β Plane Voltage and Current in FOC
authors: Haoyue Tang, Yulong Chai, Zhicong Dong, Guoqiang Han, He Cheng, Weili Li
abstract: With the increasing power and torque density of the permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM), the windings will experience higher electrical and thermal stresses, which may induce the inter-turn short circuit fault (ISCF). This paper proposes a diagnosis method for the ISCF of the PMSM using α-β plane variables in Field-Oriented Control (FOC). The mathematical model of the PMSM with ISCFs in the α-β plane is derived, and the usable fault features existing in the analytical expressions are analyzed. The second-order generalized integrator (SOGI) is utilized to construct the current residual that includes the short-circuit ratio and short-circuit current for identifying the ISCF. The polarity characteristic presents in the α-β plane voltage with different fault phases are explored. The fault factor for fault location is defined through the combination and logical operation of the characteristic variable. The processed fault factor is the DC quantity that is highly sensitive to the ISCF and is independent of motor parameters. The advantage of the proposed method is that the fault factor is derived from the analytical expression of the voltage and current inherently in the α-β plane, avoiding the iterative calculation, additional hardware, signal injection, and the establishment of specialized models for extracting fault characteristics. The experiment result demonstrates that the proposed method can identify and locate the ISCF, and has the robustness to sudden changes in operating conditions and parameter uncertainties.

title: A Hybrid RIS-assisted Broadcasting Scheme: Max-Min SNR Optimization and Prototype Implementation
authors: Zihang Shen, Hongyuan Li, Han Qing Yang, Weicong Chen, Wankai Tang, Jun Yan Dai, Qiang Cheng, Shi Jin, Tie Jun Cui
abstract: Broadcasting systems often suffer from coverage limitation and uneven service quality, particularly in non-line-of- sight (NLOS) environments. Reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) has emerged as a promising technology capable of reshaping electromagnetic propagation paths to enhance signal coverage in complex wireless environments. However, the inability for RIS to directly acquire channel state information (CSI) restricts its adaptability. In this paper, we propose a novel hybrid RIS (HRIS)-assisted broadcasting scheme that maximizes the minimum signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of users, where HRIS enables simultaneous signal reflection and real-time CSI acquisition. We formulate the problem as the maximization of the worst-case SNR of users by optimizing the HRIS phase configuration based on directly acquired CSI. A gradient-based optimization algorithm is developed to iteratively update the phase matrix of HRIS while preserving the fairness among users in broadcasting system. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme achieves considerable improvements in SNR and sum rate of all users. Moreover, a prototype system is implemented with an 8×8 HRIS array. The measurement results show good consistency with the simulation results. The experimental validation further confirms the effectiveness of the proposed scheme, achieving up to 16.47 dB performance enhancement.

title: BOLT-PM: An Adaptive Bayesian Optimization Framework for Latency-Sensitive Task and Power Management in AIoT Devices
authors: Biao Hu, Chenyu Cai, Xincheng Yang, Mingguo Zhao
abstract: The integration of AI tasks into Artificial Intelligence of Things (AIoT) devices has gained significant attention for its ability to reduce transmission latency and enhance data privacy compared to traditional cloud-based solutions. However, heterogeneous AIoT platforms face the challenge of balancing stringent latency constraints with energy efficiency under dynamic AI workloads. To address this issue, we propose BOLT-PM, a Bayesian Optimization framework for Latency-sensitive Task and Power Management, which dynamically allocates computing resources and optimizes power consumption in AIoT devices. We design a multi-branch neural network to accurately predict task execution times under varying resource constraints, enabling precise and adaptive performance estimation. We further enhance the Bayesian optimization process by embedding a variational autoencoder (VAE) to construct a smooth latent representation of the search space, thereby accelerating convergence and improving optimization stability. We implement BOLT-PM as a lightweight, platform-agnostic runtime framework that continuously adapts to workload fluctuations, maintaining latency guarantees while minimizing energy consumption. We evaluate BOLT-PM on several commercial AIoT boards, including RK3588, Jetson TX2, and Raspberry Pi, and compare it against classical heuristic methods and state-of-the-art approaches. Experimental results show that BOLT-PM achieves substantial energy savings while ensuring low-latency AI task execution, demonstrating its effectiveness as a robust and energy-efficient solution for power-aware AIoT applications.

title: Sanitizer: Blazing-fast, Private and Robust Federated Learning
authors: Hanxiao Chen, Hongwei Li, Meng Hao, Jia Hu, Hao Ren, Haomiao Yang, Tianwei Zhang, Guowen Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: Recently, private and robust federated learning (FL) schemes have been proposed to address privacy inference and Byzantine attacks simultaneously. However, existing schemes are inefficient in private and robust aggregation protocols due to the employment of heavy cryptographic techniques. To approach the above problem, we propose Sanitizer, an efficient, private, and robust FL framework. Specifically, we first design a Byzantine-robust defense for communication-efficient sign-based FL. We further propose a customized private and robust aggregation scheme built on our Byzantine-robust defense for FL. The core of our construction is two new efficient protocols, i.e., high-dimensional boolean summation and weighted boolean majority vote, which serve as the main building blocks of Sanitizer. Extensive evaluations on real-world datasets demonstrate that Sanitizer is blazing fast, achieving 19 ∼ 23× less runtime compared to the state-of-the-art. Meanwhile, Sanitizer achieves the same accuracy as the plaintext and superior Byzantine robustness against various classic attacks.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A

title: SDN-driven Dynamic Traffic Management for ISPs Serving Inter-cloud Communication
authors: Zbigniew Duliński, Rafał Stankiewicz, Grzegorz Rzym, Piotr Wydrych
abstract: This article presents an advanced framework for Dynamic Traffic Management (DTM), significantly extending our prior work on minimizing inter-domain traffic costs. The main extension enables cost optimization among multiple sending and multiple receiving domains. The mechanism works for volume and 95th percentile tariffs. The main paradigm of DTM operation remains unchanged: DTM reacts dynamically on traffic changes on inter-domain links shifting some part of traffic (manageable traffic) from one link to another. This mechanism is based on flow load-balancing, it is centrally controlled by SDN controllers. It requires cooperation between operators receiving and generating traffic. Communication between domain operators is realized by SDN controllers (inter-domain SDN). DTM has been applied to inter-cloud communication for DCs located in different ISP domains. The experiments conducted in the hardware testbed, involving numerous sending and receiving sites, demonstrate that DTM can save up to 36% of the transfer cost.

title: Transformative Learning in the Gen-AI Landscape: Challenges and Capacities for Organizational Evolution_supp1-3683981.docx
authors: Umair Tanveer
abstract: Generative artificial intelligence (Gen-AI) marks a transformative phase for organizations, creating both major opportunities and challenges. Anchored in organisational learning theory, this study examines how organizations adopt Gen-AI by unpacking the related challenges, learning processes, and required capabilities. Using a multiple-case study design with semistructured interviews across four pioneering organizations, the research highlights the importance of adaptability, rapid decision-making, risk tolerance, and a strong learning culture in shaping organizational learning during Gen-AI adoption. The findings show that Gen-AI creates distinctive learning demands compared with earlier technologies. Its cognitive reasoning and adaptive functionalities require organizations to engage in iterative, continuous learning to integrate these tools into both strategic and operational systems. The study offers practical guidance for organizations seeking to overcome adoption challenges, strengthen learning cultures, and realize Gen-AI's transformative potential. It also contributes to the broader Gen-AI discourse by providing a theoretically grounded and empirically supported roadmap for managing AI integration, including strategies such as iterative feedback loops, risk-tolerant decision-making, and embedding ethical considerations in AI governance.

title: Behavioral Conflict Modeling for AI-Enabled Intelligent Manufacturing: A Risk-Attitude Extension of Graph Model for Conflict Resolution_supp1-3687286.pdf
authors: Peide Liu
abstract: Intelligent transformation in manufacturing enterprises (MEIT) aims to reduce costs, improve efficiency and quality, and enhance market competitiveness. However, conflicts arise among stakeholders in the artificial intelligence-enhanced MEIT (AI+MEIT) ecosystem because of divergent interests, unequal constraints, and heterogeneous responses to transformation-related risks and uncertainties. This study examines how heterogeneous risk attitudes can be incorporated into the Graph Model for Conflict Resolution (GMCR) for AI+MEIT conflict analysis. First, based on multi-criteria information, an aspiration-driven mechanism is developed to characterize stakeholders' perceived gains and losses across alternative states and to identify their risk attitudes from the decision environment. Second, risk-sensitive adjustment factors are introduced to capture differences in perceived gains and losses under heterogeneous risk attitudes. Third, a mixed-attitude equilibrium concept is proposed to support stability analysis under behavioral diversity. Finally, the improved GMCR framework is illustrated through an application example and sensitivity-based robustness analysis, showing how heterogeneous risk attitudes reshape conflict evolution paths and equilibrium patterns. The proposed framework extends GMCR in a risk sensitive direction and provides structured support for conflict analysis and decision-making in AI+MEIT.

title: From Knowledge Graphs to Decision Boundaries: Separable Embeddings for Open-Set Specific Emitter Identification
authors: Lu Sun, Rui Xue, Haoran Zha, Shunyao Cao, Ruofei Li, Guan Gui, Yun Lin
abstract: Specific emitter identification (SEI) underpins identity authentication and security management in the electromagnetic domain. However, most prior work adopts a closed-set assumption and therefore cannot handle the continual appearance of unknown emitters. Open-set SEI (OS-SEI) aims to recognize known classes while reliably rejecting unknown ones. Yet, many approaches rely on single-feature cues and lack mechanisms that jointly enforce intra-class compactness and inter-class separation, resulting in brittle decision boundaries. To address this gap, we propose a knowledge-graph–driven OS-SEI method. We first construct a signal knowledge graph that explicitly encodes relations among heterogeneous features. Building on this, we introduce a Local–Global Knowledge Graph Network (LG-KGN) that (i) aggregates semantically related nodes within domain-specific subgraphs via attention and (ii) preserves cross-domain structure using a relational GNN, producing discriminative, separable embeddings. Finally, metric-learning losses enhance intra-class compactness and inter-class separation, while a distance-based open-set score with a calibrated threshold improves the rejection of unknown samples. Experiments conducted on a real ADS-B dataset consistently demonstrate superior OS-SEI performance compared to competitive baselines.

title: Low-frequency Acoustic Temperature Monitoring for Coal Powder Storage in Reverberant and Noisy Environment
authors: Jingyu Gao, Yong Bao, Feng Dong, Chao Tan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Stored pulverized coal is prone to oxidation, leading to internal temperature rise and increased risks of spontaneous combustion. Therefore, accurate monitoring of the internal temperature is of critical importance. Acoustic thermometry is regarded as a promising technique for tracking internal temperature changes within stored pulverized coal, owing to its high penetration ability, non-invasive nature, rapid response and low equipment cost. Nonetheless, the effectiveness of its penetration hinges on employing low-frequency sound waves, which may suffer from measurement inaccuracy due to reverberation interference and ambient noise, especially for temperature monitoring in enclosed spaces. To address this issue, this study developed a deep learning dereverberation and parameter extraction algorithm using CNN-Transformer. Experimental results demonstrate that the method effectively suppresses reverberation in enclosed spaces and enables high-precision recovery of temperature-related acoustic parameters. These improvements are expected to support subsequent acoustic tomography, in which accurate parameter estimation is essential for reconstructing the temperature distribution of stored pulverized coal.

title: A computer vision-aided lightweight attention-augmented approach for nondestructive steel wire rope defect detection
authors: Ziwan Shen, Yong Lv, Rui Yuan, Hongan Wu, Wen Bo, Zhiwen Lu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Steel wire rope (SWR), as critical load-bearing components in engineering, require precise and real-time nondestructive testing of surface defects to ensure industrial safety. However, in practical engineering environments, machine vision-based methods face severe challenges such as complex backgrounds, varying lighting conditions, and low-contrast defect features, making it difficult to achieve both high accuracy and high real-time performance. To address this, this paper proposes a computer vision-aided lightweight attention-augmented approach for nondestructive steel wire rope defect detection. The core of the work is a comprehensive vision measurement system, featuring a custom multi-sensor synchronized device for full-coverage imaging and a standardized preprocessing pipeline to enhance defect features. Algorithmically, this paper designs the YOLO-CPC framework, which incorporates lightweight PartialConv-based modules (C3k2-PC1, C3k2-PC2) for efficient feature encoding and a channel-spatial attention block (CASAB) with SiLU activation for improved perception of low-contrast defects. Validated on a factory-collected dataset, the method achieves a state-of-the-art mAP@0.5 of 95.7% with only 5.7 GFLOPs. The method successfully balances computational efficiency with high measurement accuracy, presenting an integrated solution for the nondestructive inspection and quantitative assessment of surface defects in SWR.

title: Shared-Aperture Multi-Band Base Station Antennas
authors: Morteza Moradi, Younes Ra’di
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This paper investigates the evolution of shared-aperture antenna designs from dual-band to triple-band configurations for sub-6 GHz base station applications. Building on dual-band foundational strategies—side-by-side, embedded, stacked, cavity-backed, and interleaved—we explore their limitations when extended to support three distinct frequency bands. Through an in-depth review of recent literature, we identify practical constraints and categorize feasible triple-band architectures into hybrid schemes combining stacked layers, interleaved radiators, and spatial decoupling techniques. Key enabling methods include frequency-selective surfaces (FSS), parasitic decoupling, and current suppression structures. Our analysis highlights the shift toward configurations that balance aperture compactness, inter-band isolation, and radiation performance. The primary goal of this work is to serve as a comprehensive resource and forward-looking guide for scientists and engineers developing the next generations of telecommunication systems, helping them navigate the emerging challenges of multi-band integration in these rapidly evolving technologies.

title: Adaptive Multipath-Based SLAM for Distributed MIMO Systems
authors: Xuhong Li, Benjamin J. B. Deutschmann, Erik Leitinger, Florian Meyer
abstract: Localizing users and mapping the environment using radio signals is a key task in emerging applications such as reliable low-latency communications, location-aware security, and safety-critical navigation. Recently introduced multipath-based simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) methods can jointly localize a mobile agent and map reflective surfaces in radio frequency (RF) environments. Most existing approaches assume that map features and their corresponding RF propagation paths are statistically independent, conditioned on the state of the mobile agent. This assumption neglects inherent dependencies that arise when a single reflective surface contributes to multiple propagation paths or when an agent communicates with multiple base stations. Existing approaches that aim to fuse information across propagation paths are further limited by their inability to perform ray tracing in RF environments with nonconvex geometries. In this paper, we propose a Bayesian multipath-based SLAM method for distributed multiple-input-multiple-output (MIMO) systems that addresses these limitations. In particular, we exploit amplitude statistics to establish adaptive, time-varying detection probabilities. Based on the resulting “soft” ray-tracing strategy, the proposed method can fuse information across propagation paths in RF environments with nonconvex geometries. A Bayesian estimation framework for the joint estimation of map features and agent state is developed by applying the message passing rules of the sum-product algorithm (SPA) to a factor graph representation of the proposed statistical model. We further introduce a new initialization procedure for reflective surfaces that enables the introduction of new surface states even when measurements arise solely from double-bounce paths. The proposed method is validated using both synthetic and real RF measurements obtained in challenging scenarios with nonconvex geometries and obstructed line-of-sight conditions. The results demonstrate that it provides accurate localization and mapping performance and approaches the posterior Cramér–Rao lower bound.

title: Power fuzzy clustering: flexible distance metrics and inclusion of covariates
authors: Phuc T. Nguyen, Cristina Tortora, Antonio Punzo
abstract: Clustering is a fundamental task in statistics and machine learning aimed at identifying homogeneous groups within data. Among the available approaches, fuzzy clustering (FC) is particularly suited to poorly separated boundaries, as it allows partial memberships and captures uncertainty and overlapping structures. We introduce Power Fuzzy Clustering (PFC), a general framework that unifies several FC methods, including fuzzy K-means and probabilistic distance clustering. By complementing the classical fuzzifier with a power parameter on distances, PFC enhances model flexibility while retaining interpretability. To accommodate clusters with different shapes and scales, we incorporate a volume parameter and alternative distance metrics (e.g., Mahalanobis and Minkowski). We further propose the Power Fuzzy Cluster-wise Regression (PFCR) model, a generalization of PFC that reduces to it when only intercepts are included. Simulation studies investigate the impact of the power and fuzzification parameters, and a real-world application illustrates the method's practical relevance.

title: An Analytical Framework for Modeling Inhomogeneities in Open-Ended Coaxial Probe Measurements
authors: Rotem Gal-Katzir, Emily Porter, Yarden Mazor
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: Open-ended coaxial probes (OECP) are commonly used for dielectric characterization across the RF/Microwave frequency range. Measuring inhomogeneous materials using OECPs is a significant challenge due to the complexity of modeling the fields within the sample. In this letter, we present an experimental verification of a novel analytical model for the extraction of the physical parameters of a small scatterer from OECP reflection measurements. Using our analytical framework, we demonstrate that with two different probes, two key parameters (scatterer radius and depth) can be accurately estimated (2%−20% relative error), correlating the properties of an inhomogeneous material with a simple OECP measurement for the first time

title: Online Trajectory Planning for Hypersonic Glide Vehicle under Multiple No-Fly Zones: An Attention Mechanism-based BiGRU Framework
authors: Jiarui Sun, Chuanjiang Li, Guangtao Ran, Bohui Wang, Yanning Guo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: In trajectory planning for hypersonic glide vehicles (HGVs) under multiple no-fly-zone constraints, existing methods struggle to achieve effective avoidance due to limitations in the vehicle's maneuverability. Additionally, the computational burden of iterative planning hinders their applicability for online implementation. To address the avoidance of dense no-fly zones, a waypoint generation method based on no-fly zone tangents is designed to produce feasible paths. Subsequently, the threat-aware spatial ivy algorithm (TAS-IVYA) is proposed for offline feasible path decision making. Then, to mitigate computational delays while ensuring prediction accuracy, a BiGRU network incorporating a dual attention mechanism (2ATT-BiGRU) is developed for online control decision-making. Numerical simulations validate the reliability of the proposed method for online no-fly zone avoidance and the accuracy of bank angle prediction.

title: Matrix Completion-Based Learning of Probabilistic Power Flow Using a Meta-Graph Generative Autoencoder Network
authors: Garima Prashal, Parasuraman Sumathi, Narayana Prasad Padhy
abstract: Probabilistic power flow (PF) analysis is critical for secure operation of distribution networks under uncertainty from distributed energy resources. However, low observability in secondary networks and frequent topology reconfigurations complicate PF estimation and demand large sample sizes. This paper proposes a meta-graph generative autoencoder (Meta-GGAE) to learn probabilistic PF distributions under sparse sensing. Meta-GGAE employs node-level matrix completion within a bilevel meta-learning framework to infer unmeasured states, where physics-guided regularizers are adaptively learned using a graph attention network. A variational generative encoder embeds system physics to model local dependencies, while a conditional variational decoder captures probabilistic PF outputs by integrating latent variables with attention-based features. The proposed framework reconstructs missing measurements, generalizes to unseen topologies, and enables robust PF estimation under low observability. Simulations on IEEE test systems demonstrate improved accuracy and physical consistency under N-1 contingencies and uncertain injections compared with baseline methods.

title: Optimal Control of Markov Decision Processes for Efficiency with Linear Temporal Logic Tasks
authors: Yu Chen, Xunyuan Yin, Shaoyuan Li, Xiang Yin
abstract: We revisit the problem of optimal control synthesis for Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) with both qualitative and quantitative objectives. The qualitative task is defined by a Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) formula, while the quantitative metric is captured by the notion of efficiency, which is the ratio between rewards and costs. The overall objective is to synthesize a control policy that not only ensures the LTL task is satisfied with probability one but also maximizes efficiency. We present an effective approach for synthesizing a stationary control policy that achieves $\epsilon$-optimality by integrating state classifications of MDPs with perturbation analysis in a novel manner. Our results extend existing work on efficiency-optimal control synthesis for MDPs by incorporating qualitative LTL tasks. Case studies in robot task planning are provided to illustrate the proposed algorithm.

title: Hybrid Spectral - Gradient Saliency Pruning: A combination of multiple filter scoring criteria
authors: Thanh-Thien Nguyen, Hoang-Loc Tran, Vo-Chi-Dung Nguyen, Viet-An Nguyen, Duc-Lung Vu
abstract: Deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have achieved remarkable performance in visual recognition but remain computationally expensive for deployment on embedded or edge devices. This paper introduces Hybrid SpectralGradient Saliency Pruning (HSGSP), a structured pruning framework that unifies spectral analysis and data-driven gradient saliency to achieve efficient CNN compression. The proposed method incorporates a lightweight Frequency Relevance Network (FRN) that learns to estimate the spectral importance of convolutional filters through frequency-band energy ratios, enabling fast, task-driven scoring. A hybrid saliency metric fuses the FRNs spectral relevance with gradient-based Taylor sensitivity, ensuring filters are preserved only when important both spectrally and task-wise. An adaptive iterative schedule dynamically adjusts pruning intensity based on validation feedback, preventing over-pruning and maintaining stability. Experiments on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 using VGG-16BN demonstrate up to 90% parameter reduction with negligible accuracy loss, outperforming recent structured pruning methods. Furthermore, on a Raspberry Pi 5, our pruned model delivers a 3.4x inference speedup while slightly improving accuracy, and when permitting only a 1% accuracy trade-off, the speedup increases dramatically to 7.5x. The results confirm that combining spectral cues with gradient saliency offers a robust and interpretable path toward efficient CNN deployment. The official implementation code of our method is available at https://github.com/locth/HSGSP.git.

title: Radio Frequency Heating: Assessing its Viability for Composite Tape Processing Line
authors: Hanisa Hasrin, Maksim Kuznetcov, Symon Podilchak, Iram Shahzadi, Alex T. Don, Amer Syed, Hatice Sas, Conchúr Ó Brádaigh, Colin Robert
abstract: This paper assesses the potential of radio frequency (RF) heating as a non-contact, low-power, and localised volumetric heating method for carbon fibre reinforced polymer (CFRP) composite tape processing, with a view toward integration into a larger-scale powder-epoxy tapeline. A stand-alone RF heating module was developed comprising an RF signal generator, a 100 W power amplifier, and a fringing field PCB antenna transducer enclosed within a custom-built Faraday cage. Impedance matchingwas evaluated through measurement of the S<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</sub> reflection coefficient using a vector network analyzer and validated with full-wave electromagnetic simulations. The integrated module exhibited multi-band resonance behaviour with reflection coefficients as low as −26 dB and narrow bandwidth matching in the 100 to 200 MHz and 500 to 550 MHz ranges. Frequency sweep experiments identified 112 MHz as the optimal heating frequency within the lower band. At this frequency, the system demonstrated a linear thermal response of approximately 1.46 ◦C/W, achieving measurable heating of carbon fibre tow under low input powers (up to 4 W) providing proof-of-concept demonstration of the system. Electric field strength (EFS) leakage measurements further showed non-linear scaling with input power and up to 98% field attenuation by the Faraday cage enclosure. However, operation at higher matched frequencies (e.g., 483 MHz and 520 MHz) resulted in negligible heating despite being impedance matched, accompanied by elevated EFS leakage levels exceeding 30 V/m at 5Winput power, indicating inefficient energy absorption.These results demonstrate that while impedance matching is necessary for efficient RF heating, it is not alone sufficient to guarantee effective material energy transfer. The study also highlights key system sensitivities to near-field loading conditions and identifies critical design considerations for achieving robust, safe, and energy-efficient RF heating suitable for continuous composite tape manufacturing.

title: A Federated Split Autoencoder for Privacy-Preserving Non-IID Graph Learning
authors: Kun Guo, Kui Gao, Qingqing Huang, Yuting Liang, Yilun Jin, Kai Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: Federated graph neural networks (Federated GNNs) have gained increasing attention in academia and industry owing to their ability to train graph learning models in a collaborative manner while complying with the growing stringency of privacy protection regulations. In practice, participants’ local graphs often contain overlapping vertices and links, embodying topological non-independence within non-independently and identically distributed (non-IID) graphs, thereby degrading the accuracy of federated GNNs. In this paper, we propose a federated split graph autoencoder (FSGAE) for privacy-preserving unsupervised learning on graphs characterized by topological non-independence. First, we design a framework based on federated and split learning, where the model training process is collaboratively executed by three distinct roles to protect each participant’s graph privacy. Second, FCECS guarantees zero accuracy loss by incorporating a multiparty collaborative computation mechanism to address overlapping vertices and links for accurate vertex embedding computation during forward propagation, as well as a split gradient computation strategy to guarantee precise model parameter updates during backward propagation. Experimental results on real-world networks demonstrate that FSGAE achieves a 13.4% improvement in accuracy.

title: AI-Driven Framework for Analyzing Visitor Patterns in Indoor Venues with Wireless Localization
authors: Jae-Woong Lee, Jangkyum Kim, Taeyeab Kim, Jeong-Wook Kim
abstract: Advances in indoor positioning technologies have enabled detailed collection and analysis of location data within built environments. However, most existing studies focus on population-level mobility in outdoor contexts, with limited attention to individual-level behaviors in indoor venues. This study proposes a analytical framework utilizing wireless localization technology to model visitor behavior and predict the number of visitors per sector by integrating diverse environmental data. A Feature Tokenizer Transformer-based model is employed to jointly process numerical and categorical variables and effectively learn complex nonlinear relationships. The framework addresses a post-Day-1 operational prediction task, where Day-1 visitor observations are combined with static environmental variables to predict cumulative sector-level visitor distribution across the full exhibition period. The proposed approach is validated using actual data collected from the Korea International Medical & Hospital Equipment Show, comprising 333,470 location records, 210 spatial nodes, and 25 environmental variables. Experimental results show that the proposed model outperforms the second-best model by 8% in terms of predictive accuracy. These results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed approach in accurately predicting visitor distribution in complex indoor environments and highlight its potential for facilitating optimization of spatial management.

title: TauGenNet: Plasma-Driven Tau PET Image Synthesis via Text-Guided 3D Diffusion Models
authors: Yuxin Gong, Se-in Jang, Wei Shao, Yi Su, Kuang Gong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Radiation and Plasma Medical Sciences
abstract: Accurate quantification of tau pathology via tau positron emission tomography (PET) scan is crucial for diagnosing and monitoring Alzheimer’s disease (AD). However, the high cost and limited availability of tau PET restrict its widespread use. In contrast, structural magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and plasma-based biomarkers provide non-invasive and widely available complementary information related to brain anatomy and disease progression. In this work, we propose a text-guided 3D diffusion model for 3D tau PET image synthesis, leveraging multimodal conditions from both structural MRI and plasma measurement. Specifically, the textual prompt is from the plasma p-tau217 measurement, which is a key indicator of AD progression, while MRI provides anatomical structure constraints. The proposed framework is trained and evaluated using clinical AV1451 tau PET data from the Alzheimer’s Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) database. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach can generate realistic, clinically meaningful 3D tau PET across a range of disease stages. The proposed framework can help perform tau PET data augmentation under different settings, provide a non-invasive, cost-effective alternative for visualizing tau pathology, and support the simulation of disease progression under varying plasma biomarker levels and cognitive conditions.

title: A MRC-Srformer network enhanced by adjustable time-frequency domain feature extraction for heart sound detection
authors: Jinpo Wang, Xiaohan Ye, Zijian Qiao, Huabo He
abstract: Cardiovascular diseases pose a significant threat to human health and life. Inexperienced doctors often encounter challenges in auscultation diagnosis, which may result in misjudgments. The advent of computer-aided auscultation diagnosis technology has offered a powerful solution to address this issue. This study innovatively proposes an adjustable time-frequency domain feature extraction enhanced MRC-Srformer Network (MSNet) method to automatically detect heart sounds. By making delicate adjustments to the processing method in the time-frequency domain, this method can effectively extract heart sound features. Specifically, through accurately searching for the balance point where the energy is most concentrated, a unique intermediate domain between the time domain and the frequency domain has been successfully found, providing a new perspective and ideas for the analysis of heart sound features. In addition, the proposed method incorporates features from two distinct dimensions, integrating adjustable time-frequency domain feature extraction (ATDFE) with one-dimensional heart sound features. The one-dimensional architecture of this model is predominantly built upon spectral block (SB) modules and attention mechanisms. The two-dimensional architecture adopted by this method innovatively applies the residual convolution technique, which enables in-depth mining and extraction of the information of two-dimensional features. The method can efficiently and accurately integrate the information obtained at different stages, thereby significantly enhancing the model's comprehensive information processing ability and representation effect. Experiments conducted on two distinct datasets have demonstrated that the proposed method outperforms other models including transformer, efficientNet, cnn-bilstm, achieving accuracy rates of 96.24% and 94.48% respectively on the two datasets.
