title: Metallic Wideband Dual-Polarized 2-D Monopulse Array Antenna Based on Dual-Mode Composite Waveguides
authors: Yang Hu, Yunlong Lu, Qingchun You, Yujian Li, Lingguo Min, Yi Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This paper presents a wideband dual-polarized 2-D monopulse array antenna (MAA) based on dual-mode composite waveguides (DMCWs). The proposed antenna consists of an 8 × 8-element dual-polarized array and a dual-mode monopulse comparator. The monopulse comparator is constructed from proposing dual-mode 3-dB couplers, 90° phase shifters, and mode separators, all based on composite dual-mode structure of rectangular waveguide (RW) and coaxial line. The radiating part of the dual-polarized array includes square slots, cross-shaped slots, and new T-shaped orthomode transducers (OMTs), generating stable dual-polarized boresight radiation without grating lobes across the operating frequency band. To reduce the occupied size (meeting the scalability of the array), another type of compact DMCW based on double-ridge waveguide (DRW) and coaxial line is used to implements a compact wideband full-corporate feeding network. Benefiting from the DMCW-based feeding network and monopulse comparator, the whole MAA significantly reduces structural complexity and profile height. Experimental results show that the fabricated prototype has stable sum-beam radiation for both polarizations within the frequency range of 14 to 18 GHz. The null depth of the difference-beam is less than -36 dB, and the amplitude difference is less than 0.5 dB.

title: Reconfiguration-Driven Flexibility Extensions in Coupled Power-Transportation Networks With Uncertainties
authors: Xiaodong Yang, Hongquan Xu, Zihao Li, Wei Lou, Yue Yang, Da Xu, Lijian Ding, Jinyu Wen
abstract: Under high photovoltaic (PV) penetration, distribution networks (DNs) face insufficient operational flexibility across both spatial and temporal dimensions. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a reconfiguration-driven framework aimed at flexibility extensions for coupled power-transportation networks under uncertainty, which seeks to improve the operational flexibility of DNs. The framework leverages multi-reconfiguration-driven spatiotemporally controllable devices to enable multi-level reconfiguration of feeders, voltage source converters and modular transportable energy storage systems, thereby facilitating cross-node and cross-period energy coordination while significantly enhancing scheduling flexibility and PV utilization efficiency. To handle uncertainties from traffic congestion, load fluctuations, and PV generation, a dynamic-distance-based spatiotemporal decision-making method integrated with rolling optimization is developed to represent the time-varying spatial relationships of vehicles and battery modules during transportation and scheduling, while addressing future uncertainties and enhancing the adaptability of the scheduling strategy. Furthermore, modular battery availability screening and safety assurance model are incorporated to support dynamic identification and refined management of battery modules, preventing over-charging/over-discharging and state mismatches. Simulation studies on both a modified IEEE 33-node distribution network with a 14-node transportation network and a 10 kV, 220-node distribution network in a specific region of Anhui Province, China demonstrate that the proposed framework effectively extends DN flexibility, enhances PV utilization, and ensures reliable operation.

title: SIX: A Self-supervised Interactive Algorithm for Low-dose X-ray Fluoroscopy Image Denoising
authors: Mingzhi Wen, Tianling Lyu, Zhan Wu, Yan Xi, Pinzheng Zhang, Yang Chen, Yuanjing Feng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Low-dose X-ray fluoroscopy is essential in interventional procedures to reduce radiation exposure, yet image quality is inevitably degraded by predominantly Poisson noise, compromising diagnostic reliability. Existing denoising methods commonly rely on large annotated medical datasets, which are scarce in practice, and struggle to balance noise suppression with the preservation of fine structural details. To overcome the above-mentioned problems, we propose SIX, a zero-shot self-supervised interactive denoising framework for low-dose fluoroscopy. The framework integrates a physics-driven noise model with a deep image prior. It performs prior-based denoising via probabilistic modeling with an intuitive hyperparameter, allowing a flexible balance between noise suppression and structural fidelity according to specific diagnostic requirements. Experiments on an in-house dataset show that SIX outperforms various conventional and deep learning methods in PSNR and SSIM, as well as in clinically critical metrics including contrast-to-noise ratio and spatial resolution. With its flexibility and user-friendly design, SIX holds strong potential for deployment in clinical image-guided workflows.

title: KGTNet: Efficient Point Cloud Semantic Segmentation with Kernelized Graph Transformer
authors: Xiangfei Li, Feifan Bian, Zihao Pan, Junyi Hou, Lei Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Point cloud semantic segmentation is a fundamental task in 3D scene understanding, yet it remains challenging due to the irregular and sparse nature of point clouds and the high computational cost of global context modeling. To address these issues, we propose KGTNet, a novel deep learning architecture that integrates dynamic graph convolution for local geometric feature extraction and a linear-complexity kernelized attention mechanism for efficient global dependency modeling. Specifically, we introduce a kernel approximation technique using positive random features to approximate the softmax attention, thereby reducing its computational complexity. Furthermore, we design a dual-branch structure with a gated fusion module to effectively combine local and global features. To enhance robustness against non-uniform sampling and noise, we incorporate random projection based on the Johnson–Lindenstrauss lemma. Extensive experiments on ModelNet40, ShapeNet Part, and S3DIS datasets demonstrate that KGTNet achieves competitive accuracy in classification, part segmentation, and semantic segmentation tasks, while significantly improving computational efficiency and generalization capability. The source code and pre-trained model of KGTNet will be released on https://github.com/fei081100/KGTNet.

title: Polarization Array–Based Target Reconstruction Framework for Measurement in Complex Environments
authors: Xiankun Pu, Xin Wang, Xinjian Gao, Chongfeng Wei, Jun Gao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: This paper presents a measurement methodology for reconstructing the polarization properties of obscured targets using a multi-view polarization camera array. Vision-based measurement in complex environments is frequently compromised by foreground occlusions, which prevent direct observation of the target and introduce significant uncertainty. To address this fundamental challenge in optical metrology, we develop a Polarization Array–Based Target Reconstruction Framework (PATR) for measurement and inversion. The proposed framework integrates a custom polarization camera array instrument with a learning-based measurement inversion pipeline designed to fuse multi-view polarization data. The primary objective of this work is to develop a methodology that leverages this multi-view polarization dataset to perform quantitatively characterized reconstruction of physical measurands, including the geometric structure and the full polarization state of targets obscured by foreground occlusions at varying distances and densities. Extensive experimental evaluations under controlled and field occlusion scenarios demonstrate that the PATR framework effectively reconstructs target intensity and polarization measurands, including the full polarization state of obscured targets. The results show that the proposed method not only suppresses occlusion interference but also accurately reconstructs target polarization characteristics, thereby effectively enhancing measurement capability and target discriminability in conditions where conventional vision systems fail. This work advances polarization-based metrology for target measurement in occluded environments.

title: Nonlinear Granger Causality Analysis Using Neural Network Architectures for Sequential Data
authors: Diego Nicolás Chacón Wilches, Álvaro David Orjuela Cañón
abstract: This work presents an analysis of nonlinear Granger causality (NNGC) computation based on artificial neural networks (ANN) architectures. The study evaluates the impact of using computational intelligence models as ANN, and how the training parameters can modify the causality estimation. For this, the employing from three chaotic maps (Henon, Ikeda, and Tinkerbell) and one neuron-like map (Rulkov) in bivariate scenarios were implemented. Three architectures from the ANN were used such as multilayer perceptron in a mode of the nonlinear autoregressive models, long-short memory term, and gated recurrent unit architectures were used to compute the NNGC, applying a forecasting on sequential data techniques. Results demonstrated that NNGC is highly sensitive to neural network parameters, such as the number of neurons, lag length, and batch size, with an optimal configuration by varying across chaotic maps. Comparisons with classical Granger causality were tested, revealing that neural networks effectively discover nonlinear relationships missed by linear methods, particularly in the Henon and Rulkov maps.

title: Space Environment Simulation for a Hardware-in-the-Loop Test Facility
authors: Nikita Bykovskiy, Vladimir Sayenko, Roman Solovyev
abstract: Before launching spacecraft into space, comprehensive tests are conducted to verify the functionality of all its systems and components. One such test is functional testing, which is performed on a specialized hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) simulation testbench. A key aspect of these tests is the verification of the attitude determination and control systems (ADCS) of small satellites, which plays a crucial role in ensuring the satellite's pointing accuracy in orbit. To achieve high testing accuracy and quality, research has been conducted, and specialized software has been developed for the HIL testbench. This software enables testing based on modern models, including the Simplified General Perturbations model 4 and the International Geomagnetic Reference Field model (IGRF-13). These models allow to make simulation and prediction of spacecraft motion and attitude in space for a more accurate. Consequently, functional testing on the HIL simulation testbench makes it possible to verify the performance of the spacecraft's attitude and stabilization systems under conditions that closely approximate real space environment conditions.

title: A Method for the Formation and Analytical Validation of a Medical Speech Dataset for Robust Automatic Speech Recognition
authors: Aidar Nureev, Natalia Staroverova
abstract: Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) is increasingly adopted in healthcare for automating medical documentation, supporting telemedicine services, and developing assistive interfaces for patients with speech impairments. However, the practical deployment of ASR in clinical environments is hindered by a significant decline in recognition performance compared to controlled laboratory settings. The primary limitation is not model architecture but the scarcity, imbalance, and low representativeness of available clinical speech datasets, which often fail to capture the acoustic variability and pathological speech patterns observed in real-world clinical scenarios. This study presents a formalized methodology for constructing a robust combined medical speech dataset that incorporates both normative and pathological speech, augmented with analytically controlled transformations to emulate clinical acoustic conditions. Pathological speech is modeled through a composition of monotonic temporal, formant, and phonatory perturbations constrained within physiologically plausible limits, ensuring the preservation of linguistic content and diagnostically relevant acoustic features. The proposed dataset is validated through acoustic–spectral analysis, statistical evaluation of self-supervised embeddings, and ASR-based functional testing. Experimental results demonstrate that the augmented dataset enhances ASR robustness, reduces sensitivity to pathological variability, and mitigates domain shift, without compromising critical diagnostic cues. The methodology provides a reproducible framework for dataset engineering, establishing a foundation for scalable, reliable, and ethically compliant clinical speech technologies.

title: Artificial Intelligence-based Centralized Dispatcher Control System for Organizing Train Movement
authors: Sunnatillo T. Boltayev, Obidjon O. Muhiddinov, Qamara A. Kosimova
abstract: The article presents recommendations for improving centralized dispatching control systems and introducing modern technologies. This system is aimed at ensuring efficient train traffic management, enhancing safety, and optimizing transportation processes. By using artificial intelligence and automated systems, train operations can be managed more effectively, while reducing the workload of train dispatchers and increasing labor productivity. The train traffic scheduling system developed on the basis of an automated workstation enables automated plotting of train movement graphs, early prediction of faults, and reduction of delays. In addition, the system contributes to reducing energy consumption, increasing economic efficiency, and improving safety. These processes enhance railway line capacity and reduce the occurrence of emergency situations in railway transport.

title: Methodology of Building Machine Learning Models for Predicting the State of a Technical Object at Class Imbalance
authors: Yuliya Kuvayskova, Anastasiya Izyurova
abstract: Timely detection of failures in the operation of a technical object minimizes emergency response time. This requires predicting the object’s state and potential operational deviations by analyzing a set of functional parameters. To address this challenge, machine learning methods are suitable. However, model efficiency depends on hyperparameter selection and class imbalance in the training data, which may result in prediction errors for critical situations. In this paper, we propose a novel methodology for building machine learning models that addresses class imbalance and optimizes hyperparameters to improve anomaly prediction accuracy in technical systems. The methodology comprises three key components: identification of the most significant operational parameters; application of hyperparameter tuning techniques; use of sampling methods, including hybrid algorithms, to mitigate class imbalance. A specialized Python program implements the proposed methodology. Its effectiveness was evaluated through a case study on a hydraulic system. The results demonstrate that the methodology enhances prediction quality – by an average of 26 % for AUC ROC and 59 % for the F score – and significantly reduces model training time (from more than 10 minutes to less than 12 seconds). These findings suggest that the methodology is promising for monitoring complex technical systems with significant class imbalance.

title: A Hybrid CNN-LSTM Architecture for Time Series Analysis of Symptoms in Electronic Veterinary Records
authors: D. G. Antonov, V. S. Moshkin, E. S. Mytarin
abstract: This paper proposes a hybrid CNN-LSTM architecture for analyzing time series of symptoms in electronic veterinary records. The model integrates the ability of convolutional networks to detect local patterns in data and the ability of a recurrent LSTM layer to account for chronological dependencies between symptoms. Convolutional layers are responsible for detecting local patterns in the data. LSTM preserves significant information over long time intervals and filters out irrelevant data. Application of the method to a dataset of 15,000 clinical records resulted in a 7.4% increase in classification accuracy and a 7.7% increase in the macro-F1 measure compared to a baseline CNN, demonstrating its effectiveness for diagnosing diseases with pronounced temporal dynamics, such as parvovirus enteritis and gastrointestinal stasis.

title: YOLO-Based Defect Detection in Induction Soldering: A Comparative Study of YOLOv8 to YOLOv12 Architectures
authors: Elizaveta V. Shutkina, Vadim S. Tynchenko, Sergey O. Kurashkin
abstract: Today, induction soldering is widely used to connect critical aerospace parts in machine-building enterprises. Non-contact pyrometers are usually used for temperature control in this process, however, the vapors formed during soldering affect the measurement accuracy of the pyrometers used, which leads to defects such as burnout, non-soldering and overheating. This paper presents a comparative analysis of various YOLO (You Only Look Once) neural network models used to detect solder joint defects during induction soldering. The comparison involved versions from YOLOv8 to YOLO12, including models from nano to extra. The YOLOv9t model reached a mAP@50 value of 0.9018 and a mAP@50-95 value of 0.5992 with an inference time of 33.6 ms.

title: Network Intrusion Detection based on Hybrid Telemetry Analysis using Transformer-LSTM Architecture
authors: Ivan O. Gusev
abstract: This paper investigates the problem of network intrusion detection in the context of targeted transport-layer metadata obfuscation. We propose a robust attack detection method based on a Transformer-LSTM machine learning architecture for the concurrent analysis of diverse network traffic features and operating system telemetry. The solution integrates system performance metrics (CPU utilization, RAM state, system call frequency, etc.) with network features at the network, transport, and application layers. Testing results demonstrate a 75.8% reduction in false negative rates compared to baseline network-only analysis, achieving an F1-score of 0.9802 (versus 0.9673). The paper concludes by outlining future research prospects regarding this complex and evolving problem.

title: Characterizing Radiation-Induced Transient Noise in Focal Plane Arrays
authors: Daniel Woodbury, Brad C. Smith, Daniel A. Clymer
abstract: Focal plane arrays in a persistent flux of charged particles experience transient accumulation of excess charge, which causes both radiation-induced tracks in imagery as well as additional single pixel temporal noise. The theory for modeling and simulating these noise spikes, which we briefly review, has been well developed in previous literature. However, when comparing radiation test data with simulated models, confounding effects such as standard sensor noise, inter-pixel crosstalk, and diffusion may complicate data analysis. We present a method for characterizing and bounding these radiation-induced transients in test data, even in situations where individual radiation spikes are comparable to or significantly below the noise floor of the focal plane array. We provide a mathematical derivation of our approach, as well as physics-based simulations of pixel spike distributions that illustrate the underlying phenomena. Examples of our analysis technique using noisy simulated data show that method works in realistic conditions, as long as there are enough radiation events per pixel per integration time for statistical confidence. Finally, we discuss limitations of our approach, especially in the presence of radiation induced luminescence.

title: Fan-Out Parallel Packaging Concept for Scalable High-Current GaN HEMTs
authors: Mingrui Zou, Ruihong Luo, Jiakun Gong, Yuxi Liang, Peng Sun, Gaofei Tang, Yongfu Li, Zheng Zeng
abstract: The current capability of Gallium nitride (GaN) HEMTs is constrained by die-level yield degradation and parasitic-induced current imbalance in conventional device paralleling. This letter proposes a fan-out parallel packaging concept for GaN to address these limitations. By selectively integrating matched low-current dies at the wafer level, the proposed approach decouples current scaling from individual die yield while enabling highly symmetric and low-parasitic interconnections. A two-die prototype is developed to validate the concept. Experimental results demonstrate doubled current capability while preserving intrinsic device characteristics. Compared with conventional discrete paralleling exhibiting severe imbalance and oscillation, the transient current imbalance is reduced by approximately 94% during turn-on and 65% during turn-off under 400 V / 100 A condition. These results confirm the proposed concept is an effective and scalable solution for high-current GaN HEMTs applications.

title: Approximation of the Effective Capacity for Multi-Server URLLC Systems With Batch Arrivals_supp1-3683159.pdf
authors: Anton Karamyshev
abstract: Modern communication networks shall support mission-critical and real-time applications that demand high data rates, low latency, and ultra-high reliability, which is known as Ultra-reliable Low-latency Communications (URLLC). In URLLC systems, the effective capacity, i.e., the maximum traffic rate at which latency and reliability constraints are satisfied, is a key metric for resource management. Its estimations are usually obtained from analytical tools, which, importantly, shall be accurate, generalizable, and easily computable to adapt to diverse and rapidly changing scenarios. However, existing analytical tools, often rooted in queueing theory, rely on unrealistic assumptions, neglect essential traffic features like batch arrivals or unique URLLC constraints, or incur high computational costs, limiting their practical use. To fill this gap, the paper proposes an approximation framework to estimate the delay violation probabilities and the effective capacity for general multi-server queues with batch arrivals (i.e., <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$G^{\mathcal {B}}/G/s$</tex-math></inline-formula>). Rooted in queueing and stochastic process theories, the framework yields low-complexity, closed-form solutions suitable for online analysis, providing critical insights into the relationship between URLLC constraints and the effective capacity. Extensive simulations confirm the framework’s accuracy across diverse URLLC-relevant settings. Finally, the paper illustrates its practical relevance in a modern cellular system with URLLC traffic.

title: MFR-Net: Motion-Guided Feature Refinement Network for Video Small Object Detection in Vision-Based Airport Surveillance Systems
authors: Shengjie Zhang, Yang Yang, Yanbo Zhu, Shengsheng Qian, Xiaoxiao Zhang, Kaiquan Cai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Video-based surveillance in critical airport surface environments is essential for comprehensive situational awareness, yet it presents a unique and challenging task which we formally define as Video Small Object Detection in Vision-based Airport Surveillance Systems (VSOD-VASS). This task is characterized by a confluence of compounding difficulties, including 1) diminutive object size and severe long-tail imbalance of semantic categories, and 2) consistent modeling of long-range motions and stochastic transient dynamics. To address these challenges, we propose a two-part framework: an offline data augmentation pipeline, named Trajectory-anchored Object Augmentation (TOA), and a Motion-guided Feature Refinement Network (MFR-Net). The TOA pipeline combines trajectory-constrained object generation for controlled appearance diversification and delayed trajectory replay for reusing valid historical object states. The MFR-Net then integrates two key innovations: an adaptive Motion-aware Feature Alignment (MFA) module that robustly models transient dynamics from adjacent frames, and a selective Distant Proximal Temporal Feature Aggregation (DPTFA) module that uses attention to filter noise and aggregate valuable long-range dependencies. To facilitate robust evaluation, we introduce VASSO, a large-scale real-world airport surveillance dataset featuring 1,332,223 instances across 12 categories, where over 87.51% of all objects are small or tiny. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MFR-Net achieves performance with 72.50% <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$mAP$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and 93.43% <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$mAP_{50}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, significantly improving 4.70% <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$mAP$</tex-math> </inline-formula> with existing SOTA methods. Our work provides a formal problem definition, a strong SOTA method, and a challenging large-scale dataset to advance research in this critical domain. Code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/ATMHub-515/MFR-Net</uri>

title: Byzantine-Resilient Federated Learning under Heterogeneity and Heavy Tails_supp1-3686661.pdf
authors: Youming Tao
abstract: Byzantine resilience is essential in federated learning (FL) to safeguard model training from malicious or faulty participants. However, existing Byzantine-resilient methods struggle when faced with heavy-tailed gradient noise, a common challenge in heterogeneous environments. In this work, we propose a Byzantine-resilient FL framework specifically designed to handle both heterogeneity and heavy-tailed noise. Our approach builds on robust distributed stochastic heavy-ball optimization, incorporating update normalization and gradient/momentum clipping to mitigate the effects of heavy-tailed noise. We establish the first high-probability convergence guarantees for Byzantine-resilient FL under these conditions, showing that our algorithms achieve optimal Byzantine resilience and align with known lower bounds. Additionally, we introduce an efficient variant of the nearest neighbor mixing technique, leveraging random projections to significantly reduce computational costs in high-dimensional settings. Through rigorous theoretical analysis and extensive empirical evaluations, we demonstrate that our methods outperform existing approaches in robustness against both Byzantine failures and heavy-tailed noise.

title: Stiffness Map Generation for Soft Materials Using Axis-Aligning Non-Contact Measuring Device
authors: Taiki Yamaguchi, Makoto Kaneko, Kensuke Harada
abstract: This paper presents a novel non-contact sensing device for constructing stiffness distribution maps on soft-material surfaces. Since directly contacting and measuring each point is inefficient, the device applies an air jet to the surface to induce deformation, and the resulting displacement is measured with a laser displacement sensor. By employing a confocal laser displacement sensor and aligning illumination and collection on the same optical axis, the actuation point of the jet and the sensing point coincide, enabling stable measurements even on tilted or locally rough surfaces. The device is compact and lightweight, allowing it to be mounted on the wrist of a robot. We further introduce an estimation algorithm that quantitatively evaluates stiffness in both the normal and tangential directions of the surface. Experimental results demonstrate that, unlike conventional contact-based methods, the proposed approach can rapidly generate stiffness maps with sufficient accuracy even on elastic surfaces whose stiffness varies spatially.

title: A Hybrid Perching Mechanism for Aerial Robots: Branch and Surface Perching Robust to Large Surface Orientation Misalignment
authors: Sudheera Akalanka Kariyawasam, Mahmud Hasan Saikot, Bo Cheng, Jianguo Zhao
abstract: Abstract—Perching has recently emerged as a key capability in aerial robotics, enabling substantial gains in energy efficiency and operational endurance for long-duration missions. In this work, we present a multifunctional perching mechanism capable of both branch and surface perching. For branch perching, the mechanism uses four simultaneously-actuated claws to grasp branches with diameters up to 35 mm. For surface perching, it leverages a bistable mechanism with five suction cups, allowing perching onto flat smooth surfaces with large orientation misalignment (with surface tilt angles of up to $\mathbf{80}^\circ$). The mechanism is fabricated using a combination of multimaterial 3D printing and silicone molding, resulting in a lightweight prototype with a mass of 32g. Experimental results show that the proposed design achieves $\boldsymbol{6.4\times }$ adhesion success compared to a bistable mechanism with a single suction cup. Furthermore, real-world perching experiments onto cars and tree branches demonstrate the effectiveness of the mechanism in different environments, highlighting its potential for energy-efficient and adaptable aerial robotic operations.

title: Relational Feature Modeling: Enhancing Predictive Learning through Network-Based Features Augmentation
authors: Ankur Kumar Singhal, Anurag Singh
abstract: Feature representation and augmentation are central factors for improving predictive accuracy of the prediction model, particularly in domains constrained by limited data availability such as agriculture and biological systems. Data scarcity, usually caused by annotation costs, variability in environmental factors, and the rarity of critical events, restricts the effectiveness of conventional models. While traditional prediction models perform well in data-rich scenarios, their performance degrades in small-data scenarios, leading to reduced predictive accuracy. To address limitations, a network-based feature augmentation prediction framework is proposed for small-data scenarios. An instance-specific parenclitic network is constructed for each data instance by modeling deviations among features, where nodes represent features and edges show the pairwise relationships. Further, three important structural descriptors edge count (global connectivity), entropy centrality (dispersion of influence), and clustering coefficient (local cohesiveness) are extracted and concatenated with the original feature vector to form an augmented representation. The augmented data are then modelled using a regularised multivariate Gaussian distribution, with class-conditional likelihoods. The framework is evaluated on three different datasets and performs better than the baseline methods, with accuracy improvements of 5.1%, 5.6%, and 4.0%. Additional analyses using residual and contour plots confirmed improved stability and interpretability, showing the proposed model is scalable and effective across domains with limited data.

title: IEEE Control Systems Society Publication Information
authors: N/A

title: Adaptive UAV Positioning to Enhance SNR in Air-to-Water Optical Wireless Channels
authors: Sk Samiul Reza, Murat Yuksel
abstract: Underwater communication systems mostly use acoustic signals relayed from a buoy into the water. This approach avoids the high radio attenuation in the water but is limited to the narrow bandwidth of acoustic signals. Optical wireless communication (OWC) has emerged as a promising technology for high-speed underwater networking applications, including global climate monitoring, military operations, and marine biology research. Leveraging the unlicensed optical spectrum enables high data transmission rate. However, this approach faces challenges from absorption, scattering, and turbulence, particularly at the air-to-water (A2W) interface. The dynamic nature of water surfaces causes significant disruptions, compromising the stability and reliability of the end-to-end communication link. This study investigates the impact of water surface variations on the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and bit error rate (BER) at the underwater receiver in an A2W OWC channel when the transmitter is mounted on an unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV). A Monte Carlo–based ray propagation framework is adopted to model stochastic scattering effects, and the system performance is evaluated for two different configurations: a small-aperture receiver with lower transmit power and a large-aperture receiver with higher transmit power. We propose a movement optimization framework for the UAV. By dynamically adjusting its position, the UAV can compensate for signal degradation caused by variations in the water surface. We develop and evaluate three movement optimization strategies, tailored for performance, reliability, and value, and visualize the resulting UAV motion using streamplots. Furthermore, we analyze the influence of water surface and system parameters, such as water surface steepness and receiver depth, on different metrics of the UAV movement optimizations. Additionally, we analyze the overall SNR and BER performance by comparing these different optimization approaches. The findings demonstrate that adaptive UAV positioning significantly enhances communication performance, even in the presence of highly dynamic water surfaces.

title: VGESO-Based Neural Network Adaptive Platoon Control for Autonomous Vehicles Under Sensor and Actuator Attacks
authors: Haonan Qi, Tianfei Chen, Lijun Sun, Xiaoping Liu
abstract: In complex traffic environments, the spacing policy of vehicle platoon is a crucial research direction for enhancing the safety of intelligent connected vehicles. This paper proposes a control method based on variable gain extended state observers (VGESOs) and variable time-headway policy to ensure stability for connected vehicle platoon. First, the variable time-headway policy is applied to the platoon to reduce the fluctuations generated by followers when the states of the leader change. Second, the modified VGESOs with high gain characteristics estimate external disturbances from the platoon, while eliminating the gain decay problem. Additionally, a neural network adaptive strategy is employed to mitigate unknown impacts from sensor and actuator attacks. By applying Lyapunov stability theory, all signals in the system are semi-globally uniformly ultimately bounded, thereby ensuring internal stability and string stability. The effectiveness of the proposed scheme is verified through simulation experiments and comparative results.

title: Writing, Presenting, and Publishing a Paper at an IEEE Industry Applications Society Conference
authors: David B. Durocher
abstract:
THE IEEE IS THE WORLD’S LARGEST TECHNICAL PROFESSIONAL organization dedicated to advancing technology for the benefit of humanity. The organization consists of over 500,000 members and sponsors more than 2,000 conferences and events in 109 countries. Over 7 million documents including journals, conference proceedings, standard, eBooks and educational courses are cataloged in the world’s largest online technical content web portal known as the IEEE Xplore library. Most papers in Xplore were presented at technical conferences sponsored by one of 39 technical Societies and eight Councils that cover a wide range of technical interests. One technical Society, the IEEE Industry Applications Society (IAS), boasts a tagline “Linking Research to Practice.” While many IEEE societies support conferences and technical papers based primarily on academic and/or industry research, IAS conferences include a variety of content, including both research papers, and technical papers at industry-focused conferences, written and presented by industry practitioners.

This article investigates best practices in writing, presenting, and publishing IAS conference technical papers where the primary author is a researcher or a practicing engineer. A step-by-step process description begins with selecting a paper topic, submitting a paper title, drafting a compelling abstract, selecting coauthors, recruiting mentors, writing the paper, peer review, final submission, and preparing impactful presentation slides, followed by guidance on effectively presenting the paper. Post presentation, the process in selecting conference prize papers is reviewed, followed by steps to submitting the conference paper for publication in one of the IAS periodicals. The purpose of this article is to inspire IAS authors and prospective authors to dismiss their fears and commit to writing and presenting a technical paper at a future IAS technical conference.

title: A New Universal Overreading Model for Vortex Wet Gas Metering Based on Capacitance Void Measurement
authors: Jinxia Li, Kun Qin, Hongjun Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: To improve the vortex metering accuracy in wet gas flow, a new meter overreading model is developed based on gas void fraction measurement, which could give a universal formula form in a wide application range. For the gas void fraction measurement, a semi-cylindrical capacitive sensor is developed, the geometric parameters of the sensor (length and opening angle of the electrode plate as well as the shielding distance) are optimal designed by using orthogonal tests, and the voltage output is calibrated with ±3.0% accuracy for the gas void fraction range of 0.3~1.0. Then, the meter overreading model is developed with gas void fraction and density ratio, which provides universal model formula (with only one different coefficient) both under vertical and horizontal flows, implying that gas void fraction is the major scale parameters of the meter overreading. Compared with the existing overreading models, the predicted accuracy is decreased from a maximum of 27% to ±5% (95.75% confidence interval) for the operational conditions of pressure 0.15~8MPa and pipe diameter 15~100mm. In addition, based on the overreading model and the developed vortex flowmeter-capacitive sensor dual-mode measurement system, an explicit non-iteration wet gas metering model is established, and the predicted error for gas flow rate is reduced from up to13% to ±2% (96.30% confidence interval).

title: Proof-Based Secure Semantic Communication for Privacy-Critical Consumer Internet of Things
authors: Renwen Ma, Zhaoqian Liu, Chenxin Yuan, Yiming Miao, Marios Kountouris, Pin-Han Ho
abstract: Semantic communication (SemCom) has emerged as a promising paradigm for consumer IoT (CIoT) systems by transmitting task-relevant semantic representations instead of raw data, significantly reducing communication overhead. However, this paradigm shift introduces a new security challenge: adversaries may tamper with semantic computation to induce incorrect task decisions even when the channel remains bitwise correct. Conventional bit-level integrity mechanisms provide no guarantees of semantic execution correctness. While full zero-knowledge proofs (ZKPs) could enforce such guarantees, their overhead is impractical for latency-sensitive CIoT deployments. Accordingly, in this paper, we propose a hybrid, proof-based secure SemCom framework for privacy-critical CIoT, where a ZKP attests to correct semantic encoding without exposing raw inputs, and an optimistic fraud-proof mechanism verifies decoding and task inference with low overhead in the common case. To prevent semantic privacy leakage during disputes, the protocol binds intermediate states with lightweight commitments and resolves challenges via privacy-preserving re-execution with secure multiparty computation. Experiments on consumer-oriented semantic workloads, including image classification, object detection, semantic autoencoding, and language modeling, show that the proposed design reduces end-to-end verification overhead by one to three orders of magnitude compared with full ZKP baselines, while maintaining millisecond-level verification latency and negligible semantic utility loss. This work provides a principled, deployable approach for trustworthy and secure semantic communication in privacy-critical consumer IoT systems.

title: Adaptive Cloud–Edge Coordination for Real-Time Phishing URL Detection with Distributed Caching and ONNX-Based Inference
authors: Minh Linh Dam, Duy Hung Ha, Trong Thua Huynh, Cong Hung Tran, Miroslav Voznak
abstract: Phishing detection systems continue to struggle with real-time responsiveness in distributed web environments. This paper proposes a cloud–edge coordination framework that integrates browser-side Uniform Resource Locator (URL) interception, adaptive cache coordination (ACC), and open ONNX-accelerated inference. The lightweight hybrid model (DistilBERT–MLP fusion) fuses DistilBERT embeddings with standardized numerical features through a multilayer perceptron (MLP). Experiments demonstrate a detection accuracy above 99%, millisecond-level neural inference latency, and end-to-end detection times below 150 ms in live browser environments. ACC reduces cold-cache initialization from multisecond delays to microsecond-level cache lookups. Before ACC, a first-time blacklist fetch takes 7–8 s, and subsequent lookups complete in under 1 μs, with total detection latency ranging between approximately 2 and 110 ms. This behavior demonstrates the effectiveness of ACC in reducing repeated network queries. The experimental results substantiate three principal contributions: an efficient hybrid lexical–numeric detector, an ACC-driven mechanism that mitigates cold-start delays, and a cloud–edge design that strengthens real-time phishing protection at the web scale.

title: Dual-Functional Buffer Layer Enabling High-Sensitivity in MAPbI3 X-ray Flat-Panel Detectors
authors: Mingzhu Hu, Huaqing Yang, Lijuan Huang, Xunyong Yang, Yuangan Chen, Yuan Li, Shunjie Yu, Xiaohu Hou, Yu Li, Xuechen Jiao, Junfa Zhu, Yuncheng Han, Wei Qian, Shibing Long, Qin Hu
abstract: In this research, we present an interfacial modulation strategy utilizing a fluorinated polyimide‑perovskite composite (FPI‑MAPbBr<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>) buffer layer for high-performance perovskite X‑ray detectors. This buffer layer functions through injection barrier modulation and interfacial passivation, effectively reducing dark current and enhancing detection sensitivity. The optimized device demonstrates a record high sensitivity of 1.3 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">5</sup> μC Gy<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">air</sub><sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup> cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-2</sup>, a low detection limit of 43.8 nGy<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">air</sub> s<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup>, and a high spatial resolution of 0.65 lp pixel<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup>, as well as maintaining good operational stability under continuous irradiation. Integrated with a thin film transistor (TFT) backplane, the detector realizes a functional flat‑panel prototype capable of resolving fine internal structures. This work provides a practical interfacial engineering approach toward efficient and stable perovskite X‑ray detectors for real‑world imaging applications.

title: Harmful Visual Content Manipulation Matters in Misinformation Detection Under Multimedia Scenarios
authors: Bing Wang, Ximing Li, Changchun Li, Jinjin Chi, Tianze Li, Renchu Guan, Shengsheng Wang
abstract: Nowadays, the widespread dissemination of misinformation across numerous social media platforms has led to severe negative effects on society. To address this challenge, the automatic detection of misinformation, particularly under multimedia scenarios, has gained significant attention from both academic and industrial communities, leading to the emergence of a research task known as Multimodal Misinformation Detection (MMD). Typically, current MMD approaches focus on capturing the semantic relationships and inconsistency between various modalities but often overlook certain critical indicators within multimodal content. Recent research has shown that manipulated features within visual content in social media articles serve as valuable clues for MMD. Meanwhile, we argue that the potential intentions behind the manipulation, e.g., harmful and harmless, also matter in MMD. Therefore, in this study, we aim to identify such multimodal misinformation by capturing two types of features: manipulation features, which represent if visual content has been manipulated, and intention features, which assess the nature of these manipulations, distinguishing between harmful and harmless intentions. Unfortunately, the manipulation and intention labels that supervise these features to be discriminative are unknown. To address this, we introduce two weakly supervised indicators as substitutes by incorporating supplementary datasets focused on image manipulation detection and framing two different classification tasks as positive and unlabeled learning issues. With this framework, we introduce an innovative MMD approach, titled Harmful Visual Content Manipulation Matters in MMD (Havc-m$^{4}$d). Comprehensive experiments conducted on four prevalent MMD datasets indicate that Havc-m$^{4}$d significantly and consistently enhances the performance of existing MMD methods.

title: Solid-State Circuit Breakers for DC-Powered Buildings: An Overview of Standards, Directives, and Technologies
authors: Henrique Bizzi Morari, Lucas Vizzotto Bellinaso, Edivan Laercio Carvalho, Satish Naik Banavath, Giovanni De Carne, Dmitri Vinnikov
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Power Electronics
abstract: This paper presents a review of low-voltage direct current (LVDC) solid-state circuit breakers (SSCBs) for building power systems, focusing on relevant standards, safety directives, and design requirements for DC protection. A structured classification scheme is proposed for LVDC SSCBs based on key design criteria including the number of poles, bidirectional current capability, isolation method, semiconductor technology, and circuit topology. This paper also proposes a categorization of SSCB protective functions into elementary safety functions (short-circuit interruption, overcurrent and overload protection, and selective coordination) and additional advanced functions (arc-fault detection, insulation monitoring, and earth-fault protection), distinguishing fundamental requirements from enhanced safety features. Furthermore, a risk assessment framework grounded in ISO 12100 principles is introduced and tailored to LVDC installations employing SSCBs. This framework systematically identifies potential hazards and SSCB failure modes, guiding risk mitigation measures and the development of test procedures to ensure compliance with safety standards. Collectively, these contributions provide clear guidelines to support the safe integration, standardization, and future development of SSCBs in emerging DC power distribution networks.

title: Application of Multi-Agent Modeling for Adaptive Control of a Virtual Power Plant Based on the Mesa Library Using an Online Forecasting Algorithm
authors: Artem A. Bezzubov, Mihail D. Senyuk, Konstantin A. Aksyonov
abstract: This paper explores the application of agent-based modeling to virtual power plant (VPP) management to optimize the balance between energy production and consumption in real time. The focus is on the implementation of online learning algorithms that enable agents to adapt to the high volatility of renewable energy generation and demand variability. The novelty of this work lies in the development and exploration of a multi-agent model of an adaptive VPP on the Mesa platform. Its key features include the integration of lightweight online learning models into distributed generation agents, enabling the refinement of generation forecasts in real time based on incoming weather data, and a decentralized decision-making mechanism in which the aggregator agent, using confidence intervals from local agent forecasts, dynamically optimizes the charging/discharging schedule of buffer storage devices for participants. The proposed approach allows agents to continuously update their strategies based on incoming data streams, minimizing active power imbalance and operating costs. This article presents simulation results demonstrating improved wind power plant efficiency compared to static control strategies while maintaining system reliability requirements. The study confirms that combining agent-based modeling and online learning is a promising approach for creating flexible, fault-tolerant, and cost-effective virtual power plants.

title: Equipment Resource Reproduction Management Based on Simulation Modeling
authors: Vladislav Kukartsev, Anastasia Kozlova, Alena Stupina
abstract: The article addresses the problem of managing the reproduction of an enterprise's production capacity through maintenance and repair processes. The author moves away from the traditional perception of service processes as auxiliary and interprets them as a mechanism for replenishing the fund of available equipment operating time within the smart manufacturing concept. The relevance of the research is determined by the need to align production plans with the capabilities of repair services under conditions of dynamic equipment wear and limited resources. The paper proposes a formal model of capacity deficit, defined as the gap between the requirements of the production program and the actual residual resource of the equipment fleet. The key result is a decision support algorithm based on simulation modeling. The algorithm implements an iterative process for selecting optimal restoration actions, considering personnel and material-technical inventory constraints. The presented approach allows for a transition from rigid planning to adaptive management of production potential, ensuring the system's resilience to capacity deficits. The developed strategy matrix formalizes the choice of methods for compensating deficits depending on the level of resource shortage and time constraints.

title: An Approach to Designing Multimodal Analytical Scenarios for Industry 4.0 Systems
authors: Valentin Charugin, Valery Charugin, Alexander Chesalin
abstract: Within the framework of the Industry 4.0 concept, heterogeneous data generated during equipment operation and the functioning of automation systems are being increasingly utilized. Such data are employed in intelligent analytics and machine learning algorithms to address tasks related to equipment condition monitoring, diagnostics, and decision support for managing the technical state of equipment. The absence of a formalized description of the structure and processing rules for heterogeneous data at the analytical scenario design stage complicates the adoption of multimodal analytical solutions in industrial environments and leads to hidden data integration errors, reduced reproducibility, and limited reusability of analytical solutions. This paper proposes an approach to designing multimodal analytical scenarios for Industry 4.0 systems, based on a formal representation of the scenario as a directed acyclic data processing graph, the use of modality contracts and data role annotations, as well as a set of correctness rules applied at the design stage. The proposed approach enables the explicit specification of permissible processing operations, modalities fusion strategies, and conditions for the coordinated use of data. The applicability of the proposed approach is demonstrated using an analytical scenario implemented in a visual analytics process constructor. The obtained results show that the proposed approach enables the identification of structural, format-related, and semantic errors at the scenario design stage and contributes to improving the reproducibility and reliability of industrial analytical solutions.

title: A Data Management Infrastructure for Reproducible Experimental Research
authors: Ziyad Mabrouk, Bohdan Dyshlevyy, Damien Saucez, Panagiotis Hadjidemetriou, Panayiotis Andreou
publish: 2026 29th Conference on Innovation in Clouds, Internet and Networks (ICIN)
abstract: Cloud, Internet, and network infrastructures are now tightly integrated, making experimental research increasingly dependent on advanced technical expertise and real-system deployments. As a result, purely theoretical evaluations are often insufficient to capture real-world behavior. This shift has driven the rise of data-driven research, where hypotheses are validated using high-quality experimental data. However, such data are frequently retained by their producers, limiting reproducibility and reuse. The EU SLICES-RI project addresses this challenge by providing a scientific platform that supports reproducible, large-scale experiments on state-of-the-art hardware and software and enables the systematic sharing of experimental data and metadata across the research community. This demo presents the SLICES-RI Data Management Infrastructure and its Metadata Registry System, demonstrating how lightweight tools can be used to efficiently publish and share experimental results. This Metadata Registry System has already been adopted by other European projects, such as Converge, 6GXCEL, and 6G-Sunrise.

title: Machine Learning Approaches for Information Security of Agent-Based Swarm Systems in the Digital Industry
authors: Radda Iureva, Daria A. Galkina
abstract: The rapid digitalization of industrial processes has led to the widespread adoption of distributed and autonomous systems, including agent-based swarm systems. These systems are actively used in smart manufacturing, logistics, robotics, and cyber-physical infrastructures. However, their decentralized nature, dynamic behavior, and large-scale interaction significantly expand the attack surface and introduce new information security challenges. This paper explores the role of machine learning methods in ensuring the information security of agent-based swarm systems in the digital industry. We analyze typical threat models, including data poisoning, spoofing, sybil attacks, and coordinated adversarial behavior. Particular attention is given to anomaly detection, intrusion detection, trust evaluation, and adaptive defense mechanisms based on supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning. In addition, we consider a meta-monitoring mechanism in which multiple agents not only detect failures and attacks but also evaluate the reliability of the models used by peer agents for failure detection. This cross-validation logic improves robustness against detector drift, local model corruption, and coordinated false alarms. The paper also discusses architectural aspects of secure swarm intelligence and presents illustrative experimental results demonstrating the effectiveness of machine learning-based security mechanisms. The results show that learning-based approaches significantly improve resilience, adaptability, and robustness of swarm systems in hostile and uncertain environments.

title: Construction and Analysis of a Graph-Based Knowledge Structure Model Using Psycholinguistic Experiment Data
authors: Aleksandr Petrosian, Andrey Philippovich
abstract: This article is devoted to the development and experimental testing of a method for visualizing and analyzing the cognitive organization of knowledge based on graph models constructed from data of a directed associative experiment. Unlike traditional assessment methods focused on identifying specific aspects of topic understanding, the proposed approach allows for analyzing their holistic structure, taking into account the significance of concepts and the strength of connections between them. Experimental testing of the method was conducted as part of a longitudinal study involving more than 100 IT students from Moscow Polytechnic University. Key concepts for the chosen subject domain of network technologies were identified based on associative data; adjacency matrices and heatmaps were constructed. They were visualized as graph models using Gephi. Comparison of individual student graphs with the group averaged model, built on aggregated data, demonstrated the applicability of the method for identifying gaps and weak inter-conceptual connections. The results indicate the potential of the proposed approach for diagnosing knowledge structure and analyzing interdisciplinary connections, which is significant in training IT specialists, as well as for supporting personalized educational trajectories within the Education 4.0 concept.

title: Study of Normal and Emergency Modes of Operation of the Power Supply System of Industrial Enterprises Using Computer Modeling
authors: Mariya N. Semenova, Yuri V. Bebikhov, Ilya A. Yakushev
abstract: This article examines the normal and emergency operating modes of an industrial power supply system using computer simulation, using the example of an outdoor 220/110 kV switchgear at the Fabrika-3 electrical substation. MatLab software, the Simulink application, and the SimPowerSystems block library were used to simulate transient processes. The article presents the results of developing computer models in MatLab that enable calculations of transformer winding data, determination of currents in a transformer T-shaped equivalent circuit, determination of short-circuit and overvoltage currents in the power supply system, including those from external sources (lightning strikes), and a detailed analysis of electromagnetic transient processes in electrical switchgear. A description of the substation's outdoor and indoor switchgear, as well as power transformers, is provided. A universal computer model of an industrial power supply system previously developed by the authors was used for simulation, which was subsequently refined. As a result of modeling in normal operating mode, we obtained a virtually complete match of three-phase voltage values at corresponding levels, with an error of no more than 2%. When modeling a three-phase short circuit, we obtained short-circuit current values at corresponding points and also observed their impact on other voltage levels. By modeling a lightning strike on a 220 kV power line phase, we obtained the lightning strike current and overvoltage level in the network, and also observed its impact on other phases and voltage levels.
